_note: Major NT→OT citations and allusions with source identification. Curated. Expand as chapters are added. Format: { 'MRK 1:2': { 'type': 'citation'|'allusion', 'source': 'MAL 3:1', 'greek_matches': 'LXX'|'MT'|'paraphrase', 'notes': '...' }}
_source: NA28 marginal references + scholarly consensus (Beale & Carson NT Use of the OT, UBS GNT apparatus).

1TH 5:3.notes: Echo of OT prophetic critique of false prophets who proclaim ‘peace, peace’ (LXX Jer 6:14, 8:11 ‘εἰρήνη εἰρήνη, καὶ ποῦ ἐστιν εἰρήνη’; Ezek 13:10 ‘λέγοντες· εἰρήνη, καὶ οὐκ ἦν εἰρήνη’). Paul applies the prophetic indictment to those who trust the imperial Pax-et-Securitas slogan rather than Christ. Composite allusion; no single direct quotation.

1TH 5:8.notes: Lexical echo of LXX Isa 59:17 (καὶ ἐνεδύσατο δικαιοσύνην ὡς θώρακα καὶ περικεφαλαίαν σωτηρίου): θώρακα + περικεφαλαίαν + σωτηρ-stem all preserved. Paul substitutes faith/love for righteousness, hope-of-salvation for helmet-of-salvation, and casts the believer (rather than YHWH) as the one armoring up. Cf. Eph 6:14-17 for Paul's expanded armor metaphor.

1TI 2:13.notes: Adam formed first, then Eve — order-of-creation argument.

1TI 5:18.notes: 'ox not muzzled while threshing' matches LXX. Second half ('worker deserves wages') cites Luke 10:7 as 'Scripture' — notable early NT-as-Scripture reference.

1TI 6:7.notes: 'Brought nothing in, take nothing out' echoes Job.

2TH 1:9.notes: ἀπὸ προσώπου τοῦ κυρίου echoes LXX Isa 2:10, 19, 21 (ἀπὸ προσώπου τοῦ φόβου κυρίου 'from the face of the fear of the Lord'). Same prepositional construction. The Isaiah parallel-context is theophanic-judgment (people hiding 'from the face of the Lord'), supporting the SEPARATION reading of ἀπό at 2 Thess 1:9 (vs. SOURCE reading).

ACT 10:12.notes: CLEAN/UNCLEAN CREATURE CATEGORIES (τετράποδα + ἑρπετὰ + πετεινὰ) in Peter's vision mirror the LXX Leviticus 11 threefold-classification: quadrupeds vv.2-8 (split-hoof + cud-chewing = clean), swarming-creatures vv.29-31, 41-45 (reptiles mostly unclean), birds vv.13-19 (specific not-to-eat list). Peter's refusal at v.14 ('never eaten anything common/unclean') invokes this Mosaic-distinction explicitly. The divine voice's 'what God has cleansed' (v.15) overrides the Levitical classification — establishing God's sovereign-authority to redefine clean/unclean in the new-covenant era (anticipated by Mark 7:19 'cleansing all foods'). DEU 14:3-21 parallel.

ACT 10:34.notes: DIVINE IMPARTIALITY FORMULA — Peter's hapax προσωπολήμπτης 'face-taker/favoritist' is an LXX-style compound translating the Hebrew nasa-panim נָשָׂא פָּנִים 'lift-face = show-partiality' idiom. Standard LXX expression: DEU 10:17 'ὁ θεὸς...οὐ θαυμάζει πρόσωπον' (does not marvel-at face); 2 CH 19:7 LXX 'οὐκ ἔστιν μετὰ κυρίου θεοῦ ἡμῶν ἀδικία οὐδὲ θαυμάσαι πρόσωπον'; JOB 34:19; SIR 35:12 LXX. Peter activates this OT-impartiality-theme but extends its scope from economic-judicial (the classic OT application) to ethnic-covenantal (Jew-Gentile) — the extension is the radical move. Paul picks up the same formula at ROM 2:11, GAL 2:6, EPH 6:9, COL 3:25.

ACT 10:36.notes: PREACH PEACE formula εὐαγγελιζόμενος εἰρήνην — the specific combination of εὐαγγελίζω + εἰρήνη is Isaianic-Nahum-lexical. ISA 52:7 LXX 'ὡς πόδες εὐαγγελιζομένου ἀκοὴν εἰρήνης' (feet-of-the-one-announcing-peace); NAH 1:15 LXX (MT 2:1) 'ἰδοὺ ἐπὶ τὰ ὄρη πόδες εὐαγγελιζομένου καὶ ἀπαγγέλλοντος εἰρήνην.' Both texts envision a herald announcing salvation-after-exile. Peter applies the Isaianic-feet-of-the-messenger image to the apostolic-gospel-proclamation — and implicitly to himself as such a feet-of-messenger reaching Caesarea. Paul also cites ISA 52:7 at ROM 10:15. PSA 147:14 LXX 'ὁ τιθεὶς τὰ ὅρια σου εἰρήνην' secondary-resonance.

ACT 10:38.notes: SPIRIT-ANOINTING formula ἔχρισεν αὐτὸν ὁ θεὸς πνεύματι ἁγίῳ — direct lexical echo of ISA 61:1 LXX 'Πνεῦμα κυρίου ἐπ’ ἐμέ, οὗ εἵνεκεν ἔχρισέν με εὐαγγελίσασθαι πτωχοῖς.' The χρίω + πνεῦμα combination is Isaianic-Servant-anointing vocabulary. Jesus himself read this passage in the Nazareth synagogue and claimed fulfillment (LUK 4:18-21). Peter's kerygma makes Jesus's entire ministry (εὐεργετῶν καὶ ἰώμενος 'doing-good and healing') the fulfillment of the Isaianic-anointed-servant mission. The καὶ δυνάμει addition ('and power') echoes LUK 1:35 Gabriel-to-Mary 'δύναμις ὑψίστου ἐπισκιάσει σοι' and LUK 4:14 'ἐν τῇ δυνάμει τοῦ πνεύματος'.

ACT 10:39.notes: HANGING ON A TREE (κρεμάσαντες ἐπὶ ξύλου) — formulaic echo of DEU 21:22-23 LXX 'κεκατηραμένος ὑπὸ θεοῦ πᾶς κρεμάμενος ἐπὶ ξύλου' (cursed-by-God is everyone hanging on a tree). The apostolic preaching deliberately uses Deuteronomy-language to frame Jesus's death within the cursed-tree category — a paradox that Galatians 3:13 will unpack ('Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law, becoming a curse for us, as it is written, Cursed is everyone hanging on a tree'). Peter's sermonic formula 'killed by hanging on a tree' recurs at ACT 5:30, 10:39, 13:29 — consistent primitive-apostolic framing that Paul inherits and theologizes. 1 PET 2:24 'ἐπὶ τὸ ξύλον' continues the pattern.

ACT 10:4.notes: MEMORIAL OFFERING (μνημόσυνον) is the LXX Levitical technical term (LEV 2:2 ΤΟ ΜΝΗΜΟΣΥΝΟΝ ΑΥΤΗΣ; 2:9, 2:16, 6:15) for the portion of grain-offering burnt on the altar as a 'pleasing aroma' reminding God of the worshiper (Hebrew אַזְכָּרָה azkarah). The angel deliberately applies sacrificial-memorial imagery to Cornelius's Gentile prayers+alms, asserting God accepts them-as-equivalent to Levitical offerings — despite Cornelius lacking access to temple sacrifice. Theologically radical: Luke establishes at the chapter's outset that Gentile-devotion already functions sacrificially before God, prior to any Christological application.

ACT 10:42.notes: JUDGE OF LIVING AND DEAD formula — Christ's universal-judgment-authority echoes DAN 7:13-14 LXX Son-of-Man receiving ἐξουσία + βασιλεία that no-one-can-take-away, κρίσις implicitly; PSA 9:9 LXX 'κρινεῖ τὴν οἰκουμένην ἐν δικαιοσύνῃ, κρινεῖ λαοὺς ἐν εὐθύτητι'; PSA 96:13 LXX (MT 97:9) 'κρινεῖ τὴν οἰκουμένην.' The 'living-and-dead' scope (ζώντων καὶ νεκρῶν) extends beyond OT-earthly-judgment-scope to post-resurrection universal-judgment. Apostolic formula recurs: 2 TIM 4:1 'Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ τοῦ μέλλοντος κρίνειν ζῶντας καὶ νεκρούς'; 1 PET 4:5 'ζώντας καὶ νεκρούς.' The Apostles' Creed 'judge the living and the dead' derives from this apostolic-formula cluster.

ACT 10:43.notes: PROPHETS-TESTIFY cluster (πάντες οἱ προφῆται μαρτυροῦσιν) — collective-prophetic-witness to Messianic forgiveness-of-sins. ISA 53:11 LXX 'δικαιῶσαι δίκαιον εὖ δουλεύοντα πολλοῖς, καὶ τὰς ἁμαρτίας αὐτῶν αὐτὸς ἀνοίσει' (servant-bearing-iniquities = justification-for-many); JER 31:34 LXX 'ἵλεως ἔσομαι ταῖς ἀδικίαις αὐτῶν καὶ τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν αὐτῶν οὐ μὴ μνησθῶ ἔτι' (new-covenant forgiveness — 'I will remember their sins no more'); DAN 9:24 (atoning for iniquity and bringing everlasting righteousness). Peter's sweeping 'all the prophets' claim is a summary-stance about the prophetic-canon's collective witness rather than a single-verse citation — the apostolic conviction that OT prophecy as a whole foresaw Christ's atonement-ministry. Luke himself summarizes this at LUK 24:25-27, 24:44-47 (Jesus opening the Scriptures showing all three canonical divisions pointed to his suffering/resurrection/forgiveness).

ACT 10:45.notes: POURED-OUT SPIRIT (ἐκκέχυται) formula — direct lexical continuation of Peter's ACT 2:17 Pentecost-sermon citation of JOEL 2:28-29 LXX 'ἐκχεῶ ἀπὸ τοῦ πνεύματός μου ἐπὶ πᾶσαν σάρκα.' At Pentecost Peter applied Joel's prophecy to Jewish believers; now at ACT 10:45 Luke uses the same Joel-verb (ἐκχέω) for the Gentile-outpouring — declaring the prophecy's scope fully-extends to 'all flesh' (πᾶσαν σάρκα) including Gentiles. Narratively, the verb-choice is deliberate: Luke signals this is Joel-Pentecost-extension, not a different-category event. The theological claim: Gentile-Spirit-reception IS part of the same Joel-2:28 outpouring that began on Pentecost.

ACT 11:15.notes: PETER RE-NARRATING SPIRIT-OUTPOURING: ἐπέπεσεν τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον ἐπ’ αὐτοὺς ὥσπερ καὶ ἐφ’ ἡμᾶς ἐν ἀρχῇ — Peter's retrospective-interpretation of the Cornelius-Spirit-event EXPLICITLY pairs it with 'the-beginning' = Pentecost (ACT 2:1-4) where Peter had cited JOEL 2:28-29 as the interpretive-frame. The Joel-prophecy's πᾶσαν σάρκα (all-flesh) scope is the theological-bridge Peter here appeals to — the same Joel-outpouring now extends to Gentile-flesh. Parallels ACT 2:17 direct-citation + ACT 10:45 ἐκκέχυται echo.

ACT 11:16.notes: PETER QUOTING JESUS QUOTING JOEL: 'John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit' — Peter cites Jesus's ascension-promise (ACT 1:5 'ὑμεῖς δὲ ἐν πνεύματι βαπτισθήσεσθε ἁγίῳ') as the interpretive-key for the Cornelius-Spirit-event. Jesus's own saying at ACT 1:5 framed the promised Spirit-baptism through the lens of Joel 2:28-29 (which Peter then explicitly-cited at Pentecost, ACT 2:17). Thus ACT 11:16 is a double-embedded citation: Peter quoting Jesus quoting (interpretively) Joel. Triple-stage prophetic-fulfillment-recognition.

ACT 11:6.notes: PETER RE-NARRATING VISION: the four-category list (τετράποδα + θηρία + ἑρπετὰ + πετεινὰ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ) expands ACT 10:12's threefold list with θηρία 'wild beasts' added as a distinct group — Peter's first-person retelling more-vividly invokes the LXX Leviticus 11 clean/unclean creature categories (quadrupeds vv.2-8, swarming-creatures vv.29-31, 41-45, birds vv.13-19). Same Mosaic-distinction that Peter will cite as his refusal-ground at v.8. Parallel DEU 14:3-21. See ACT 10:12 DB entry for primary treatment.

ACT 12:11.notes: PETER'S ANGELIC-DELIVERANCE ACKNOWLEDGMENT: 'Νῦν οἶδα ἀληθῶς ὅτι ἐξαπέστειλεν ὁ κύριος τὸν ἄγγελον αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐξείλατό με' shares close vocabulary and narrative-structure with (a) PSA 34:7 LXX (παρεμβαλεῖ ἄγγελος κυρίου κύκλῳ τῶν φοβουμένων αὐτὸν καὶ ῥύσεται αὐτούς — 'the angel of the Lord encamps around those who fear him, and delivers them'); and (b) DAN 6:22 LXX/Th (ὁ θεός μου ἀπέστειλεν τὸν ἄγγελον αὐτοῦ — 'my God sent his angel' — Daniel rescued from lions' den by royal decree-execution, structural parallel to Peter rescued from Herod's execution). Peter's phrasing patterns the psalmist's and Daniel's angelic-rescue-from-royal-execution typology onto his own deliverance. Lukan-Acts motif: God's angel as deliverer-agent (ACT 5:19 previous Peter-rescue; ACT 27:23 Paul on ship).

ACT 12:23.notes: DIVINE-JUDGMENT ON ROYAL SELF-DEIFICATION: 'ἀνθ’ ὧν οὐκ ἔδωκεν τὴν δόξαν τῷ θεῷ' inverts ISA 42:8 LXX (Ἐγὼ κύριος ὁ θεός, τοῦτό μού ἐστιν τὸ ὄνομα, τὴν δόξαν μου ἑτέρῳ οὐ δώσω — 'I am YHWH my God, this is my name; my glory I will not give to another'). Herod received/accepted-without-returning the glory-that-belongs-only-to-God. Also structural-narrative parallel DAN 4:30-37 (Nebuchadnezzar's royal-boast 'Is not this great Babylon that I have built...' → immediate heavenly-voice-judgment → humiliation-until-he-acknowledges-God-rules). Same pattern in ACT 12: royal public-acclamation (v.21-22) → immediate divine-strike (v.23) → death as judgment-for-glory-stealing. ἐξέψυξεν (v.23) clusters lexically with ACT 5:5 Ananias + ACT 5:10 Sapphira — Luke's signature judgment-death verb for lying-against-or-stealing-glory-from-God. The σκωληκόβρωτος 'worm-eaten' motif has apocryphal parallel 2 Macc 9:9 (Antiochus IV Epiphanes); Luke's Greco-Jewish readership would recognize the tyrant-eaten-by-worms topos as judgment-on-self-deifying-rulers.

ACT 12:24.notes: GOSPEL-WORD AS CREATION-BLESSING-FULFILLMENT: 'ὁ δὲ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ηὔξανεν καὶ ἐπληθύνετο' uses the LXX creation-blessing verb-pair αὐξάνω + πληθύνω, which appears in Gen 1:22, 1:28, 8:17, 9:1, 9:7, 17:20, 35:11, 48:4 (αὐξάνεσθε καὶ πληθύνεσθε). The blessing originally applied to animal/human procreation is here applied to the λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ as the creational-force Luke sees operating in the post-Pentecost age. Luke-transitional-summary formula (same verb-pair pattern: ACT 6:7 'ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ηὔξανεν', ACT 19:20 'οὕτως κατὰ κράτος τοῦ κυρίου ὁ λόγος ηὔξανεν'; thematic 9:31, 16:5). Theological-narrative juxtaposition with v.23: Herod's-claim-to-glory dies; God's-word-true-glory grows. Creation-blessing-continuity implicit: the divine intent of Gen 1:28 ('be fruitful and multiply') is fulfilled through gospel-word reaching all-flesh (echoing Peter-on-Joel at ACT 2:17 and ACT 11:15-18).

ACT 13:10.notes: Paul's rebuke to Elymas οὐ παύσῃ διαστρέφων τὰς ὁδοὺς κυρίου τὰς εὐθείας ('will you not stop perverting the straight ways of the Lord?') echoes LXX phraseology — HOS 14:10 (εὐθεῖαι αἱ ὁδοὶ τοῦ κυρίου 'the ways of the Lord are straight') + PRO 10:9 (ὁ πορευόμενος ἁπλῶς πορεύεται πεποιθώς — ways-of-walking-straight). Also PSA 10:7 LXX (= MT PSA 10:7) for δόλος-filled-speech rhetoric. Not a direct citation but an OT-prophetic rebuke register that Paul deploys as deliberate scriptural-diction.

ACT 13:17.notes: Composite allusion: μετὰ βραχίονος ὑψηλοῦ ἐξήγαγεν αὐτοὺς 'with an uplifted arm he led them out' echoes LXX EXO 6:6 (ἐν βραχίονι ὑψηλῷ) + DEU 5:15 + PSA 136:12 (LXX 135:12) — the formulaic uplifted-arm-from-Egypt divine-power language. Also τὸν λαὸν ὕψωσεν 'made the people great' alludes to EXO 1:7, 12, 20 (Israel-multiplied-in-Egypt). Paul begins his Pisidian-Antioch sermon with the standard synagogue-homily pattern of Israel-historical-review.

ACT 13:18.notes: Paul's τεσσερακονταετῆ χρόνον ἐτροποφόρησεν αὐτοὺς ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ ('for about 40 years he put-up-with them in the wilderness') echoes LXX DEU 1:31 which has variant readings ἐτροποφόρησεν / ἐτροφοφόρησεν ('put up with' / 'nursed'). SBLGNT's reading 'endured-their-conduct' prepares the warning of vv.40-41. Also alludes to NUM 14:33 (wilderness-generation consequences). 40-years-in-wilderness formula: EXO 16:35, NUM 14:33-34, 32:13, DEU 2:7, 8:2, 29:5, JOS 5:6.

ACT 13:19.notes: καθελὼν ἔθνη ἑπτά ἐν γῇ Χανάαν alludes to LXX DEU 7:1 (seven nations Israel was to dispossess — Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites). κατεκληρονόμησεν τὴν γῆν αὐτῶν alludes to LXX JOS 14 (land-inheritance distribution). The hapax κατακληρονομέω ('give as inheritance') is a deliberately Septuagintal verb.

ACT 13:21.notes: ᾐτήσαντο βασιλέα 'asked for a king' alludes to 1SA 8:5, 19 — the people's-request-for-a-king episode. ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς ὁ θεὸς τὸν Σαοὺλ υἱὸν Κίς alludes to 1SA 9:1-2 (Saul son of Kish from Benjamin). The 40-year regnal-figure for Saul is not explicit in MT but matches Josephus Ant. 6.378. Paul's attention to Benjamin-tribe origin is possibly self-referential (Phil 3:5).

ACT 13:22.notes: Composite-citation: εὗρον Δαυὶδ τὸν τοῦ Ἰεσσαί, ἄνδρα κατὰ τὴν καρδίαν μου, ὃς ποιήσει πάντα τὰ θελήματά μου blends LXX 1SA 13:14 (ζητήσει κύριος ἑαυτῷ ἄνθρωπον κατὰ τὴν καρδίαν αὐτοῦ — 'the Lord will seek for himself a man after his own heart') with LXX PSA 89:20 (= LXX 88:21 — εὗρον Δαυίδ τὸν δοῦλόν μου — 'I found David my servant'). The third clause (ποιήσει πάντα τὰ θελήματά μου) is Paul's own summary-characterization, not a direct OT phrase. This composite-quotation technique is standard Jewish-midrashic practice.

ACT 13:23.notes: τούτου ὁ θεὸς ἀπὸ τοῦ σπέρματος κατ’ἐπαγγελίαν ἤγαγεν τῷ Ἰσραὴλ σωτῆρα Ἰησοῦν alludes to 2SA 7:12-16 (the Davidic covenant — 'I will raise up your seed after you... and establish his throne forever') + PSA 132:11 (LXX 131:11 — 'the Lord swore to David... I will set upon your throne of your seed'). σπέρμα + ἐπαγγελία language is Pauline-theological-signature (Rom 4, Gal 3-4).

ACT 13:29.notes: καθελόντες ἀπὸ τοῦ ξύλου 'taking him down from the tree' — ξύλον ('tree/wood') is the Septuagintal technical-term for the-cross (same pattern ACT 5:30, 10:39, GAL 3:13, 1PE 2:24). Alludes to DEU 21:23 LXX (ἐπικατάρατος πᾶς ὁ κρεμάμενος ἐπὶ ξύλου 'cursed is every one hanged on a tree') — the curse-motif Paul theologizes in Gal 3:13.

ACT 13:33.notes: Direct quotation of LXX PSA 2:7 (= MT 2:7): υἱός μου εἶ σύ, ἐγὼ σήμερον γεγέννηκά σε 'You are my Son, today I have begotten you.' Greek matches LXX exactly. The 'today' of the psalm is interpreted by Paul as the day-of-resurrection-enthronement (cf. ROM 1:4). Same verse cited at HEB 1:5 (divine Sonship) and HEB 5:5 (high-priesthood). A foundational Messianic-psalm-of-coronation.

ACT 13:34.notes: Direct quotation of LXX ISA 55:3: δώσω ὑμῖν τὰ ὅσια Δαυίδ τὰ πιστά 'I will give you the holy and sure [things/promises] of David.' Greek matches LXX; MT reads differently ('I will make with you an everlasting covenant, my steadfast-love for David which is sure'). LXX's τὰ ὅσια Δαυίδ τὰ πιστά renders Hebrew חַסְדֵי דָּוִד הַנֶּאֱמָנִים 'faithful-hesed-of-David.' Paul's argument: these permanent-covenant-promises-to-David's-line require an eternal-Messianic-seed (setting up the PSA 16:10 citation at v.35).

ACT 13:35.notes: Direct quotation of LXX PSA 16:10 (LXX 15:10): οὐ δώσεις τὸν ὅσιόν σου ἰδεῖν διαφθοράν 'you will not let your Holy One see decay.' Greek matches LXX exactly. Same verse cited by Peter at ACT 2:27, 2:31 in the Pentecost sermon — proof-text-continuity between Petrine and Pauline kerygma. Paul's argument-logic: David died+decayed (v.36); therefore the Holy-One-who-doesn't-decay must be a Davidic-descendant — fulfilled in Jesus's resurrection (v.37).

ACT 13:37.notes: ὃν δὲ ὁ θεὸς ἤγειρεν οὐκ εἶδεν διαφθοράν ('but he whom God raised did not see decay') applies LXX PSA 16:10 (quoted at v.35) to Jesus. The direct citation is at v.35; v.37 is the applicative-conclusion bringing the quotation home to Christ. Same LXX-matching pattern as v.35.

ACT 13:41.notes: Direct quotation of LXX HAB 1:5: ἴδετε, οἱ καταφρονηταί, καὶ θαυμάσατε καὶ ἀφανίσθητε, ὅτι ἔργον ἐργάζομαι ἐγὼ ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις ὑμῶν, ἔργον ὃ οὐ μὴ πιστεύσητε ἐάν τις ἐκδιηγῆται ὑμῖν. Greek closely matches LXX. LXX reading καταφρονηταί ('scoffers') differs from MT בַגּוֹיִם ('among the nations' — possibly reflecting Aramaic בגדים 'treacherous'). LXX's 'scoffers' reading aligns with 1QpHab interpretation-method. Paul uses HAB 1:5's original-context (Babylonian-invasion-judgment Judah-scoffers-won't-believe) as typological-warning: a new divine-work (Messianic salvation + concomitant-judgment for rejection) will be incredible — don't repeat Habakkuk's-scoffers' fate.

ACT 13:47.notes: Direct quotation of LXX ISA 49:6 (second half): τέθεικά σε εἰς φῶς ἐθνῶν τοῦ εἶναί σε εἰς σωτηρίαν ἕως ἐσχάτου τῆς γῆς 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may be for salvation to the ends of the earth.' Greek matches LXX closely. The verse is Messianic in origin (the Servant); Paul applies to apostolic-mission by-extension (as Messiah's-agents participating in the Servant's-commission). Same verse referenced at LUK 2:32 (Simeon's Nunc Dimittis) and ACT 26:23. The 'ends-of-the-earth' echoes ACT 1:8 Great Commission.

ACT 14:10.notes: The verb ἅλλομαι ('jumped up/leaped') in Luke's description of the healed lame man at Lystra verbatim matches LXX Isa 35:6 (τότε ἁλεῖται ὡς ἔλαφος ὁ χωλός 'then will the lame leap like a deer'), the locus classicus of Messianic-age healing prophecy. Luke's lexical choice (rare ἅλλομαι, only 3x NT: John 4:14, Acts 3:8, here) signals deliberate Isaianic-fulfillment framing for Paul's Gentile-healing: the Messianic age has arrived, and signs once promised for Israel's redemption now reach the nations. Also structurally parallels Acts 3:8 (Peter's lame-healing ἐξαλλόμενος) — Peter-Paul typology in Spirit-empowered ministry.

ACT 14:15.notes: Paul's creation-merism (τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν καὶ τὴν θάλασσαν καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐν αὐτοῖς) echoes the Decalogue Sabbath-rationale LXX Exo 20:11 (ὁ θεὸς ἐποίησεν τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν καὶ τὴν θάλασσαν καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐν αὐτοῖς) and the near-identical LXX Psa 145:6 (EN 146:6 ὁ ποιήσας τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν τὴν θάλασσαν καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐν αὐτοῖς). The 4-fold heaven+earth+sea+all-in-them merism is a stock LXX creation-formula also at Gen 1 (implicit), Neh 9:6, Psa 145:6, Isa 42:5, and programmatically at Acts 4:24 (believers' prayer post-arrest) and Rev 10:6 (angel's oath). This formal LXX-formula framing establishes Paul's Gentile kerugma within OT Creator-theology — God's creator-identity is the first premise of Gentile-mission preaching.

ACT 14:17.notes: Paul's general-providence catalogue (οὐρανόθεν ὑμῖν ὑετοὺς διδοὺς καὶ καιροὺς καρποφόρους, ἐμπιπλῶν τροφῆς ... τὰς καρδίας ὑμῶν) echoes LXX Psa 146:8-9 (EN 147:8-9 τῷ ἑτοιμάζοντι τῇ γῇ ὑετὸν ... τῷ διδόντι τοῖς κτήνεσι τροφὴν αὐτῶν) and LXX Deu 11:14 (δώσω τὸν ὑετὸν τῇ γῇ σου καθ' ὥραν πρώιμον καὶ ὄψιμον ... καὶ ἐμπλησθήσῃ — rain-in-its-season + grain-abundance covenant-promise to Israel). Paul applies these Israel-specific divine-providence motifs to Gentile humanity generally — the God who sends rain + food is witnessed-by-providence to all nations (cf. Matt 5:45). This is the OT exegetical grounding for Paul's 'general revelation' theology later formalized at Rom 1:19-20 and Athens-Areopagus (Acts 17:24-29).

ACT 15:14.notes: James's phrase λαὸν τῷ ὀνόματι αὐτοῦ ('a people for his name') alludes to the OT motif of Israel as YHWH's chosen covenant-people. DEU 14:2 (λαὸς περιούσιος; LXX) 'the LORD has chosen you out of all the peoples' + similar 7:6, 26:18 covenant-election-formulas. 2CH 7:14 'my people who are called by my name' also echoes here. James's rhetorical move is to expand this people-for-God's-name category to include ἔθνη (Gentiles), preparing for the Amos 9:11-12 proof-text in vv.16-18. Not a direct quotation — thematic-allusion using covenant-language.

ACT 15:15.notes: Introduction-formula καθὼς γέγραπται ('as it is written') transitioning into the Amos 9:11-12 LXX citation that follows in vv.16-18. The plural οἱ λόγοι τῶν προφητῶν ('the words of the prophets') anticipates a composite-prophetic-argument grounded in the Amos text. See ACT 15:16, 15:17, 15:18 for the full citation content. James's rhetorical-practice is to preface Scripture-citation with a formula that signals his scriptural-proof is about to follow — standard early-Christian argumentation-pattern.

ACT 15:16.notes: DIRECT CITATION of Amos 9:11 LXX (continues into vv.17-18 citing Amos 9:12). James opens with μετὰ ταῦτα ἀναστρέψω ('after these things I will return') — LXX has ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ ('on that day') at Am 9:11; the 'μετὰ ταῦτα ἀναστρέψω' substitution may reflect a variant-LXX-reading or James's exegetical-connective tying Amos to the post-Jesus era. The phrase τὴν σκηνὴν Δαυὶδ τὴν πεπτωκυῖαν καὶ τὰ κατεσκαμμένα αὐτῆς ἀνοικοδομήσω καὶ ἀνορθώσω αὐτήν closely matches LXX Am 9:11. MT Hebrew reads 'booth of David that is fallen' (parallel imagery). James uses this LXX-based text as the scriptural grounding for Gentile-inclusion, with the key hinge being the LXX reading at v.17 (Am 9:12) — see that entry.

ACT 15:17.notes: DIRECT CITATION continues Amos 9:12 LXX — the theologically-critical verse. MT reads יִירְשׁוּ אֶת־שְׁאֵרִית אֱדוֹם ('they may possess the remnant of Edom'); LXX reads ἐκζητήσωσιν οἱ κατάλοιποι τῶν ἀνθρώπων τὸν κύριον ('the remnant of men may seek the Lord'). The LXX reading substitutes 'seek' (דרשׁ) for 'possess' (ירשׁ) — visually very similar Hebrew roots differing only by one letter (dalet/yod) — plus reads אָדָם (humanity) for אֱדוֹם (Edom, same consonants). James's argument for Gentile-inclusion without-circumcision depends entirely on the LXX reading — the MT gives him no Gentile-universal proof-text. NT scholarly-consensus treats this as James quoting from an existing Greek-Bible tradition, possibly reflecting a Hebrew Vorlage close to one attested at Qumran (4QFlorilegium cites Amos 9:11 in a Messianic-context). The phrase 'all the Gentiles upon whom my name has been called' parallels the OT covenant-ownership-formula (cf. 2CH 7:14). κατάλοιπος here is hapax in NT; very common in LXX (e.g., ISA 11:11, 37:4, MIC 2:12) for the remnant-motif.

ACT 15:18.notes: Closing tag of the Amos 9:11-12 citation. γνωστὰ ἀπ' αἰῶνος ('known from eternity') functions as the divine-signature sealing the quotation. SBLGNT reading (accepted here) is shorter than the Byzantine conflation that reads 'known to God are all his works from eternity.' See vv.16 + 17 entries for the full citation structure. James treats the divine-plan-to-include-Gentiles as eternally-foreseen, not-a-new-divine-initiative.

ACT 15:20.notes: The four-regulation Apostolic Decree alludes to Leviticus 17-18 — the 'Holiness Code' section applied to גֵּר ('resident-alien / sojourner') living within Israel. LEV 17:8-9 (sacrifices-only-at-tabernacle; prohibits-idol-sacrifice), LEV 17:10-14 (prohibits-eating-blood; includes the sojourner), LEV 17:13-14 (proper-slaughter draining-blood; prohibits-strangled-meat), LEV 18:6-30 (sexual-prohibitions explicitly bound on-sojourners at v.26). GEN 9:4 (Noah-covenant blood-prohibition as universal-human-law). Early-Christian-interpretation read these Levitical-sojourner-rules as the natural-framework for Gentile-believers integrating into Messianic-covenant without-full-Torah-conversion. Cited at v.29 with variant wording. Not a direct-quotation — thematic-allusion grounding the four-restrictions in the OT-stranger-code.

ACT 16:17.notes: The python-spirit's title tou theou tou hupsistou ('the Most High God') is the standard LXX rendering of Hebrew El-Elyon — used of YHWH from Genesis 14:18-22 (Melchizedek's blessing of Abram) onward. The hupsistos-title also appears in Daniel 3:26 LXX (Nebuchadnezzar's confession) and Psalm 7:17 LXX (psalmist's praise). Critically, the title was BIDIRECTIONALLY-functional: in Hellenistic-Jewish circles hupsistos = YHWH El-Elyon; in Hellenistic-pagan circles hupsistos = Zeus-the-supreme-deity. The python-spirit's choice of this term exploits the religious-ambiguity — pagans hear Zeus, Jews hear YHWH. Paul's exorcism (v.18) does not deny the title's truth-content but rejects the corrupt source. Allusion not direct citation — the OT vocabulary informs the title's evangelical-theological-frame.

ACT 16:26.notes: The earthquake-prison-deliverance scene (foundations shaken, doors opened, chains loosed) thematically echoes Psalm 107:14-16 LXX (Ps 106 LXX numbering): 'kai exegagen autous ek skotous kai skias thanatou kai tous desmous auton dierrexen ... hoti synetripsen pylas chalkas kai mochlous siderous syneklasen' (he brought them out of darkness and shadow of death, and burst their chains asunder ... for he shattered bronze gates and broke iron bars). The Psalm-tradition of YHWH-as-prison-deliverer informs Luke's framing of the divine-action. Paul and Silas singing hymns in v.25 may well have been singing exactly this kind of deliverance-Psalm. Thematic-allusion not verbatim-quotation.

ACT 17:16.notes: Luke's word-choice paroxyneto to pneuma autou ('his spirit was being-provoked') deliberately echoes the LXX divine-anger-against-idolatry vocabulary cluster: PSA 106:29 LXX (Ps 105:29: parexynan auton en tois epitedeumasin auton 'they provoked him with their practices' — re Israel's idolatry); DEU 9:7-8, 9:18 (parexynate kurion 'you provoked the LORD' re golden-calf episode); DEU 32:16, 32:21 LXX (Song of Moses: parexynan me en allotriois 'they provoked me with strange-gods', en eidolois 'with idols'); HOS 8:5; ZEC 8:14. Paul's emotional reaction to Athenian saturation-with-idols is theologically-charged — his anger mirrors divine wrath at idolatry as recorded in OT prophets and Mosaic Law. The verb-cluster preserves a Lukan editorial statement that Paul's pastoral indignation conforms to divine moral-disposition.

ACT 17:24.notes: Paul's opening claim 'God who made the world and everything in it ... Lord of heaven and earth' fuses the Genesis 1:1 LXX vocabulary (epoiesen ho theos ton ouranon kai ten gen) with the merism heaven-and-earth (PSA 115:15 = LXX 113:23 ho ouranos kai he ge; cf. GEN 14:19, 22). The 'does not dwell in temples made-with-hands' (ouk en cheiropoietois naois katoikei) directly echoes 1 KGS 8:27 LXX (Solomon's temple-dedication: 'Will God indeed dwell on earth? Behold heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you, much less this house...') and ISA 66:1-2 LXX (heaven my throne, earth my footstool — what house would you build for me?). cheiropoietos is the LXX-technical-term for IDOLS in the prophetic anti-idol-polemic (LEV 26:1, 26:30; ISA 31:7, 46:6; HAB 2:18) — Paul polemically extends the idol-vocabulary to Athenian temple-architecture itself.

ACT 17:25.notes: Paul's 'He himself gives all life and breath' (didous pasi zoen kai pnoen) echoes GEN 2:7 LXX (enephusesen eis to prosopon autou pnoen zoes 'he breathed into his face the breath of life') and ISA 42:5 LXX ('the God who made the heaven ... who gives breath [pnoen] to the people on it'). The premise that God needs nothing from human hands (ouk ... therapeuetai prosdeomenos tinos) echoes PSA 50:10-13 LXX (Ps 49 LXX) where God says 'if I were hungry, I would not tell you, for the world is mine and all its fullness ... do I eat the flesh of bulls or drink the blood of goats?' Together these texts form the OT-prophetic critique of cultic-feeding-of-the-deity.

ACT 17:26.notes: Paul's anthropological claim 'from one man he made every nation of humans to dwell on the whole face of the earth, having determined appointed times and the boundaries of their dwelling' weaves together: GEN 1:27 LXX (creation of one Adam from whom all descend); GEN 1:28 LXX cultural-mandate (be-fruitful-fill-the-earth); DEU 32:8 LXX (Song of Moses) which contains the Vorlage that Paul presupposes — '[the Most High] set the bounds (horia) of the peoples according to the number of the angels of God' (LXX agrees with DSS 4QDeut-j 'sons of God' against MT 'sons of Israel'); and PSA 74:17 LXX (Ps 73 LXX: 'you set all the borders [horia] of the earth'). horothesia (boundary-setting, hapax NT) is the verbal-cognate of horia in DEU 32:8 LXX — Paul deliberately invokes the Mosaic-eschatological-passage that defines national boundaries as divine-allotment. This is anti-autochthony argument: Athenians cannot claim primacy by virtue of being earth-born.

ACT 17:27.notes: Paul's seek-and-find appeal echoes DEU 4:29 LXX (zetesete ekei kurion ton theon ... kai heuresete) and ISA 55:6 LXX (zetesate ton kurion). The grope-blindly metaphor (psephesian) echoes DEU 28:29 LXX where blind-groping is judicial-curse — Paul's irony: idolaters with eyes nonetheless grope as if blind. The God-not-far claim (ou makran ... hyparchonta) directly parallels JER 23:23 LXX ('Am I a God at hand, declares the LORD, and not a God far off?'), PSA 145:18 LXX (Ps 144: 'the Lord is near to all who call upon him'), and DEU 4:7 LXX ('what nation has gods so near to it as the LORD our God?'). Paul's monotheism is presence-based not deistic-distance — counter to Epicurean view of distant non-interfering gods.

ACT 17:30.notes: Paul's claim that God 'overlooked' (hyperidon) the times of ignorance until now extends his earlier Lystra-speech (ACT 14:16: God 'allowed [eiasen] all the nations to go their own ways' in past generations) and parallels ROM 3:25 (paresin 'passing-over' of former sins in divine forbearance) and ROM 2:4 (the kindness of God leading to repentance). The OT-resonance is implicit rather than direct-citation: divine-forbearance-leading-to-eschatological-judgment is the Pauline theological frame, drawing on the prophetic texts that announce eschatological judgment after a period of divine patience (e.g., HAB 1-3, JOEL 2).

ACT 17:31.notes: Paul's eschatological-judgment claim 'he has set a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness' (krinein ten oikoumenen en dikaiosune) reproduces the formulaic LXX-Psalter judgment-vocabulary: PSA 9:8 LXX (9:9 LXX numbering: krinei ten oikoumenen en dikaiosune); PSA 96:13 LXX (Ps 95 LXX: krinei ten oikoumenen en dikaiosune); PSA 98:9 LXX (Ps 97 LXX: same formula). All three psalter-occurrences use the identical LXX-phrase oikoumenen en dikaiosune. ECC 12:14 closes the wisdom-tradition with universal-judgment-of-all-deeds. Paul deploys the canonical OT-judgment-formula but locates the appointed-judge specifically in the resurrected Jesus — the Pauline eschatological-pivot.

ACT 18:10.notes: Two OT-allusion-clusters in this verse: (1) 'egō eimi meta sou' (I-am-with-you) covenant-presence formula — GEN 26:24 LXX to Isaac; GEN 28:15 to Jacob ('egō eimi meta sou'); EXO 3:12 LXX to Moses; JOS 1:5 LXX to Joshua; JER 1:8 + 1:19 to Jeremiah. Same Greek verbal construction (egō eimi + meta + sou) repeated. (2) Extended 'laos moi polys en tē polei tautē' — 'I have-people many in this city'. The noun laos is LXX-technical for God's-covenant-people (Israel); here Jesus-to-Paul extends the term prospectively to include yet-to-be-converted Gentile Corinthians. Parallel theological-extension at ACT 15:14 (James: 'God chose from among the Gentiles a laos for-his-name'); cf. ISA 43:4 'laos mou' + ROM 9:25-26 (Paul's citation of HOS 2:23 LXX for Gentile-inclusion). The pre-election-of-many theology motivates Paul's 18-month stay (v.11).

ACT 18:18.notes: Paul's hair-shaving at Cenchrea (keiramenos ... ten kephalen) linked to his euchē (vow) is almost-certainly the Nazirite-vow-completion ritual per NUM 6:1-21 LXX. NUM 6:5 prescribes that during the vow-period the consecrated-person must not cut hair; NUM 6:18 prescribes that at the completion of the vow the Nazirite shaves his consecrated-hair 'at the door of the tent of meeting' and burns it on the fire of the peace-offering — requiring the altar at Jerusalem, which explains Paul's subsequent journey up-to-Jerusalem in v.22 (anabas). Luke gives no reason for the vow (possible motivations: post-Gallio thanksgiving, pre-voyage-safety prayer, consecration-of-ministry). Same Nazirite-pattern recurs ACT 21:23-26 where Paul sponsors four Nazirite-completions at Jerusalem to demonstrate Torah-compatibility. Confirms Luke's Paul-as-Torah-observant-Christian apologetic (cf. 1COR 9:20 'to-Jews-as-a-Jew').

ACT 18:6.notes: Paul's declaration 'Your blood be on your own heads! I am clean' draws on the OT watchman/blood-guilt formula. EZE 33:4-6 LXX establishes the watchman-principle — if the watchman warns, the blood-guilt transfers to those-who-ignored-the-warning; if he fails to warn, blood-guilt stays with him. 2SA 1:16 LXX (David's sentence on Amalekite: to haima sou epi ten kephalen sou) and 1KI 2:33 LXX (Solomon on Joab) provide the self-responsibility idiom. Paul applies the Ezekielian watchman-principle: having fulfilled his warning-duty at the Corinthian synagogue, he is ceremonially 'clean' (katharos) of their blood-guilt. The symbolic gesture ektinaxamenos ta himatia (shook-out-garments) parallels the Deut 25:9 ritual and the dominical instruction MAT 10:14 / LUK 9:5 (shake-dust-from-feet).

ACT 18:9.notes: The divine 'me phobou' (do-not-fear) + 'lalei kai me siopeses' (speak + do-not-be-silent) doublet draws on the OT prophet-commissioning formula. GEN 15:1 (Abram: me phobou); GEN 26:24 LXX (Isaac: me phobou, meta sou gar egō eimi); GEN 46:3 (Jacob: me phobou katabenai eis Aigypton); ISA 41:10 LXX (me phobou, meta sou gar egō eimi); ISA 43:1, 5 (me phobou); JER 1:8 LXX (me phobou apo prosopou auton, dioti meta sou egō eimi). Paul is commissioned in the prophetic-patriarch pattern — the Corinthian vision places him in continuity with Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and Jeremiah as recipients of the same divine-reassurance formula.

ACT 19:17.notes: 'emegalyneto to onoma tou kyriou Iesou' — 'the name of the Lord Jesus was magnified' — draws on the LXX-formulaic divine-name-exaltation language. PSA 34:3 LXX (megalynate ton kyrion syn emoi); 2SA 7:26 LXX (megalyntheie to onoma sou); MAL 1:11 LXX (emegalynthe to onoma mou en tois ethnesin); LUK 1:46 Magnificat (megalynei he psyche mou ton kyrion). The magnifying-formula is applied in Acts 19:17 to the post-resurrection name of the Lord Jesus — a high-Christology marker implying divine-identity transfer from YHWH-megalyne-context to Jesus-megalyne-context. Lukan deliberate intertextual-echo.

ACT 19:26.notes: Paul's summarized anti-idol polemic 'ouk eisin theoi hoi dia cheiron ginomenoi' ('those made by hands are not gods') preserves the LXX-technical-vocabulary cluster against idolatry. ISA 44:9-20 LXX (idol-maker mock-polemic: wood-half-for-fire-half-for-god); ISA 46:6-7 (gold-silver idol unable to save); JER 10:3-5 LXX (scarecrow-idol cannot walk or speak); PSA 115:4-7 + PSA 135:15-18 LXX (idols have-mouths-but-not-speak — ta eidola ton ethnon ergon cheiron anthropon); DEU 4:28 LXX (man-made-gods that cannot see/hear). cheiropoietos (made-with-hands) is LXX-technical-term for idols (LEV 26:1, 26:30; ISA 31:7, 46:6; HAB 2:18). Parallel to Paul's Athens-speech application at ACT 17:24-25 (cheiropoietoi temples) and Stephen's at ACT 7:48. Demetrius accurately summarizes Paul's teaching here — the summary itself is witness-to-Paul's consistent anti-idol proclamation across the Asia Minor mission.

ACT 1:20.notes: DOUBLE CITATION composed via gezerah-shavah catchword linking (Jewish exegetical technique pairing two texts via shared theme). Both quotations are introduced by γέγραπται γάρ and linked by καί. (a) PSA 69:25 (LXX 68:26) γενηθήτω ἡ ἔπαυλις αὐτοῦ ἔρημος καὶ μὴ ἔστω ὁ κατοικῶν ἐν αὐτῇ — Luke follows LXX singular 'his' where MT has plural 'their'. ἔπαυλις (NT hapax here). (b) PSA 109:8 (LXX 108:8) τὴν ἐπισκοπὴν αὐτοῦ λαβέτω ἕτερος — exact LXX match. Peter applies David's imprecations against treacherous enemies typologically to Judas, establishing scriptural warrant for filling the vacated apostolic office. ἐπισκοπή is the same lexeme Paul uses of church office at 1TI 3:1 — the apostolic-succession-through-episcopal-office thread begins here.

ACT 1:3.notes: FORTY-DAY MOTIF. δι' ἡμερῶν τεσσεράκοντα ('throughout forty days') is Luke's distinctive post-resurrection datum (not found in Matthew/Mark/John resurrection sequences). The forty-day span is a deliberate typological echo of OT forty-day revelatory periods: Moses with YHWH on Sinai (EXO 24:18; 34:28); Elijah's journey to Horeb (1KI 19:8); Jesus' own wilderness-temptation forty days (LUK 4:2). In each case, forty days marks a revelatory/formational period between-times. Luke frames Jesus' post-resurrection teaching as analogous revelatory preparation — the apostles receiving decisive kingdom-instruction before the new-covenant Pentecost. Typological pattern, not lexical quotation.

ACT 1:8.notes: ENDS-OF-THE-EARTH commissioning phrase ἕως ἐσχάτου τῆς γῆς is a direct lexical match to Isa 49:6 LXX (ἕως ἐσχάτου τῆς γῆς, Servant-as-light-to-the-nations). Luke deliberately echoes Isaiah's Servant-mission at the programmatic outset of Acts, framing the disciples' witness-task as continuing the Isaianic Servant's mission to the nations. Paul makes the citation explicit at ACT 13:47 (when he and Barnabas turn to the Gentiles). This single allusion controls the geographical outline of the book: Jerusalem → Judea/Samaria → ends-of-the-earth.

ACT 1:9.notes: CLOUD-ASCENT IMAGERY: νεφέλη ὑπέλαβεν αὐτόν — a cloud receives Jesus as he ascends. Deliberate echo of DAN 7:13 (LXX/Θ) cloud-borne Son-of-Man ascending to the Ancient-of-Days. Luke's gospel-ending already invoked this (LUK 24:51 entry); here in Acts the cloud is lexically explicit (νεφέλη). The enthronement-coronation reading of Dan 7:13-14 (Son-of-Man given dominion and kingdom) fits the Ascension-as-Exaltation Christology. Companion verse to LUK 24:51 allusion; also resonates forward to the angels' second-coming promise at v. 11 (returning in the same way — cloud-accompanied).

ACT 20:10.notes: PROPHETIC-RESURRECTION-PROSTRATION typology — Paul's ἐπέπεσεν αὐτῷ καὶ συμπεριλαβών (fell-upon-him and embraced) replicates the Elijah/Elisha resurrection-prostration pattern. 1 KI 17:21 LXX (3 KGS LXX) 'καὶ ἐνεφύσησεν τῷ παιδαρίῳ τρίς' (and-he-breathed-upon-the-child three-times) — Elijah at Zarephath. 2 KI 4:34 LXX (4 KGS LXX) 'συνέκαμψεν ἐπ' αὐτόν' (he-bent-himself-down upon-him) — Elisha at Shunem. Both prophetic-resurrections involve a bodily-prostration over the dead-child. Lukan-Paul-as-prophet-typology continues the Acts pattern (cf. ACT 9:36-42 Tabitha-resurrection by Peter — same prophetic-typology). The Lukan ψυχή-statement ('his life is in him') parallels 1 KI 17:22 LXX 'ἐπεστράφη ἡ ψυχὴ τοῦ παιδαρίου' (the-life-of-the-child returned). Confirms our Eutychus-as-genuine-resurrection reading vs. apparent-death — Lukan-prophetic-pattern requires actual-death-life-reversal.

ACT 20:26.notes: WATCHMAN BLOOD-GUILT FORMULA — Paul's 'καθαρός εἰμι ἀπὸ τοῦ αἵματος πάντων' invokes the Ezekielian watchman-paradigm. EZK 33:6 LXX 'ἐκ τῆς χειρὸς τοῦ σκοποῦ ἐκζητήσω τὸ αἷμα αὐτοῦ' (from the watchman's hand I will require his blood); EZK 33:8-9 the inverse: faithful-warning discharges the watchman's blood-responsibility. Paul positions himself as Ezekielian-watchman over the Ephesian church — innocent-of-blood because faithfully-warned (cf. v.27 πᾶσαν τὴν βουλὴν τοῦ θεοῦ). Spiritual-not-physical death-language (per uW figs-explicit). Recurrent-Pauline-self-exemption from-blood-guilt (cf. ACT 18:6 same-formula 'τὸ αἷμα ὑμῶν ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν ὑμῶν').

ACT 20:28.notes: ACQUIRED-PEOPLE FORMULA — περιεποιήσατο 'obtained-for-self' echoes LXX-acquisition-language for God's covenant-people. PSA 74:2 LXX (73:2 LXX) 'συναγωγῆς σου ἧς ἐκτήσω' (your assembly which-you-acquired); ISA 43:21 LXX 'λαόν μου ὃν περιεποιησάμην' (my-people whom-I-acquired-for-myself). The verb περιποιέω + cognate-noun-acquisition is LXX-favored for Yahweh's-covenantal-redemption of Israel. Paul applies the LXX-Yahweh-acquires-Israel formula to Christ-acquires-the-church via διὰ τοῦ αἵματος τοῦ ἰδίου — christological-transfer of YHWH-language to Jesus's-redemptive-blood. Compare 1 PE 2:9 (λαὸς εἰς περιποίησιν, also from ISA 43:21 LXX), TIT 2:14 (περιούσιος, EXO 19:5 LXX); EPH 1:14 (περιποίησις).

ACT 20:29.notes: WOLVES-AGAINST-FLOCK FORMULA — λύκοι βαρεῖς preserves the LXX-prophetic-vocabulary for predatory-leaders threatening God's-people. JER 5:6 LXX 'λύκος ἕως τῶν οἰκιῶν ὠλέθρευσεν αὐτούς' (a-wolf even-to-their-houses destroyed-them); EZK 22:27 LXX 'οἱ ἄρχοντες αὐτῆς ἐν μέσῳ αὐτῆς ὡς λύκοι' (her-rulers in-her-midst as-wolves); ZEP 3:3 LXX 'οἱ κριταὶ αὐτῆς ὡς λύκοι' (her-judges as-wolves). The OT-wolves are corrupt-Israelite-leaders abusing-the-flock; Paul reapplies the trope to false-teachers-from-within-the-church. Echoes Jesus's similar wolves-in-sheep's-clothing warning at MAT 7:15 + the good-shepherd-versus-hireling discourse JOH 10:12. Pauline-prophecy-fulfilled in 1-2 TIM Asian-apostasy + REV 2:1-7 letter-to-Ephesus.

ACT 20:32.notes: SANCTIFIED-INHERITANCE FORMULA — τὴν κληρονομίαν ἐν τοῖς ἡγιασμένοις πᾶσιν preserves LXX-Mosaic-blessing language. DEU 33:3-4 LXX 'πάντες οἱ ἡγιασμένοι ὑπὸ τὰς χεῖράς σου ... νόμον ὃν ἐνετείλατο ἡμῖν Μωυσῆς κληρονομίαν' (all-the-sanctified under-your-hands ... the-law which-Moses-commanded-us as-an-inheritance). WIS 5:5 'ἐν ἁγίοις ὁ κλῆρος αὐτοῦ' (his-lot among-the-saints). Pauline-application: the Christian-inheritance is in-the-sanctified, transferring the Mosaic-Israel-as-sanctified-inheritors trope to-the-church. Echoed in EPH 1:11,14,18; COL 1:12; HEB 9:15; 1 PE 1:4.

ACT 20:33.notes: DECALOGUE-COVETING FORMULA + SAMUEL'S-FAREWELL pattern. ἐπεθύμησα 'I coveted' echoes the 10th-commandment LXX οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις (EXO 20:17, DEU 5:21 LXX) — Paul self-asserts compliance with the foundational-Mosaic-prohibition. The silver-gold-clothing triad also echoes Samuel's-farewell self-defense: 1 SA 12:3 LXX 'τίνος ὑμῶν ... εἴληφα ... ἢ τίνος εἴληφα ἐξίλασμα' (whose-of-yours have-I-taken? whose ransom?). Pauline-self-vindication patterns the prophetic-Samuel farewell-speech. Pauline-financial-integrity recurs 1 COR 9:12-18, 1 TH 2:5-10, 2 COR 12:14-18.

ACT 22:14.notes: MESSIANIC TITLE: τὸν δίκαιον "the Righteous One" as Lukan Christological title. Continuation of the chain at ACT 3:14 (Peter), ACT 7:52 (Stephen), now ACT 22:14 (Ananias via Paul) — three Lukan speakers use the same title. Echoes Isa 53:11 LXX δικαιῶσαι δίκαιον (Suffering Servant) and Ps 16:10 LXX τὸν ὅσιον σου (Holy One, already cited at Acts 2:27 Christologically). Luke builds Christological titles cumulatively.

ACT 22:16.notes: "Calling on his name" — ἐπικαλεσάμενος τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ. The phrase echoes LXX Joel 3:5 "πᾶς ὃς ἂν ἐπικαλέσηται τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου σωθήσεται" — "everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved." Already cited explicitly at ACT 2:21 (Peter at Pentecost) and reused by Paul at ROM 10:13. Same allusion at ACT 9:14, 9:21 (Damascus crowd describing believers). The Thai render "ร้องออกพระนาม" preserves the Joel-echo across all four Acts occurrences. Sacramental-baptism + name-calling theologically linked.

ACT 22:21.notes: Paul-to-Gentiles commissioning formula. Continuation of ACT 9:15 commission. Double allusion: (1) ISA 49:6 LXX δέδωκά σε εἰς διαθήκην γένους εἰς φῶς ἐθνῶν — Servant-to-the-Gentiles Song; same verse cited explicitly at LUK 2:32 (Simeon) and at ACT 13:47 (Paul to Pisidian-Antioch as his commissioning-warrant). (2) JER 1:5 LXX προφήτην εἰς ἔθνη τέθεικά σε — Jeremiah commissioning to nations; Paul echoes this at GAL 1:15. The trigger-word "ἔθνη" (Gentiles) here at 22:21 is what triggers the mob-rage at 22:22 — the Gentile-mission scope is the controversial element.

ACT 22:6.notes: Theophanic-light-from-heaven motif, parallel to ACT 9:3, ACT 26:13. The sudden bright light around Saul echoes the Sinai-theophany cloud-and-fire (LXX Exo 19:16-18, 24:17 the glory of YHWH "as devouring fire") and the Ezekielian merkabah-vision (LXX Eze 1:4 a great cloud with fire flashing, 1:27-28 the radiant kavod-glory of YHWH on the throne). ACT 22:6 amplifies this with the noon-detail (περὶ μεσημβρίαν), heightening the supernatural-character vs. solar-light. Cf. 2 COR 4:6 — Paul his own theological-reflection on the Damascus-light as creation-light theophany.

ACT 22:8.notes: Ἐγώ εἰμι Ἰησοῦς — Jesus self-identification formula. The absolute "I am" construction echoes LXX Exo 3:14 ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν divine-self-identification, the same formula used Christologically in JHN 6:35, 8:58 etc. Parallel to ACT 9:5 + ACT 26:15 (all three Damascus-road retellings). The theological freight is Christological-identity — the risen Christ identifies himself with the YHWH-formula.

ACT 23:3.notes: WHITEWASHED-WALL metaphor: τοῖχε κεκονιαμένε. Paul's invective against Ananias echoes EZE 13:10-15 LXX where false-prophets whitewash a flimsy wall; YHWH sends storm-rains to break it down. Synoptic-parallel allusion: MAT 23:27 (Jesus' whitewashed-tombs invective against Pharisees, τάφοις κεκονιαμένοις - same κονιάω verb). Paul borrows the prophetic-and-Dominical rebuke-tradition. Background: rabbinic m. Sanhedrin 4-5 forbids striking-the-accused-mid-defense (Lev 19:15, Deut 25:1-2 fair-judgment principle); Paul's curse is legally-grounded.

ACT 23:5.notes: DIRECT CITATION with gegraptai introduction. Greek matches LXX EXO 22:28 archonta tou laou sou ou kakos ereis near-verbatim (only word-order: SBLGNT-NT puts kakos after ereis; LXX before). The OT prohibition addresses the same situation Paul has just violated - speaking-evil-of-a-ruler-of-the-people. Paul's submission to Torah-discipline even when defending himself models ROM 13:1-7 (subject-to-authorities) and 1 PE 2:23 (Christ hos loidoroumenos ouk antelodorei).

ACT 23:9.notes: PILATE-CHRIST-VERDICT echo: 'we find nothing wrong with this man' (ouden kakon heuriskomen en to anthropo touto) - Pharisaic-faction's verdict on Paul mirrors Pilate's threefold-declaration about Jesus (LUK 23:4, 14, 22 ouden heurisko en to anthropo touto aition / ouden aition thanatou heuron en auto). Luke deliberately echoes the formula - innocent-witness-before-hostile-religious-leadership. Also echoes ACT 5:38-39 (Gamaliel's parallel-conditional, completed there but truncated here by violence). Paul-as-the-next-iteration of the Christ-and-Stephen pattern.

ACT 24:15.notes: Paul's two-fold-resurrection formula 'of the just and the unjust' (anastasis ... dikaion te kai adikon) echoes the LXX form of DAN 12:2 'and many of those who sleep in the dust of the earth shall awake — some to everlasting life and others to everlasting shame and contempt.' DAN 12:2 is the OT's clearest two-fold-resurrection text and the foundational source for the Pharisaic doctrine Paul affirms (cf. ACT 23:6, 23:8, 24:21, 26:6-8). The Greek does not match LXX-DAN-12:2 verbatim — Paul uses anastasis (resurrection-noun) and the dikaios/adikos pair rather than DAN's egeirthesontai (will be raised) and the zoe-aionios/oneidismos-aionios pair. Allusion, not citation. The Pharisaic-Sadducean split on this doctrine was foregrounded in ACT 23:6-8; Paul here insists 'they themselves accept' it — overstating slightly since Sadducees rejected resurrection. JHN 5:28-29 is the parallel Johannine formulation.

ACT 26:13.notes: Paul's λαμπρότης ('brightness, beyond the brightness of the sun') Damascus light echoes the eschatological 'Sun of Righteousness with healing in his wings' (LXX MAL 3:20 = MT MAL 4:2). The same Lukan word-family (λάμπω) appears in LK 1:78 (ἀνατολή 'sunrise from on high') drawing on the Malachi imagery. Allusion, not citation — Paul invokes the Sun-of-Righteousness type to identify the Damascus encounter as the eschatological-messianic dawn breaking into his life. SBLGNT-MT both attest. Per RULES §0 evangelical-Protestant typological reading: the Damascus Christ IS the Sun of Righteousness.

ACT 26:17.notes: Christ's commissioning of Paul fuses two prophetic-call topoi: (1) JER 1:8/1:19 'I am with you to deliver you' (the divine-promise-of-deliverance to a prophet sent into hostile reception) — Paul's 'rescuing you from the people [Jews] and from the Gentiles' picks up this Jeremianic deliverance-formula. (2) ISA 49:6 'I will make you a light to the nations' — the Servant-of-the-LORD as commissioned to the ἔθνη. The combined-source pattern (Jer + Isa servant-songs) is consistent with how Acts elsewhere fuses prophetic call-narratives (cf. ACT 13:47 directly cites ISA 49:6). Allusion not citation — no verbatim LXX reproduction.

ACT 26:18.notes: The five-fold mission summary (open blind eyes, dark→light, Satan→God, forgiveness, inheritance among sanctified) draws on a cluster of LXX servant-songs and prophetic-eschatological texts: ISA 42:7 (open blind eyes, bring out from prison), ISA 42:16 (lead the blind by ways they have not known, darkness to light), ISA 9:2 (people walking in darkness see great light), ISA 35:5 (eyes of blind shall be opened), ISA 61:1 (proclaim release to captives — Lukan favorite, LK 4:18). For 'inheritance among the sanctified' (κλῆρον ... ἡγιασμένοις): DAN 12:13 LXX 'you shall stand for your κλῆρος at the consummation of the days' + LEV 11:44 LXX 'you shall sanctify yourselves and be holy' as the holiness-foundation. The verse compresses the OT prophetic mission-vocabulary into a single Christian commissioning. Per evangelical-Protestant tradition (RULES §0): typological-fulfillment rendering of Servant-Song mission in the apostolic mandate.

ACT 26:20.notes: Paul's 'deeds worthy of repentance' (ἄξια τῆς μετανοίας ἔργα) echoes the Isaianic prophetic ethic — repentance must produce ethical deeds. ISA 1:16-17 LXX ('cease your evil ... learn to do good, seek justice, correct oppression') is the prototypical OT 'fruits of repentance' text. The phrase echoes John the Baptist's preaching at LK 3:8 / MT 3:8 directly (ποιήσατε ... ἄξιον τῆς μετανοίας) — the Baptist himself drawing on the Isaianic ethic. Allusion via the John-the-Baptist intertext: Paul positions his message in continuity with John's prophetic preaching, which itself stood in continuity with Isaiah. Triple-layered intertext (Isa → John → Paul). No verbatim LXX reproduction; thematic-vocabulary echo.

ACT 26:23.notes: The triadic Christ-suffer / first-from-resurrection / proclaim-light-to-people-and-Gentiles formula compresses the Isaianic Servant-Song + early Christian kerygma. ISA 53:3 (man of sorrows, acquainted with grief) + ISA 53:11 (servant justifies many) for παθητός 'subject to suffering.' For 'first from the resurrection of the dead' πρῶτος ἐξ ἀναστάσεως νεκρῶν: PSA 16:10 LXX (will not abandon to Hades) is the Petrine-Pauline resurrection-prooftext (cf. ACT 2:25-31, 13:35), implicitly drawing also on DAN 12:2's general-resurrection framework with Christ as the inaugurating πρῶτος (cf. 1CO 15:20 ἀπαρχή; COL 1:18 πρωτότοκος). For φῶς ... τῷ τε λαῷ καὶ τοῖς ἔθνεσιν 'light to the people [Israel] and the Gentiles': ISA 49:6 (servant as 'light to the nations / salvation to the ends of the earth') — the same passage cited in ACT 13:47 and LK 2:32 (Simeon's Nunc Dimittis). The combined-source kerygmatic structure echoes 1CO 15:3-4 'died ... raised on the third day according to the Scriptures.'

ACT 28:26.notes: Paul cites Isaiah 6:9 LXX in full (Πορεύθητι πρὸς τὸν λαὸν τοῦτον καὶ εἰπόν· Ἀκοῇ ἀκούσετε καὶ οὐ μὴ συνῆτε, καὶ βλέποντες βλέψετε καὶ οὐ μὴ ἴδητε) including the divine commission imperatives (πορεύθητι/εἰπόν) which Matthew 13:14 omits but Acts retains. Speaker-attribution: Paul attributes to τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον (the Holy Spirit) speaking through Isaiah — Trinitarian-prophetic-formula (cf. ACT 1:16, HEB 3:7, 10:15). Parallel: MAT 13:14 (full LXX form, fulfillment formula), MRK 4:12 (paraphrase), LUK 8:10 (paraphrase), JHN 12:40 (Isa 6:10 with text-form-divergence). The Acts use is unique in pairing the LXX-form citation with a Pauline-Gentile-pivot conclusion (v.28). Phrase-locked Thai rendering: 'พวกเจ้าจะฟังแล้วฟังเล่า แต่จะไม่เข้าใจ และจะดูแล้วดูเล่า แต่จะไม่มองเห็น' (per MAT 13:14 lock).

ACT 28:27.notes: Paul continues the Isaiah 6:10 LXX citation (ἐπαχύνθη γὰρ ἡ καρδία τοῦ λαοῦ τούτου, καὶ τοῖς ὠσὶν βαρέως ἤκουσαν, καὶ τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτῶν ἐκάμμυσαν· μήποτε ἴδωσιν τοῖς ὀφθαλμοῖς καὶ τοῖς ὠσὶν ἀκούσωσιν καὶ τῇ καρδίᾳ συνῶσιν καὶ ἐπιστρέψωσιν, καὶ ἰάσομαι αὐτούς). Speaker-shift mid-quote from prophet to YHWH-voice (first-person ἰάσομαι 'I will heal'). Heart-hardening as judgment-language. Parallel: MAT 13:15 (full LXX form, identical wording), JHN 12:40 (Isa 6:10 with significant text-form-divergence — has different verb-cluster ἐπώρωσεν/τετύφλωκεν instead of ἐπαχύνθη). Phrase-locked Thai rendering: 'เพราะใจของชนชาตินี้กระด้างไป...และเราจะรักษาพวกเขาให้หาย' (per MAT 13:15 lock).

ACT 28:28.notes: LUKAN INCLUSIO + Isaianic-Servant-allusion. The phrase σωτήριον τοῦ θεοῦ (the salvation of God, neuter substantive) is a distinctively-Lukan vocabulary item — appears 4× in NT, all in Luke-Acts: LUK 2:30 (Simeon's Nunc Dimittis: εἶδον οἱ ὀφθαλμοί μου τὸ σωτήριόν σου, ὃ ἡτοίμασας...), LUK 3:6 (Baptist citing ISA 40:5: ὄψεται πᾶσα σὰρξ τὸ σωτήριον τοῦ θεοῦ), here ACT 28:28 (Paul to Roman Jews: τοῖς ἔθνεσιν ἀπεστάλη τοῦτο τὸ σωτήριον τοῦ θεοῦ), and EPH 6:17 (περικεφαλαίαν τοῦ σωτηρίου in armor metaphor — Pauline). The four Luke-Acts uses form an inclusio: opens with Simeon promising the salvation will be 'a light to the Gentiles and glory to your people Israel' (LUK 2:32 — citing ISA 49:6 'φῶς ἐθνῶν...εἰς σωτηρίαν ἕως ἐσχάτου τῆς γῆς'); closes with Paul confirming 'the salvation of God HAS BEEN SENT to the Gentiles, and they WILL hear.' Acts 13:47 already cited ISA 49:6 directly in the same Pauline-mission context. The inclusio is Lukan theological closure: the Gentile-mission is the fulfillment of Simeon's prophecy and Isaiah's Servant Songs.

ACT 2:11.notes: LXX-TECHNICAL-TERM μεγαλεῖα: the phrase τὰ μεγαλεῖα τοῦ θεοῦ 'the mighty deeds of God' uses the LXX-technical term μεγαλεῖα that appears at Ex 15:11 LXX (τίς ποιῶν μεγαλεῖα — of God's deliverance at the Red Sea) and Ps 71:19 LXX / MT 72:18 (ποιῶν θαυμάσια μόνος). The term is reserved for God's exodus-and-deliverance saving acts. Luke: the disciples proclaim the New-Exodus mighty-acts (cross/resurrection/ascension) to all nations. LXX-lexeme allusion.

ACT 2:14.notes: PROPHETIC-OPENING-FORMULA: ἐνωτίσασθε τὰ ῥήματά μου 'give ear to my words' is a classic LXX-prophetic speech-opening. Joel 1:2 LXX (ἐνωτίσασθε ... πάντες οἱ κατοικοῦντες τὴν γῆν) is the closest — particularly apt since Peter is about to cite Joel 2. Also echoes Isa 1:2 LXX (ἄκουε οὐρανέ ... ἐνωτίζου γῆ) and Deut 32:1 LXX (πρόσεχε οὐρανέ ... ἀκουέτω γῆ τὰ ῥήματα ἐκ στόματός μου). Peter — the Galilean fisherman — now speaks with LXX-prophetic diction, a mark of Spirit-transformation. Formulaic-echo allusion.

ACT 2:17.notes: DIRECT CITATION of Joel 2:28 MT / Joel 3:1 LXX. Peter's citation matches LXX closely but introduces ἐν ταῖς ἐσχάταις ἡμέραις 'in the last days' (LXX reads μετὰ ταῦτα) — a deliberate editorial-adjustment aligning Joel with the Isa 2:2 / Mic 4:1 eschatological frame. Also adds λέγει ὁ θεός ('God says'). Peter interprets Pentecost as the inauguration of the 'last days' Spirit-outpouring. Young-men/old-men + sons/daughters parallelism breaks age/gender/social barriers of OT prophetic access.

ACT 2:18.notes: DIRECT CITATION Joel 2:29 MT / Joel 3:2 LXX. Peter's citation adds προφητεύσουσιν 'they will prophesy' (not in LXX Joel 3:2) — deliberate editorial-addition to underscore the prophetic-speech dimension of Pentecost. The male-servant/female-servant pair extends the Spirit-outpouring to the lowest social class. Paul will build on this inclusion at Gal 3:28.

ACT 2:19.notes: DIRECT CITATION Joel 2:30 MT / Joel 3:3 LXX. Apocalyptic-cosmic signs. The signs were not visible at Pentecost itself — Peter cites the full Joel oracle because Pentecost (Spirit-outpouring) is part one, and the cosmic signs await eschatological completion. Inaugurated-eschatology framework.

ACT 2:2.notes: SINAI-THEOPHANY + SPIRIT-BREATH COMPOSITE: ἦχος ... πνοῆς βιαίας — (1) Sinai theophany at Ex 19:16-19 featured wind + thunder + fire when YHWH descended (thunder, cloud, sound of trumpet exceeding loud); Luke frames Pentecost as the New-Covenant Sinai, giving the Torah-of-the-Spirit. (2) πνοή (not ἄνεμος) specifically echoes Ezek 37:9 LXX (ἐλθὲ τὸ πνεῦμα ... ἐμφύσησον) — the Spirit-breath bringing dry bones to life, now fulfilled in the new-covenant-people. (3) πνοή also echoes Gen 2:7 LXX (ἐνεφύσησεν εἰς τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ πνοὴν ζωῆς) — the breath-of-life breathed into Adam, now re-breathed into the new-humanity. Thematic allusion, not direct citation.

ACT 2:20.notes: DIRECT CITATION Joel 2:31 MT / Joel 3:4 LXX. Textual point: LXX ἐπιφανῆ 'manifest/resplendent' differs from MT הנורא 'dreadful/awesome' — Peter follows LXX. The 'Day of the Lord' (ἡμέραν κυρίου) is OT technical prophetic term for divine-judgment-day; κύριος here = YHWH per OT-context. Solar/lunar signs echo broader prophetic-apocalyptic tradition (Isa 13:10; Ezek 32:7-8; Amos 8:9; Luke 21:25 Jesus' Olivet Discourse).

ACT 2:21.notes: DIRECT CITATION Joel 2:32a MT / Joel 3:5a LXX — the interpretive-climax of Peter's sermon. 'Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.' Κύριος in Joel = YHWH; Peter will interpret κύριος as Jesus in v.36 (implicit divine-Christ identification). Paul cites the identical verse at Rom 10:13 as the Jew-Gentile universal-salvation formula. The universal (πᾶς) anticipates v.39 ('the promise is for you and your children and all who are far off').

ACT 2:24.notes: ὠδῖνας τοῦ θανάτου 'birth-pangs/agonies of death' directly echoes LXX Ps 17:5-6 (MT 18:4-5). The LXX translator rendered MT חבלי 'cords' (of Sheol) as ὠδῖνες 'birth-pangs' — either confusing חבל 'cord' with חבל 'pang' or deliberately semantic-shifting. Luke exploits the LXX-phrase for theological purpose: Jesus' resurrection loosed the 'birth-pangs' (implying a new-birth typology) of death. LXX-lexeme echo, thematic rather than direct citation.

ACT 2:25.notes: DIRECT CITATION Ps 16:8 MT / Ps 15:8 LXX, begins the Ps 16:8-11 block (vv. 25-28). Peter's hermeneutical claim (εἰς αὐτόν, 'about Him'): David spoke these words about the Messiah. The 'I will not be shaken' (μὴ σαλευθῶ) grounds the 'death could not hold Him' claim of v.24. Κύριος = YHWH in OT-quotation.

ACT 2:26.notes: DIRECT CITATION Ps 16:9 MT / Ps 15:9 LXX. Three-fold Hebrew-poetic parallelism (heart / tongue / flesh). Peter will exegete 'my flesh will dwell in hope' in vv. 29-32 — the key phrase for his resurrection-of-Messiah argument.

ACT 2:27.notes: DIRECT CITATION Ps 16:10 MT / Ps 15:10 LXX — the verse that Peter's exegesis turns on (re-cited at v.31). LXX διαφθοράν 'corruption' translates MT שחת which can mean either 'pit' or 'corruption'. Peter's argument requires the LXX-'corruption' reading: David DID see the pit (v.29 tomb) but only the Messiah avoided corruption. LXX text-critical dependence is crucial to the pesher-argument.

ACT 2:28.notes: DIRECT CITATION Ps 16:11 MT / Ps 15:11 LXX, concludes the vv. 25-28 block. The resurrection-implication: God revealing 'paths of life' to the Holy One whose flesh rests without seeing corruption = resurrection from death. Completes Peter's first scriptural proof.

ACT 2:3.notes: TONGUE-OF-FIRE IDIOM: γλῶσσαι ὡσεὶ πυρός 'tongues as of fire' uses the biblical Hebrew-Greek idiom 'tongue of fire' = 'flame of fire' (לשון אש / γλῶσσα πυρός). The clearest LXX parallel is Isa 5:24 (LXX: ὡς καλάμη ὑπὸ ἄνθρακος πυρὸς ... φλόξ). Luke deploys the idiom to describe what came from heaven as 'tongue-shaped flames' (uW TN: not a simile but descriptive). Allusion/idiom-echo, not citation.

ACT 2:30.notes: COMPOSITE ALLUSION: (1) Ps 132:11 MT / Ps 131:11 LXX (ὤμοσεν κύριος τῷ Δαυὶδ ... ἐκ καρποῦ τῆς κοιλίας σου θήσομαι ἐπὶ τὸν θρόνον σου) — Luke's ἐκ καρποῦ τῆς ὀσφύος is near-verbatim echo (ὀσφύος replaces LXX κοιλίας). (2) 2 Sam 7:12-13 — Nathan's oracle establishing the Davidic covenant ('I will raise up your seed after you, who shall come from your own body, and I will establish his kingdom'). Peter weaves both OT texts — the Psalms oath and the Samuel covenant — into a single dynastic-promise argument fulfilled in Messianic enthronement-via-resurrection.

ACT 2:31.notes: RE-CITATION of Ps 16:10 MT / Ps 15:10 LXX (first citation at v.27). Peter shifts from David's first-person ('my') to third-person ('He') to explicitly apply the text to Christ. The 'flesh did not see corruption' is the crux of the resurrection argument — Jesus' body did not decay because it was raised; David's did decay because it wasn't. See v.27 note for full LXX-text-critical details.

ACT 2:34.notes: DIRECT CITATION Ps 110:1 MT / Ps 109:1 LXX — one of the most-cited OT verses in the NT (Matt 22:44; Mark 12:36; Luke 20:42-43; Heb 1:13; 1 Cor 15:25; Eph 1:20; Col 3:1; 1 Pet 3:22). Peter's argument: David calls the Messiah 'my Lord,' implying the Messiah is greater than David, implying the Messiah is divine. The two-κύριος structure (εἶπεν ὁ κύριος τῷ κυρίῳ μου) lets Peter's sermon land the v.36 climax: Jesus is κύριος alongside God, so Joel 2:32's 'call on the name of the Lord' applies to Jesus.

ACT 2:35.notes: RE-CITATION of Ps 110:1 LXX (continuation of v.34 quotation — the ἕως-until clause). The enemies-as-footstool metaphor signals complete subjugation. 1 Cor 15:25-28 develops this as the Son's mediatorial reign until the final enemy (death) is defeated.

ACT 2:39.notes: COMPOSITE ALLUSION: (1) Isa 57:19 LXX — εἰρήνη εἰρήνη τοῖς μακρὰν καὶ τοῖς ἐγγύς 'peace peace to those far and those near' — the far/near language of eschatological-peace-inclusion. Paul picks this up explicitly at Eph 2:13, 17 as Jew/Gentile reconciliation. (2) Joel 2:32b LXX (ὅσους κύριος προσκέκληται) 'whom the Lord summons' — the continuation of the Joel quotation-block. Peter at this point likely meant 'far' primarily as Diaspora Jews; Luke, writing with Acts 10's retrospect, lets the language do double-duty with the Gentile-inclusion reading.

ACT 2:40.notes: σκολιᾶς γενεᾶς 'crooked generation' echoes Deut 32:5 LXX (γενεὰ σκολιὰ καὶ διεστραμμένη) — Moses' Song of condemnation of the perverse exodus-generation. Peter frames the current unbelieving-Israel in the same rebellious-generation typology. Paul will later reuse the Deut 32 echo at Phil 2:15 (ἐν μέσῳ γενεᾶς σκολιᾶς καὶ διεστραμμένης).

ACT 2:6.notes: BABEL-REVERSAL: συνεχύθη at ACT 2:6 uses the exact LXX verb from Gen 11:7-9 (συγχέωμεν ... συνέχεε κύριος ... τὴν γλῶσσαν πάσης τῆς γῆς). At Babel, God confounded (συγχέω) languages to scatter the nations; at Pentecost, the crowd is confounded (συνεχύθη) precisely because the languages are now united — the nations hear their own tongues from Galilean mouths. Deliberate typological inversion: Babel scattered; Pentecost regathers; diversity now serves unity in the Spirit. Lexical-verb echo, not direct quotation.

ACT 3:13.notes: DOUBLE OT ANCHORING: (1) 'God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' echoes Ex 3:6, 15 LXX — God's self-identification to Moses at the burning bush. Jesus cited the same formula to defend resurrection at Luke 20:37 (ὡς λέγει κύριον τὸν θεὸν Ἀβραὰμ καὶ θεὸν Ἰσαὰκ καὶ θεὸν Ἰακώβ). (2) 'his Servant ... glorified' — παῖς + δοξάζω echoes Isa 52:13 LXX (ἰδοὺ συνήσει ὁ παῖς μου ... ὑψωθήσεται καὶ δοξασθήσεται 'behold my servant will understand ... will be exalted and glorified'). Peter's verb ἐδόξασεν picks up δοξασθήσεται — signaling Jesus as the Isaianic Servant now glorified post-resurrection. Composite covenant-renewal language.

ACT 3:14.notes: MESSIANIC TITLES: ὁ ἅγιος καὶ δίκαιος as a Lucan Christological title. (1) ὁ δίκαιος echoes Isa 53:11 LXX δικαιῶσαι δίκαιον ('to justify the righteous one') — the Suffering Servant. Stephen (Acts 7:52) and Ananias (Acts 22:14) use 'the Righteous One' for Jesus as Lucan signature Christological title. (2) ὁ ἅγιος echoes Ps 16:10 LXX (τὸν ὅσιον σου — 'your holy one') — already cited by Peter at Acts 2:27 Christologically. Luke builds Christological titles cumulatively across Acts. Doublet-intensification with implicit Messianic reference per uW TN.

ACT 3:18.notes: SUFFERING-MESSIAH COMPOSITE: Peter claims ALL prophets foretold Messiah's suffering (παθεῖν τὸν χριστὸν αὐτοῦ). This is not a single citation but a composite-thematic invocation of the prophetic suffering-Messiah stream: (1) Isa 52:13-53:12 Servant Songs (Servant crushed, wounded, killed); (2) Ps 22 (abandoned-one's cry, Jesus cited on cross per Luke 23); (3) Zech 12:10 (looking on the pierced one); (4) Dan 9:26 (Messiah cut off). Luke's programmatic suffering-Messiah + resurrection pattern (Luke 24:26, 24:44-46; Acts 2:22-36, 3:18, 13:27-29, 17:2-3, 26:22-23, 28:23). Thematic-composite allusion; not direct citation.

ACT 3:19.notes: SIN-BLOTTING METAPHOR: ἐξαλειφθῆναι ὑμῶν τὰς ἁμαρτίας echoes LXX sin-blotting idiom. (1) Isa 43:25 LXX ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ἐξαλείφων τὰς ἀνομίας σου ('I am he who blots out your transgressions'); (2) Ps 51:9 LXX (Ps 50:11 LXX) ἐξάλειψον πάσας τὰς ἀνομίας μου ('blot out all my iniquities' — Davidic confession). The ἐξαλείφω metaphor originates in erasing written records (cf. Col 2:14 ἐξαλείψας ... τὸ χειρόγραφον for erasing the debt-certificate). Lexical-idiom echo from LXX into Peter's homiletic-Greek.

ACT 3:20.notes: TIMES-OF-REFRESHING ESCHATOLOGY: καιροὶ ἀναψύξεως ἀπὸ προσώπου τοῦ κυρίου composite-invokes OT restoration-era motifs. (1) Isa 32:15-17 LXX — Spirit poured from on high, resulting in peace and righteousness (future messianic-era respite). (2) Isa 57:18-19 LXX — God's comfort to the afflicted, 'peace to the far and near' (already cited at Acts 2:39). (3) Num 6:25 LXX ἐπιφάναι κύριος τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ ('may the Lord make his face shine') + Ps 16:11 LXX πλήρωμα εὐφροσύνης ἐν τῷ προσώπῳ σου — the face-of-the-Lord presence-metaphor. Thematic-idiomatic allusion, not direct citation.

ACT 3:21.notes: UNIVERSAL RESTORATION: χρόνοι ἀποκαταστάσεως πάντων invokes OT new-creation + restoration themes. (1) Isa 65:17 LXX (ἔσται γὰρ ὁ οὐρανὸς καινὸς καὶ ἡ γῆ καινή — new heavens + new earth); (2) Isa 66:22 same new-creation vision; (3) Mal 4:5-6 (LXX 3:22-23) — Elijah comes 'to restore' (ἀποκαταστήσει) all before the day of the Lord. Matt 17:11 uses the cognate verb ἀποκαταστήσει of Elijah's restorative work. The lexical-family (ἀποκατάστασις / ἀποκαθίστημι) is Malachi-rooted for eschatological restoration. Thematic-composite allusion.

ACT 3:22.notes: PROPHET-LIKE-MOSES CITATION: Direct quotation of Deut 18:15 with textual-variation. SBLGNT: κύριος ὁ θεὸς (no pronoun); Deut 18:15 LXX itself: κύριος ὁ θεός σου (2sg); some NT mss: ἡμῶν (ULT) or ὑμῶν (BSB translation). Peter applies Deut 18:15 Christologically — Jesus IS the prophet-like-Moses. Second Temple Judaism held this as a live messianic expectation (John 1:21, 6:14, 7:40). Stephen repeats the same citation at Acts 7:37, confirming Lucan programmatic use. Hebrews 3:1-6 develops Jesus-greater-than-Moses from this starting point.

ACT 3:23.notes: COMPOSITE CITATION: Peter conflates Deut 18:19 LXX (the warning-verse following Deut 18:15's prophet-promise: ἐγὼ ἐκδικήσω ἐξ αὐτοῦ 'I will require it of him') with Lev 23:29 LXX (the cutting-off penalty: ἐξολεθρευθήσεται ... ἐκ τοῦ λαοῦ αὐτῆς — originally the Day of Atonement cutting-off penalty). Standard Jewish pesher-midrash technique: quotation + explanatory-reinforcement. Qumran 1QS 9:11 parallel messianic-prophet expectation + covenant-cutting-off. ἐξολεθρεύω is NT hapax (only here).

ACT 3:25.notes: ABRAHAMIC BLESSING CITATION: Direct quotation of Gen 22:18 LXX (ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν τῷ σπέρματί σου πάντα τὰ ἔθνη τῆς γῆς); parallel tradition Gen 12:3 LXX (ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν σοὶ πᾶσαι αἱ φυλαὶ τῆς γῆς) and Gen 26:4 LXX (to Isaac). Paul cites the same verse at Gal 3:8, 3:16 with the Christological-singular interpretation ('seed' = Christ). Peter's use here anticipates Paul's theological move — the Abrahamic-blessing-to-all-nations finds fulfillment in Jesus the singular-seed (πατριαί = 'families/nations/people-groups' per uW metonymy). Universal-inclusion inclusio with Acts 2:39.

ACT 3:26.notes: SERMON-CLOSING COMPOSITE: τὸν παῖδα αὐτοῦ echoes Isa 52:13 LXX (ὁ παῖς μου — the Servant title, inclusio with v.13 + v.26 framing the sermon's Christology). εὐλογοῦντα ὑμᾶς echoes the Gen 22:18 blessing from v.25 — the Abrahamic-blessing mechanism is now concrete: Jesus-the-Servant, sent to Israel first, blessing by enabling repentance. Composite thematic echo closing the sermon's OT-arc.

ACT 3:8.notes: LAME-LEAP MESSIANIC SIGN: Luke deploys ἅλλομαι in the triple participle περιπατῶν καὶ ἁλλόμενος καὶ αἰνῶν — the exact verb at Isa 35:6 LXX (τότε ἁλεῖται ὡς ἔλαφος ὁ χωλός 'then shall the lame leap like a deer'). Isa 35 is the classic messianic-age-of-healing passage (blind see, deaf hear, lame leap, mute sing) cited by Jesus at Luke 7:22 as the sign that he is the Coming One. Luke shows the messianic-healing continuing through the apostles — the lame man at the Beautiful Gate is another fulfillment-instance. Lexical-echo + semantic-fulfillment, not direct quotation.

ACT 4:11.notes: Psalm 118:22 (LXX 117:22) — direct citation, MODIFIED. SBLGNT reads ὁ λίθος ὁ ἐξουθενηθεὶς ὑφ’ὑμῶν τῶν οἰκοδόμων, ὁ γενόμενος εἰς κεφαλὴν γωνίας. Peter substitutes ἐξουθενέω 'despise' for the LXX's ἀποδοκιμάζω 'reject (after testing)' used in the synoptic-gospel citations (MAT 21:42, MRK 12:10, LUK 20:17). The substitution is pesher-style modification: personalizes the council's contempt with a stronger word. Note the vocative 'ὑφ’ὑμῶν τῶν οἰκοδόμων' (by YOU, the builders) — Peter applies the rejected-stone role directly to the council members. οἰκοδόμος (agent-noun) is NT hapax (distinct from the οἰκοδομοῦντες participle in the synoptic citations). Ps 118 = final Hallel-psalm (113-118) sung at Passover — Peter's citation carries the Hallel's festal-vindication resonance. This same verse becomes programmatic Christian Messianic-Stone rhetoric: 1 Pet 2:6-7 clusters with Isa 28:16 + Isa 8:14; Rom 9:32-33 (same cluster).

ACT 4:12.notes: Joel 2:32 LXX (= 3:5 in MT/LXX numbering): πᾶς ὃς ἂν ἐπικαλέσηται τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου σωθήσεται ('everyone who calls on the name of the Lord shall be saved'). Peter cited this verse explicitly at ACT 2:21 in the Pentecost sermon. ACT 4:12 makes the Christological identification definite: the ὄνομα (name) that saves = the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth specifically. Lexical-echo cluster ὄνομα + σωθῆναι ('name' + 'be saved') connects the two Petrine sermons. The allusion is structurally programmatic for Acts: what Joel promised, the apostolic preaching delivers — and the Lord-of-the-name is now explicitly identified as Jesus (cf. ACT 2:36 where Peter concludes the Pentecost sermon with that very identification).

ACT 4:24.notes: Psalm 146:6 LXX (= LXX 145:6): τὸν ποιήσαντα τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν, τὴν θάλασσαν καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐν αὐτοῖς — Acts 4:24 reads σὺ ὁ ποιήσας τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν καὶ τὴν θάλασσαν καὶ πάντα τὰ ἐν αὐτοῖς (near-verbatim match, with σύ pronouncing God as addressee + added καί between γῆν and θάλασσαν). Ps 146 celebrates YHWH-as-Creator and defender-of-the-oppressed (vv.7-9: 'executes justice for the oppressed, gives food to the hungry, sets the prisoners free'). Every line of Ps 146:6-9 is apologetically relevant to the just-released-from-prison apostolic-community. The same creation-formula occurs at Ex 20:11 (Sabbath rationale), Neh 9:6 (Levites' prayer), and Ps 69:34; but Ps 146:6 is the LXX-verbatim match. The church's prayer deliberately opens with Ps 146 because it is THE psalm of divine-vindication for the powerless against hostile authorities. ECHO to Exod 20:11 (same creation-formula, Sabbath commandment) and Neh 9:6 (Levites' great-confession prayer, same formula).

ACT 4:25.notes: Psalm 2:1 LXX verbatim: Ἱνατί ἐφρύαξαν ἔθνη καὶ λαοὶ ἐμελέτησαν κενά. Introduced with 'God spoke by the Holy Spirit through the mouth of David your servant' — a foundational NT prooftext for Scripture as Spirit-inspired (2 Pet 1:21, 2 Tim 3:16). Ps 2 is THE primary Davidic messianic psalm, the most-cited OT psalm in the NT (Acts 4:25-26; Acts 13:33 [Ps 2:7 applied to resurrection]; Heb 1:5; Heb 5:5; Rev 2:26-27; Rev 12:5; Rev 19:15). Original historical context = royal enthronement psalm responding to vassal-king rebellion at a new Davidic accession. NT hermeneutic = messianic fulfillment, the ultimate 'rebelling nations' = those who oppose the Messiah. φρυάσσω NT hapax 'rage/snort-in-fury' (classical: horses rearing/snorting in battle); μελετάω 'devise/plot'. The believers identify the persecuting council as the very 'nations who rage' — themselves as the Messianic-king side.

ACT 4:26.notes: Psalm 2:2 LXX verbatim: παρέστησαν οἱ βασιλεῖς τῆς γῆς καὶ οἱ ἄρχοντες συνήχθησαν ἐπὶ τὸ αὐτὸ κατὰ τοῦ κυρίου καὶ κατὰ τοῦ χριστοῦ αὐτοῦ. Completes the Ps 2:1-2 quotation begun at 4:25. Original OT context: κύριος = YHWH, χριστός = the anointed Davidic king (OT-royal-anointing sense, not yet the NT-Christology title). v.27 makes the Christological application explicit: Herod + Pilate + Gentiles + Israel = the rebelling coalition; Jesus = the Anointed. παρίστημι 'take one's stand' = military idiom for 'line up for battle' (cf. LXX Jdt 6:14). The believers' pesher: the unlikely alliance (Herod-Jewish-tetrarch + Pilate-Roman-procurator + Gentile-soldiers + Jewish-crowd) against one man is Ps 2:2's prophetic template fulfilled.

ACT 4:34.notes: Deut 15:4 LXX: οὐκ ἔσται ἐν σοὶ ἐνδεής ('there will be no needy among you') — Acts 4:34 has οὐδὲ γὰρ ἐνδεής τις ἦν ἐν αὐτοῖς. The LXX-lexical match ἐνδεής (NT hapax) is diagnostic — Luke deploys the exact Septuagint word to frame the Jerusalem-church's voluntary-redistribution as the eschatological fulfillment of the Mosaic sabbath-year ideal (Deut 15:1-18 prescribes the seventh-year-release of debts + the-no-needy-among-you principle). The allusion links the Christian community's economic-ethics to the Jubilee-Sabbath vision (Lev 25 + Deut 15). The point is not Marxism but covenant-equity: the community embodies Israel's restored covenantal-economics by voluntary redistribution. κτήτωρ 'owner' also NT hapax, further emphasizing the lexical-distinctiveness of the verse.

ACT 5:2.notes: ACHAN-ALLUSION. νοσφίζομαι ἀπό (keep back some of / embezzle from) — the same Greek verb-preposition construction used in JOS 7:1 LXX (ἐνοσφίσαντο ἀπὸ τοῦ ἀναθέματος, they took for themselves from the devoted things — Achan's sin). Only other Greek-Bible use of this construction in this sense. The parallel is theological: taking from what has been dedicated to God triggers divine judgment striking the offender dead on the spot. Acts 5 parallels Joshua 7 in the new-covenant community. The lexical echo is unmistakable to any Greek-reading LXX-catechized audience.

ACT 5:30.notes: HANG-ON-TREE CURSE-ALLUSION. κρεμάσαντες ἐπὶ ξύλου is a direct lexical match to DEU 21:22-23 LXX (κρεμάσητε αὐτὸν ἐπὶ ξύλου ... κεκατηραμένος ὑπὸ θεοῦ πᾶς κρεμάμενος ἐπὶ ξύλου, 'a hanged man is cursed by God'). Peter uses the curse-text the Sanhedrin would have invoked to justify crucifying Jesus; God's raising is the direct reversal — Jesus bore the curse and God vindicated him. Same apostolic-kerygma vocabulary at ACT 10:39, 13:29, 1PE 2:24, GAL 3:13. The apostolic stock-phrase for the cross.

ACT 5:31.notes: RIGHT-HAND EXALTATION + SAVIOR-FORGIVENESS COMPOSITE. (a) τῇ δεξιᾷ αὐτοῦ ὕψωσεν echoes PSA 110:1 LXX 109:1 — the most-cited OT text in the NT — already invoked in Peter's Pentecost sermon (ACT 2:33-35) and Stephen's vision (ACT 7:55-56); here triple Lucan kerygma-use. (b) ἀρχηγὸν καὶ σωτῆρα thematically echoes PSA 16:10 LXX 15:10 τὸν ὅσιόν σου (cited at ACT 2:27) — the Righteous/Holy One vindicated by resurrection is now the Savior who grants repentance. (c) μετάνοιαν τῷ Ἰσραὴλ καὶ ἄφεσιν ἁμαρτιῶν echoes ISA 55:7 'Let the wicked forsake his way ... for he will abundantly pardon'. Apostolic-kerygma weave of three threads into one confessional sentence.

ACT 5:32.notes: TWO-OR-THREE-WITNESSES MOTIF. The 'we-and-the-Spirit-are-witnesses' pattern parallels the Torah legal-requirement of multiple witnesses (DEU 19:15 ἐπὶ στόματος δύο μαρτύρων καὶ ἐπὶ στόματος τριῶν μαρτύρων σταθήσεται πᾶν ῥῆμα). Peter structures his witness-claim as legally-compliant by Torah standards: the apostles are witnesses, AND the Holy Spirit is witness — meeting the two-witness threshold even in the Sanhedrin's own legal framework. Rhetorical-theological double-edge: the council that sits in judgment is itself being witnessed-against.

ACT 5:41.notes: PROPHET-SUFFERING REJOICE-IN-DISHONOR motif. κατηξιώθησαν ὑπὲρ τοῦ ὀνόματος ἀτιμασθῆναι is the practical-fulfillment of Jesus' Lucan beatitude LUK 6:22-23 ('blessed are you when men hate you ... for the prophets' reward is great'). The prophet-suffering paradigm itself traces to ISA 53:3 (ἠτιμάσθη καὶ οὐκ ἐλογίσθη — the Suffering Servant dishonored and not esteemed), JER 15:15 (Jeremiah bearing reproach for YHWH's sake), PSA 69:9 ('the reproaches of those who reproach you fell on me'). The apostolic rejoicing reframes dishonor as honor through participation in Servant-prophet destiny.

ACT 5:9.notes: TEST-THE-LORD-AT-MERIBAH motif. πειράζω + πνεῦμα/θεόν echoes Israel's wilderness testing-sin at Massah/Meribah (EXO 17:2-7 'You shall not test the LORD your God'; DEU 6:16; PSA 95:8-11 LXX 94:8-11 'Harden not your hearts as at Meribah'; PSA 106:14; HEB 3:7-11 expounds Psa 95). Peter applies the wilderness-testing paradigm to Ananias and Sapphira: lying to the Holy Spirit in the covenant community is the new-covenant equivalent of testing YHWH in the wilderness. Same sin-pattern, same judgment-principle.

ACT 6:15.notes: MOSES FACE-SHINING ALLUSION. Stephen's face 'like the face of an angel' (πρόσωπον ἀγγέλου) deliberately echoes Moses' post-Sinai shining-face (EXO 34:29-35 δεδόξασται ἡ ὄψις τοῦ χρώματος τοῦ προσώπου αὐτοῦ — LXX). The narrative IRONY is pointed: the false-witnesses in ACT 6:11,14 have just charged Stephen with blaspheming Moses, but the Sanhedrin looks at him and sees Moses' own glorified-face.
