Luke the narrator thereby settles the charge — Stephen stands in the Moses-prophet succession, not against it. The Moses-typology then intensifies through Stephen's speech (ACT 7:20-44) where Moses is structured as rejected-then-vindicated-deliverer paralleling Jesus. 'Face like an angel' was also Jewish-idiom for divine-presence-marking (2 Esdras 7:97; 1 Enoch 62:15-16; Dan 10:5-6) — converging-backgrounds strengthen the Moses-allusion.

ACT 7:14.notes: Stephen's 'seventy-five souls' follows the Septuagint reading (Gen 46:27 LXX, Deut 10:22 LXX, Exo 1:5 LXX — all three LXX witnesses give 75). MT has 70. The LXX adds 5 descendants of Joseph born in Egypt (Gen 46:20 LXX addition). Stephen — like other NT authors — quotes from the LXX, which was the Greek-Jewish scripture of his day. Not an error, but fidelity to his OT-textual tradition.

ACT 7:17.notes: 'The people multiplied' — Stephen echoes Exo 1:7 LXX (ηὐξήθησαν καὶ ἐπληθύνθησαν) doublet. Transition-formula from Joseph-era to Moses-era.

ACT 7:18.notes: The Pharaoh 'who did not know Joseph.' Stephen's Greek matches Exo 1:8 LXX near-verbatim (differs only by ἄχρι οὗ vs. δὲ connective). New-king + ignorant-of-Joseph setup for Egyptian oppression.

ACT 7:19.notes: Pharaoh's 'dealing shrewdly' (κατασοφίζομαι HAPAX here, exact match with Exo 1:10 LXX where Pharaoh says 'let us deal shrewdly with them') + infant-exposure decree reflecting Exo 1:22 (Pharaoh's kill-the-male-infants order). Stephen's NT-unique κατασοφίζομαι is borrowed directly from Exo 1:10 LXX.

ACT 7:28.notes: The Israelite's rejection-of-Moses formula: 'Do you want to kill me as you killed the Egyptian yesterday?' Verbatim from Exo 2:14 LXX. Stephen quotes the exact words that Exodus itself records, then at v.35 quotes them a third time to frame Moses-the-rejected-redeemer.

ACT 7:3.notes: God's call of Abraham. Stephen quotes Gen 12:1 LXX abbreviated (omits 'and from your father's house' because he telescopes Terah's death to after Abraham's Haran stay — see v.4 compression). Foundational to Stephen's argument that God called Abraham outside the promised land, i.e., God is not location-bound.

ACT 7:30.notes: Burning-bush theophany. Stephen's phrasing 'angel appeared in a flame of fire of a bush' closely follows Exo 3:2 LXX (ὤφθη αὐτῷ ἄγγελος Κυρίου ἐν πυρὶ φλογὸς ἐκ τοῦ βάτου). The angel-equals-God equation runs through vv.30-35.

ACT 7:32.notes: God's self-identification to Moses at the burning bush: 'I am the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, Isaac, Jacob.' Stephen changes LXX-singular πατρός ('your father,' sg) to plural πατέρων ('your fathers'). This exact divine-formula is quoted by Jesus at MAT 22:32 / MRK 12:26 / LUK 20:37 as proof-text for resurrection.

ACT 7:33.notes: 'Take off your sandals, for the place where you stand is holy ground.' Stephen renders Exo 3:5 LXX near-verbatim.

ACT 7:34.notes: God's call-of-Moses formula — seen-affliction + heard-groaning + coming-down-to-deliver + sending-Moses. Stephen compresses Exo 3:7-10 LXX. The Hebrew-idiom ἰδὼν εἶδον (infinitive-absolute + finite for intensification) matches Exo 3:7 LXX exactly.

ACT 7:37.notes: Moses's prophet-like-me prophecy. Stephen's citation matches Peter's earlier use at ACT 3:22 — both use Deut 18:15 as proof-text for Jesus-as-the-prophet-like-Moses. Abbreviated — omits 'him you shall hear' extender.

ACT 7:40.notes: The Israelites' demand of Aaron at the golden-calf incident. Stephen's citation of Exo 32:1 LXX near-verbatim. The rejection-of-Moses lexical-parallel to v.27: the Israelites reject God's chosen deliverer by preferring idols.

ACT 7:41.notes: Golden-calf making + sacrifice + celebration. Stephen's μοσχοποιέω 'make-a-calf' (NT-HAPAX) is his own coinage; lexically alludes to Exo 32:4 (ἐμόσχον) with celebration-at-idol echoing Exo 32:6 LXX.

ACT 7:42.notes: Amos's rhetorical question about wilderness-sacrifices. Part 1 of the Amos 5:25-27 LXX quotation continuing into v.43. Stephen reads the Amos prophecy as indicting Israel's wilderness-era worship: formal sacrifices covered inner idolatry.

ACT 7:43.notes: Amos 5:26-27 LXX. Stephen replaces 'beyond Damascus' (LXX Amos 5:27) with 'beyond Babylon' — either a deliberate theological-update (framing the exile to its fuller Babylonian fulfillment, 586 BCE) or following a variant LXX-tradition. The LXX names Molech and Rephan as Israel's idols (both NT-HAPAX here); the MT has Sikkuth and Kiyyun — a major LXX-vs-MT lexical divergence that Stephen's NT-Greek citation preserves by following LXX. 3 hapax in this verse (Μολόχ, Ῥαιφάν, ἐπέκεινα).

ACT 7:44.notes: The tabernacle-made-per-pattern formula. Stephen's κατὰ τὸν τύπον ὃν ἑωράκει echoes Exo 25:40 LXX (κατὰ τὸν τύπον τὸν δεδειγμένον σοι ἐν τῷ ὄρει). Contrasts with v.43: pagan tabernacle (Molech) vs. true tabernacle (God's pattern).

ACT 7:45.notes: God-driving-out-nations theology. ἐξωθέω 'drove out' recalls Ps 44:3 LXX (ἐξήρανας — variant verb but thematic match) and Deut 7:1 LXX (God-driving-out the seven nations of Canaan before Israel). Stephen's summary of the conquest framework.

ACT 7:46.notes: David's desire to build a dwelling for God. Stephen alludes to Ps 132:5 LXX (εὑρεῖν τόπον τῷ κυρίῳ, σκήνωμα τῷ θεῷ Ἰακώβ). TEXTUAL VARIANT: SBLGNT splits between τῷ θεῷ Ἰακώβ ('God of Jacob,' matching LXX directly) and τῷ οἴκῳ Ἰακώβ ('house of Jacob,' early-Alexandrian variant). Our text follows the 'house of Jacob' reading per ULT/uW consensus.

ACT 7:48.notes: The Most High does not dwell in houses made by human hands. Stephen's thesis echoes Solomon's own dedication-prayer at 1 Kgs 8:27 LXX: 'Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you; much less this house that I have built!' Stephen invokes OT-Israel's own theological self-awareness to refute the charge he speaks against the temple.

ACT 7:49.notes: Isaiah's heaven-is-throne / earth-is-footstool declaration. Stephen quotes Isa 66:1 LXX verbatim. The prophet's own voice authorizes Stephen's argument that God is not confined to a man-made temple. τόπος 'place' at end of verse completes the τόπος-arc from v.7.

ACT 7:5.notes: The Abrahamic land-and-seed promise. Stephen summarizes the complex of land-promise passages (Gen 12:7 first giving; 13:15 reiteration; 15:18 covenant-cutting; 17:8 covenant-of-circumcision) plus the enslavement-then-deliverance prophecy of Gen 15:13. Stephen's point: God promised land-to-descendants BEFORE Abraham had any descendants.

ACT 7:50.notes: Continuation of the Isa 66 quote (Isa 66:2a LXX: πάντα γὰρ ταῦτα ἐποίησεν ἡ χείρ μου). Completes the χεῖρ (hand) motif: human hands make idols (v.41) and temples (v.48); God's hand made creation (v.50). The hand-motif is Stephen's own rhetorical-thread converging on the Isa-quote.

ACT 7:51.notes: Stephen's triple-accusation: 'stiff-necked' (Exo 32:9, 33:3, 33:5, 34:9, Deut 9:6, 9:13, 31:27 — the classic OT indictment of Israel's stubbornness, particularly at golden calf) + 'uncircumcised hearts/ears' (Lev 26:41, Jer 4:4, 6:10, 9:25-26, Ezek 44:7-9 — covenant-disobedience metaphor) + 'resist the Spirit' (Isa 63:10 'they rebelled and grieved his Holy Spirit'; LXX Num 27:14 for ἀντιπίπτω). 3 hapax in this verse (σκληροτράχηλος, ἀπερίτμητος, ἀντιπίπτω) — densely-packed OT-indictment vocabulary.

ACT 7:53.notes: Law-mediated-by-angels — the later-Jewish tradition that angels mediated the Sinai-law-giving. Based on Deut 33:2 LXX 'with holy myriads' (reading ἀγγέλων for Heb. קָדֵשׁ). Stephen invokes this Jewish-tradition also echoed at GAL 3:19 and HEB 2:2.

ACT 7:56.notes: Son of Man standing at God's right hand. The 'Son of Man' title directly invokes Dan 7:13 LXX (ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου), Jesus's own favored self-designation. The ONLY NT occurrence of 'Son of Man' outside the Gospels (+ REV 1:13, 14:14). Stephen's confession uses Jesus's own Messianic title — making his vision a direct-Messianic claim rejected by his hearers.

ACT 7:6.notes: Abrahamic covenant prophecy of 400 years of Egyptian enslavement. Stephen paraphrases — γῇ ἀλλοτρίᾳ vs. LXX γῇ οὐκ ἰδίᾳ; Stephen drops 'and they will humble them.' The 400-year number matches Gen 15:13 exactly (distinct from Exo 12:40's 430 years; Paul uses 430 at GAL 3:17).

ACT 7:7.notes: Composite citation — Stephen blends Gen 15:14 (κρινῶ ἐγώ, 'I will judge') with Exo 3:12 (λατρεύσουσίν μοι ἐν τῷ τόπῳ τούτῳ, 'they will serve me in this place'). τόπος 'place' is deliberately ambiguous — LXX Exo 3:12 specifies 'this mountain' (Sinai); Stephen's generic τόπος sets up the argument at v.49 that God is not bound to any τόπος (temple).

ACT 8:23.notes: PETER DIAGNOSES SIMON WITH TWIN OT-ALLUSIONS: (1) χολὴ πικρίας 'gall of bitterness' echoes DEUT 29:17-18 LXX (ῥίζα ἄνω φύουσα ἐν χολῇ καὶ πικρίᾳ — 'a root growing up in gall and bitterness,' describing the apostate-Israelite secretly turning from YHWH). Peter casts Simon as the Deut-apostate-Israelite-type — outwardly in covenant-community but inwardly harboring apostasy-root. (2) σύνδεσμος ἀδικίας 'bond of unrighteousness' echoes ISA 58:6 LXX (σύνδεσμοι ἀδικίας — 'bonds of injustice' in the jubilee-fast passage). Peter implicitly points to the Isaianic liberation available through repentance. Not explicit-quotation; diagnostic-pastoral allusion.

ACT 8:26.notes: ANGELIC-COMMISSIONING FORMULA echoes OT prophet-commissioning idioms: 1 KINGS 17:9 LXX (angel-of-the-Lord directs Elijah to Zarephath), 2 KINGS 1:3 LXX (angel-of-the-Lord directs Elijah to meet the messengers of Ahaziah). The ἄγγελος κυρίου + Ἀνάστηθι imperative + directional-travel-command pattern is OT-prophet-template now applied to Philip-the-evangelist. Not verbal-quotation; genre-pattern background establishing Philip in the prophet-lineage.

ACT 8:27.notes: EUNUCH-INCLUSION — DEUT-TO-ISAIAH REVERSAL: Deut 23:1 LXX (οὐκ εἰσελεύσεται θλαδίας οὐδὲ ἀποκεκομμένος εἰς ἐκκλησίαν κυρίου — 'no one emasculated shall enter the assembly of the Lord') barred physical eunuchs from full-Israel-assembly. But ISA 56:3-5 LXX promises the eschatological-reversal: the eunuch who keeps the covenant shall be given 'within my house and my walls a name better than sons and daughters'). The Ethiopian eunuch's conversion and baptism in vv.36-38 (and his rejoicing-departure in v.39) fulfills the Isa-56 promise. Luke's characterization of the eunuch as a God-fearer who had come to worship at Jerusalem (v.27c) sets up the Deut-restriction-reversal: the eunuch who could only worship peripherally now receives full-Christ-incorporation. Thematic-fulfillment, programmatic for Acts' inclusive-covenant-vision.

ACT 8:32.notes: DIRECT OT-CITATION: ὡς πρόβατον ἐπὶ σφαγὴν ἤχθη καὶ ὡς ἀμνὸς ἐναντίον τοῦ κείραντος αὐτὸν ἄφωνος, οὕτως οὐκ ἀνοίγει τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ — exact verbatim-match with LXX Isa 53:7 (minor accent-variation only). The passage being read by the Ethiopian eunuch in his chariot. Philip takes this as his starting-point (v.35 ἀρξάμενος ἀπὸ τῆς γραφῆς ταύτης) to preach Jesus as the Suffering-Servant. SERVANT-SONG cluster: the NT's most-explicit identification of Jesus with the Isaiah-53-Servant. Parallels: LUK 22:37 direct-citation ISA 53:12; 1 PET 2:22-25 ISA 53 application. Philip's exegesis here is programmatic for apostolic Christology.

ACT 8:33.notes: DIRECT OT-CITATION continued from v.32: ἐν τῇ ταπεινώσει ἡ κρίσις αὐτοῦ ἤρθη· τὴν γενεὰν αὐτοῦ τίς διηγήσεται; ὅτι αἴρεται ἀπὸ τῆς γῆς ἡ ζωὴ αὐτοῦ — exact LXX Isa 53:8 (variant from MT: the LXX interprets מֵעֹצֶר וּמִמִּשְׁפָּט לֻקָּח as 'in his humiliation his judgment was-taken-away' making the Servant the victim-of-injustice rather than merely afflicted). The LXX-ambiguous τὴν γενεὰν αὐτοῦ τίς διηγήσεται reads as 'who will-describe his generation/descendants?' — our translation takes the descendants-sense primary (since ISA 53:10 LXX ὄψεται σπέρμα promises seed, which the NT applies to the risen Christ's many spiritual-offspring). Philip's preaching from here produces the eunuch's baptism in vv.36-38.

ACT 8:7.notes: MESSIANIC-AGE HEALING-SIGNS: the pair παραλελυμένοι καὶ χωλοί 'paralyzed and lame' healed through Philip's ministry echoes the Isaianic Messianic-age-signs ISA 35:5-6 LXX (τότε ἁλεῖται ὡς ἔλαφος ὁ χωλός — 'then the lame shall leap as a deer'). Jesus himself cited this cluster as his credential (LUK 7:22 τυφλοὶ ἀναβλέπουσιν, χωλοὶ περιπατοῦσιν). Philip's Samaritan-ministry extends the messianic-age signs to the Samaritans — continuous with Jesus' own ministry. Thematic-fulfillment, not direct-citation.

ACT 9:14.notes: "Those who call on your name" — ἐπικαλουμένους τὸ ὄνομά σου. The phrase echoes LXX Joel 3:5 (= English Joel 2:32) "πᾶς ὃς ἂν ἐπικαλέσηται τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου σωθήσεται" — "everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved." This verse was already cited explicitly at ACT 2:21 (Peter at Pentecost) and is reused by Paul at ROM 10:13. Here at ACT 9:14 (Ananias speaking) the ἐπικαλούμενοι pattern identifies the Christian community by the Joel-prophecy designator — "those-who-call-on-the-name." The allusion is technical-identifying, not fresh-citation. Same phrase recurs at ACT 9:21 (Damascus crowd describing believers) and 22:16 (Ananias baptizing Saul: "καὶ ἐπικάλεσαι τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ"). The Thai render "ร้องออกพระนาม" preserves the Joel-echo across occurrences.

ACT 9:15.notes: Paul-to-Gentiles commissioning formula. Double allusion: (1) ISA 49:6 LXX δέδωκά σε εἰς διαθήκην γένους εἰς φῶς ἐθνῶν τοῦ εἶναί σε εἰς σωτηρίαν ἕως ἐσχάτου τῆς γῆς — Servant-to-the-Gentiles Song; this same verse is cited explicitly at LUK 2:32 (Simeon) and at ACT 13:47 (Paul quoting it to the Pisidian-Antioch synagogue as his own commissioning-warrant). The τε...καί three-fold audience (ἔθνη/βασιλεῖς/υἱοὶ Ἰσραήλ) maps Paul-to-Gentiles + also-to-kings + also-to-Israel per Isa 49:6 Servant-scope. (2) JER 1:5 LXX προφήτην εἰς ἔθνη τέθεικά σε — Jeremiah-commissioning to nations; Paul later echoes Jer 1:5 at GAL 1:15 (ἀφορίσας με ἐκ κοιλίας μητρός μου). The Isa 49:6 parallel is stronger given the Lukan citation-pattern; Jer 1:5 is secondary background. No verbal-citation; this is an allusion-in-commissioning-pattern rather than direct quotation.

ACT 9:3.notes: Theophanic light-from-heaven motif. The sudden bright light around Saul echoes the Sinai-theophany cloud-and-fire (LXX Exo 19:16-18, 24:17 the glory of YHWH "as devouring fire") and the Ezekielian merkabah-vision (LXX Eze 1:4 a great cloud with fire flashing, 1:27-28 the radiant kavod-glory of YHWH on the throne). Not a verbal citation; Luke uses theophanic-tradition vocabulary to frame the Christophany as a genuine divine-glory encounter. Paul himself will later echo this at 2 Cor 4:6 "God who said let light shine out of darkness has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ" — direct personal-reflection on his Damascus-road experience as creation-light theophany.

ACT 9:5.notes: Ἐγώ εἰμι Ἰησοῦς — Jesus self-identification formula. The absolute "I am" construction (ἐγώ εἰμι + predicate) echoes the LXX Exo 3:14 divine-self-identification (ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν "I am the existing-one"). This is the same formula used by the Johannine-Jesus for his absolute "I AM" sayings (JHN 6:35, 8:58, 10:11, etc.). Luke is not directly quoting LXX Exo 3:14 here; rather, the ἐγώ εἰμι self-disclosure pattern is intertextually charged — the risen Christ identifies himself with the ἐγώ εἰμι formula that LXX Exo 3:14 uses for YHWH himself. Allusion rather than citation; the theological freight is Christological-identity.

GAL 1:15.notes: Paul echoes the prophetic-call language of Jeremiah (called from the womb to be a prophet to the nations) and Isaiah 49 (Servant called from mother's womb). Frames his apostleship as divinely-foreordained from before birth, in line with OT prophetic vocations. ADDED TO NT_OT_CITATIONS for GAL 1.

GAL 2:16.notes: Paul echoes Psalm 143:2 LXX (οὐ δικαιωθήσεται ἐνώπιόν σου πᾶς ζῶν / no one living shall be justified before you), changing πᾶς ζῶν to πᾶσα σάρξ and adding ἐξ ἔργων νόμου to apply the universal-no-justification statement to the law-works question. Same allusion recurs Rom 3:20.

GAL 3:10.notes: Paul cites the curse-formula from the Mt. Ebal covenant-renewal ceremony. Argument: the law itself pronounces curse on anyone failing to keep ALL commands continuously. Since perfect law-keeping is impossible, all who depend on law-works are by definition under the law's own curse.

GAL 3:11.notes: Paul omits μου from LXX (HOI dikaios ek pisteos mou zēsetai) → ho dikaios ek pisteos zēsetai. Same citation: Rom 1:17; Heb 10:38. The thesis-verse of Pauline justification doctrine.

GAL 3:12.notes: Polemical-counterpoint to Hab 2:4 in v.11. Where Hab 2:4 = righteous live by FAITH, Lev 18:5 = doer lives by [the commands]. The two principles are mutually exclusive. Paul exploits Lev 18:5 as Torah's OWN witness against Torah-righteousness as a salvation system. Same citation: Rom 10:5.

GAL 3:13.notes: Paul changes LXX kekatēramenos hupo theou pas kremamenos epi xulou → epikataratos pas ho kremamenos epi xulou (matches v.10 epikataratos for cognate-echo; drops hupo theou). Applied to Christ's crucifixion: he bore the law's curse-pronouncement substitutionarily.

GAL 3:16.notes: Composite-promise reference exploiting the SINGULAR form of σπέρμα (sperma) across the Abrahamic-promise texts. Paul's christotypological hermeneutic reads the singular as referring to Christ as the one ultimate descendant in whom the promises culminate.

GAL 3:20.notes: theos heis estin echoes the Shema (Deut 6:4 LXX kurios ho theos hēmōn kurios heis estin). Paul applies the monotheistic confession not as polemic against polytheism but as supporting argument: God acting alone (without mediator) — as in the Abraham promise — implies one-party-divine-guarantee, contrast with the two-party Sinai covenant (which required mediator).

GAL 3:28.notes: arsen kai thēlu (male AND female; using kai not oude as in pairs 1-2) consciously echoes Gen 1:27 LXX (arsen kai thēlu epoiēsen autous). Suggests new-creation reality where created-distinctions are transcended in Christ, not abolished.

GAL 3:6.notes: Foundation text of Pauline justification-by-faith doctrine. Paul cites Gen 15:6 LXX verbatim except conjunction order. Same citation: Rom 4:3, 9, 22; Jas 2:23. Argument: Abraham was declared righteous BEFORE he was circumcised (Gen 17), so circumcision/law cannot be the basis of righteousness.

GAL 3:8.notes: Composite citation blending Gen 12:3 (en soi blessed) and Gen 18:18 (panta ta ethnē = all the nations). Paul preserves the universalist phrasing of 18:18 with the en soi of 12:3. The Abrahamic blessing-of-the-nations IS the gospel preached in advance (proeuangelizomai HAPAX).

GAL 4:22.notes: Summary reference to the Hagar/Sarah / Ishmael/Isaac narrative complex. Paul does not quote a specific verse; he summarizes the well-known Genesis story for the allegorical typology of vv.22-31.

GAL 4:27.notes: Verbatim LXX citation. Isaiah 54:1 originally addressed exiled-Israel (the desolate-Jerusalem) with promise of post-exilic flourishing. Paul re-applies it to the new-covenant Sarah-equivalent: heavenly-Jerusalem-mother will have more children (Gentiles + Jews believing) than earthly-Jerusalem-mother.

GAL 4:30.notes: Sarah's words to Abraham. Paul drops Sarah's tauto and changes meta tou huiou mou Isaak to meta tou huiou tēs eleutheras to fit the allegorical-categorical structure. Polemical conclusion to Hagar-Sarah allegory: law-bound (slave-children) cannot share inheritance with faith-promise (free-children).

GAL 5:14.notes: Verbatim LXX (kai dropped). One of the most-cited OT verses in NT (Matt 22:39; Mark 12:31; Luke 10:27; Rom 13:9; Jas 2:8; here). Paul's thesis: the entire Mosaic-Torah is fulfilled in this single command. Reduces law to love-as-fulfilling-essence.

GAL 6:7.notes: Sowing-and-reaping proverbial-formula echoing OT wisdom-tradition. Job 4:8 (those who sow trouble reap the same), Prov 22:8 (sows injustice reaps calamity), Hos 8:7 (sows the wind reaps the whirlwind), Hos 10:12-13 (sow righteousness, reap steadfast love). Paul applies the universalizing-moral-axiom to flesh-vs-Spirit sowing in v.8.

JHN 10:11.notes: 'I am the good shepherd' echoes the OT messianic-shepherd promise (EZE 34:11-16, 23-24 'I will set up over them one shepherd, my servant David'; PSA 23 'YHWH is my shepherd'). Strong allusion-cluster, not direct citation. Jesus's claim weaponizes the EZE 34 indictment of corrupt Israelite shepherds against the present religious leadership.

JHN 10:16.notes: 'There will be one flock and one shepherd' near-quotes EZE 37:24 'David my servant shall be king over them, and they shall all have one shepherd' (cf. EZE 34:23 same theme). The ἄλλα πρόβατα ('other sheep not of this fold') extends the promise beyond ethnic Israel — Gentile inclusion in messianic-flock prophecy.

JHN 10:34.notes: ἐγὼ εἶπα Θεοί ἐστε — direct quotation of LXX PSA 81:6 (= MT 82:6) 'I have said, you are gods, sons of the Most High, all of you.' Jesus uses this in qal-vahomer argument: if Scripture calls human judges 'gods,' the Father-sanctified-and-sent Son can certainly be called Son-of-God without blasphemy.

JHN 10:36.notes: ἡγίασεν ('sanctified, set apart') of the Father's act toward Jesus echoes priestly-consecration patterns: EXO 28:41 (Aaron and his sons consecrated to priesthood); JER 1:5 ('before I formed you in the womb I sanctified you, I appointed you a prophet to the nations'). Jesus's sanctified-and-sent designation patterns the OT consecration-for-divine-mission motif while transcending it (Father-Son sanctification rather than human priest/prophet).

JHN 11:27.notes: ὁ εἰς τὸν κόσμον ἐρχόμενος ('the One coming into the world') in Martha's confession echoes the prophet-like-Moses promise of DEU 18:15 'I will raise up for them a prophet like you from among their brothers ... and put my words in his mouth.' Standard 1st-cent. Jewish messianic expectation; cf. JHN 6:14 ('the prophet who is to come into the world'); JHN 1:21 (questioning of John the Baptist about 'the Prophet').

JHN 11:52.notes: 'Scattered children of God gathered into one' echoes the OT regathering-from-exile motif: EZE 36:24 ('I will take you from the nations and gather you'); DEU 30:3-5 (regathering-of-the-dispersed promise); ISA 11:12 ('he shall assemble the dispersed of Israel'). The Johannine extension: the regathering scope is universalized to include Gentiles ('children of God' = broader than ethnic Israel) — the atonement scope is universal-yet-particular.

JHN 12:13.notes: ὡσαννά + εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου — direct citation of PSA 118:25-26 (the Hallel psalm, recited at Passover). 'Hosanna' = Hebrew הוֹשִׁיעָה־נָּא 'save-please now'. The crowd's triumphal-entry acclamation. Same citation appears at MRK 11:9-10, MAT 21:9, LUK 19:38 (Synoptic parallels).

JHN 12:15.notes: Μὴ φοβοῦ θυγάτηρ Σιών (ZEP 3:16 'do not fear, Zion') + ὁ βασιλεύς σου ἔρχεται καθήμενος ἐπὶ πῶλον ὄνου (ZEC 9:9 'your king comes ... humble and mounted on a donkey'). John gives the gist of ZEC 9:9 rather than verbatim. The peaceful-king-on-donkey deliberately contrasts with Maccabean victory-warrior expectations.

JHN 12:38.notes: Κύριε τίς ἐπίστευσεν τῇ ἀκοῇ ἡμῶν — direct LXX citation of ISA 53:1 (opening of the Suffering Servant chapter). Same verse cited at ROM 10:16. John's contextual application: the unbelief of Israel was prophesied; Jesus's rejection fits the Servant-Messiah pattern.

JHN 12:40.notes: Τετύφλωκεν αὐτῶν τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς ... ἐπώρωσεν αὐτῶν τὴν καρδίαν — paraphrastic citation of ISA 6:10 (the hardening-prophecy from Isaiah's commission). Same verse cited variously at MRK 4:12, MAT 13:14-15, LUK 8:10, ACT 28:26-27. The Johannine version is closer to the MT than the LXX in some details. Theologically grounds the unbelief-of-Israel pattern.

JHN 13:18.notes: ὁ τρώγων μου τὸν ἄρτον ἐπῆρεν ἐπ’ἐμὲ τὴν πτέρναν αὐτοῦ — citation of LXX PSA 40:10 (= MT 41:9). David's psalm of betrayal by an intimate friend (traditionally Ahithophel, 2 SAM 15-17). Two compressed idioms: 'eating my bread' = covenant-friendship; 'lifted up his heel' = enmity / treachery. Jesus applies the typological pattern: as David was betrayed by his close-table-companion, so Jesus by Judas.

JHN 13:19.notes: ἐγώ εἰμι (absolute, no predicate) — Jesus's signature divine-self-identification echoing EXO 3:14 LXX 'ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν' (the divine name revealed to Moses) and ISA 43:10 'ἐγώ εἰμι' (the prophetic absolute-I-AM cluster). Same formula at JHN 4:26, 6:20, 8:24, 8:28, 8:58, 18:5-8.

JHN 14:27.notes: 'Peace I leave with you' echoes the Aaronic priestly-blessing of NUM 6:24-26 ('the LORD bless you and keep you ... and give you peace'). Christ as the eternal high-priest dispenses the priestly-blessing-peace. Also prefigures the resurrection-greeting 'εἰρήνη ὑμῖν' (JHN 20:19, 21, 26).

JHN 14:8.notes: Philip's request 'show us the Father' echoes Moses's theophany-petition in EXO 33:18 ('show me your glory'). The OT pattern of seeking-divine-vision is fulfilled in Christ — the Father is being seen in the Son's face (per v.9). Implicit-allusion that orients Philip's request as a continuation of OT theophany-petitions.

JHN 15:25.notes: DIRECT CITATION of PSA 35:19 LXX (= LXX 34:19) or PSA 69:5 LXX (= LXX 68:5). Both psalms contain the phrase οἱ μισοῦντές με δωρεάν 'those who hate me without cause' verbatim — Davidic righteous-sufferer laments. Jesus quotes loosely with finite ἐμίσησάν (3pl.aor.indicative 'they hated') vs. the LXX's participial μισοῦντες ('hating'). Either psalm works as the source; both are righteous-sufferer typologies that Christ identifies himself with. Per uW figs-synecdoche: 'their law' (νόμῳ) here = entire-Hebrew-Scriptures (Tanakh-as-a-whole), not strictly Pentateuch — paralleling JHN 10:34 where 'law' cites Psalm 82.

JHN 15:3.notes: Allusion to PSA 119:9 ('How can a young man keep his way pure? By guarding it according to your word') — cleansing-via-word theme. Christ's word as the means by which the disciples are 'already clean' parallels the Psalm's word-as-purifier. Echoes also EPH 5:26 'washing of water with the word' (NT-internal, not OT-claim).

JHN 17:12.notes: FULFILLMENT formula ἵνα ἡ γραφὴ πληρωθῇ — Christ identifies Judas's loss as fulfilling Scripture. Likely sources: PSA 41:9 LXX (= LXX 40:10) ‘even my close friend ... has lifted his heel against me,’ already cited explicitly at JHN 13:18. Also possibly PSA 109:8 (LXX 108:8) ‘may another take his office,’ cited at ACT 1:20 of Judas's replacement. The ‘son of destruction’ idiom (ὁ υἱὸς τῆς ἀπωλείας) typifies Judas as the-one-characterized-by-destruction; Hebraism repeated of the eschatological-antichrist at 2 TH 2:3.

JHN 17:6.notes: Allusion-cluster to the YHWH-name-revelation tradition: EXO 3:14 (the I-AM-WHO-I-AM Sinai-call name-disclosure), EXO 33:19 (YHWH proclaims his name to Moses on the cleft of the rock), EXO 34:5-7 (YHWH-name-and-attributes proclamation). Christ's ‘I have revealed Your name to those You have given me’ takes up the OT-pattern of YHWH's-self-revelation-via-the-Name and applies it to his earthly ministry — Christ as the eschatological-fulfillment of the Sinai-name-revelation.

JHN 18:1.notes: Allusion to 2 SAM 15:23 (David crossing the Kidron Brook fleeing from Absalom's rebellion). Davidic-fugitive typology — Christ as Son-of-David now-going-to-be-betrayed-by-disciple (Judas as Absalom-figure) crosses the same Kidron. The geographic-detail carries OT-typological weight.

JHN 19:24.notes: DIRECT CITATION of PSA 22:18 LXX (= LXX 21:19): διεμερίσαντο τὰ ἱμάτιά μου ἑαυτοῖς καὶ ἐπὶ τὸν ἱματισμόν μου ἔβαλον κλῆρον. PSA 22 is the most-cited-Psalm in the Passion-narratives — its-lament-pattern (vv.1, 7-8, 16, 18) finds detail-by-detail fulfillment at the cross. Christ's suffering is patterned-on the righteous-sufferer-Davidic typology.

JHN 19:28.notes: Fulfillment of PSA 22:15 ‘my strength is dried up like a potsherd; my tongue sticks to my jaws’ + PSA 69:21 ‘for my thirst they gave me sour wine to drink.’ Christ's ‘I thirst’ saying explicitly framed by John as Scripture-completion (ἵνα τελειωθῇ ἡ γραφή). The two-Psalms-of-righteous-sufferer cluster in cross-narratives.

JHN 19:29.notes: Allusion to EXO 12:22 LXX — hyssop (ὕσσωπος) used to apply Passover-lamb-blood to doorposts at the original Exodus. The hyssop-detail (otherwise odd to mention) preserves the Passover-typology. Christ as true Passover-Lamb (cf. 1 COR 5:7); the hyssop that-once-applied-blood now-touches-the-Lamb-himself.

JHN 19:36.notes: DIRECT CITATION (compound): EXO 12:46 LXX + NUM 9:12 LXX (Passover-lamb stipulation: ὀστοῦν οὐ συντρίψετε ἀπ' αὐτοῦ ‘you shall not break a bone of it’) + PSA 34:20 (LXX 33:21) (righteous-sufferer-bones-protected: ‘he keeps all his bones, not one of them is broken’). The compound-citation establishes Christ as both true-Passover-Lamb and righteous-sufferer of the Psalms — the not-breaking-of-bones (because Christ-already-dead before-soldiers-arrived) fulfills both typologies simultaneously.

JHN 19:37.notes: DIRECT CITATION of ZEC 12:10. Greek ἐξεκέντησαν (‘they-pierced’) reflects the MT דָּקָרוּ rather than the mainstream LXX rendering κατωρχήσαντο (‘they-mocked-or-trampled’) — JHN's quotation aligns with the Aquila/Symmachus/Theodotion correction-toward-MT. PHRASE-LOCK with REV 1:7 same-Zechariah-citation in eschatological-context — dual fulfillment: soldiers/onlookers see-pierced-Christ now, all-people see-pierced-Christ at parousia.

JHN 19:38.notes: Allusion to ISA 53:9 — ‘they made his grave with the wicked, and with a rich man in his death.’ Joseph of Arimathea is described as wealthy (MAT 27:57); Christ-buried in the rich-man's-tomb fulfills the prophecy after-being-crucified-with-criminals. The cross + tomb-pair completes the ISA 53:9 dual-fulfillment.

JHN 19:9.notes: Allusion to ISA 53:7 — the silent-suffering-servant: ‘he was oppressed and afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth; like a lamb that is led to the slaughter.’ Christ's-silence before Pilate fulfills the Suffering-Servant typology. The silence-as-eloquence motif central to Servant-Song fulfillment.

JHN 1:11.notes: His own people did not receive Him — alludes to Isa 53:3 'he was despised and rejected by men' (Suffering Servant). Establishes the rejection-pattern that climaxes at the cross.

JHN 1:14.notes: ἐσκήνωσεν (tabernacled) + δόξα (glory) — alludes to YHWH's tabernacling presence and glory in the wilderness sanctuary (Exod 25:8 'let them make me a sanctuary, that I may dwell among them'; Exod 40:34-38 'the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle'). Christological claim: Jesus is the new locus of divine indwelling.

JHN 1:17.notes: Law given through Moses formula — alludes to the giving of the Decalogue and the Mosaic Torah. No direct quotation; allusive reference to the OT framework that Jesus-Christ-grace-and-truth supersedes.

JHN 1:18.notes: No one has seen God — alludes to Exod 33:20 'no one shall see me and live' (also 1 Tim 6:16). Establishes the divine-invisibility-by-nature theology that Christ overcomes by exegeting/revealing the Father.

JHN 1:23.notes: John the Baptist self-identifies with the voice-in-wilderness prophecy. Follows LXX placement of 'in the wilderness' (modifying the voice, not the action of preparing) — distinct from MT placement. Synoptic parallels (Mt 3:3, Mk 1:3, Lk 3:4) all attribute this to John, but only John's Gospel has John himself recite it.

JHN 1:29.notes: Lamb of God title — multi-reference allusion-cluster: (1) Passover lamb (Exod 12 — substitutionary deliverance through blood); (2) Suffering Servant lamb (Isa 53:7 'as a lamb led to slaughter'); (3) Akedah ram (Gen 22:8 'God will provide for himself the lamb'). No single decisive OT reference; layered allusion. ADDED TO NT_OT_CITATIONS.

JHN 1:34.notes: Per SBLGNT reading ὁ ἐκλεκτὸς τοῦ θεοῦ (textual variant; NA28/Byz reads υἱὸς) — the ἐκλεκτός title echoes Isa 42:1 LXX 'ὁ ἐκλεκτός μου' (my chosen one) Servant-Song. Aligns with Lamb-of-God Servant-imagery in v.29. SBLGNT divergence from BSB documented in verse notes per RULES §5.

JHN 1:36.notes: Repetition of Lamb of God title from v.29 (same OT-cluster). The repetition deepens the title's function as the Gospel's programmatic Christological claim about substitutionary atonement.

JHN 1:47.notes: No δόλος in Nathanael — alludes to Gen 27:35 LXX (Isaac to Esau: 'your brother came with deceit, μετὰ δόλου, and took your blessing'). Jacob (renamed Israel) was characterized by δόλος; Nathanael is the inverse-Jacob, the renewed-Israel-without-deceit. Sets up v.51's Jacob's ladder allusion (Gen 28:12).

JHN 1:51.notes: Jacob's ladder allusion + Son of Man title. (1) Gen 28:12 LXX 'angels of God ascending and descending' on the ladder — Jesus replaces the ladder with the Son of Man (he is the heaven-earth bridge). (2) Dan 7:13-14 'one like a son of man' — universal-everlasting-dominion title. The Nathanael/Jacob intertextuality from v.47 ('true Israelite without δόλος' = inverse-Jacob) prepares this. ADDED TO NT_OT_CITATIONS.

JHN 20:1.notes: Allusion to GEN 1:3 (creation-light). The pre-dawn ‘still-dark’ + first-day-of-week echoes the original-creation-week's first-day light-emerges-from-darkness pattern. Christ's-resurrection inaugurates new-creation paralleling original-creation. The JHN narrative-pattern of cosmic-symbolism (light/darkness, creation/new-creation) supports this typological reading.

JHN 20:12.notes: Allusion to EXO 25:18-22 — the two cherubim on the mercy-seat of the Ark of the Covenant (one at each end). The two angels at head and feet of the empty grave-slab parallels the cherubim flanking the mercy-seat. Christ's empty grave as eschatological-mercy-seat where atonement is completed and witnessed. The cherubim-mercy-seat is where God ‘meets’ Israel (EXO 25:22) — now the empty-tomb is where the risen Christ meets the new-Israel.

JHN 20:22.notes: DIRECT VERBAL ALLUSION — same rare Greek verb ἐμφυσάω (NT-hapax here) appears in LXX GEN 2:7 (God breathes life into Adam: ‘ἐνεφύσησεν εἰς τὸ πρόσωπον αὐτοῦ πνοὴν ζωῆς’) and LXX EZK 37:9 (Spirit-breath into the dry bones: ‘ἐμφύσησον εἰς τοὺς νεκροὺς τούτους καὶ ζησάτωσαν’). Christ's-breathing-the-Spirit on disciples enacts a new-creation moment paralleling original-Adam-creation + new-Israel-from-dry-bones. The verbal-allusion is deliberate Johannine signaling. New-Adam Christology (1 COR 15:45).

JHN 20:9.notes: Allusion-cluster to OT-resurrection-prophecy. PSA 16:10 LXX (= LXX 15:10) ‘you will not let your Holy One see corruption’ — cited explicitly at ACT 2:27, 13:35 of the resurrection. ISA 53:10-12 (Suffering-Servant ‘shall see his offspring, prolong his days’ — life-after-death implication). HOS 6:2 ‘on the third day he will raise us up.’ The disciples' lack-of-understanding the-Scripture in JHN 20:9 refers-to this-cluster of-resurrection-prophecies — eventually-understood post-Pentecost (cf. LUK 24:25-27, 44-45).

JHN 2:16.notes: Conceptual allusion to Zech 14:21 (no merchant/Canaanite in the house of the LORD of hosts). The wording is not a quotation but an evocation of the eschatological-purification motif (cf. Mal 3:1-4 "the Lord whom you seek will suddenly come to his temple"). Jesus enacts in real time what Zech 14:21 prophesies for the Day of the LORD. ADDED TO NT_OT_CITATIONS for John 2 (Eremos translation 2026-04-26).

JHN 2:17.notes: Direct quotation from Ps 69:9 (LXX 68:10) ὁ ζῆλος τοῦ οἴκου σου κατέφαγέν με. John adapts the verb to future tense (καταφάγεται) reflecting prospective passion-application; LXX MSS show some variation between aorist and future. MT has perfect אכלתני. Psalm 69 is a heavily Christologically-applied psalm in the NT (Jn 15:25 quotes 69:4; Jn 19:28-29 alludes to 69:21; Rom 11:9-10 quotes 69:22-23; Rom 15:3 quotes 69:9; Acts 1:20 quotes 69:25). ADDED TO NT_OT_CITATIONS for John 2 (Eremos translation 2026-04-26).

JHN 3:13.notes: Jesus' οὐδεὶς ἀναβέβηκεν εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν ('no one has ascended into heaven') echoes Prov 30:4 LXX τίς ἀνέβη εἰς τὸν οὐρανόν ('who has ascended into heaven?') — the rhetorical-question denying human-ability-to-climb-to-heaven for divine knowledge. Cf. Deut 30:12 'who will go up to heaven for us...?' Jesus' point: only the One-who-descended-from-heaven (himself) has the heavenly knowledge to communicate. Greek does not match LXX wording — paraphrase-reuse of the rhetorical structure with explicit-Christological resolution. Implicit Christology: Jesus is the Wisdom-from-on-high that Prov 30:4's question implicitly seeks.

JHN 3:14.notes: Jesus references Moses lifting the bronze serpent on a pole in the wilderness — Israelites bitten by fiery serpents could look at the bronze-snake-on-pole and live (Num 21:8-9). Jesus draws a typological parallel to his own crucifixion-lifting: ὑψωθῆναι δεῖ τὸν υἱὸν τοῦ ἀνθρώπου. The καθὼς...οὕτως simile-formula. Wisdom of Solomon 16:6-7 (deuterocanonical, not authoritative for our project but historically informative) already moralized the serpent-incident. Greek does not match LXX wording verbatim — paraphrase-typology, not direct quotation. Christological-key for the Johannine ὑψόω motif (8:28; 12:32, 34).

JHN 3:5.notes: Jesus' phrase γεννηθῇ ἐξ ὕδατος καὶ πνεύματος (born of water and Spirit) alludes to Ezekiel 36:25-27 — God's new-covenant promise: 'I will sprinkle clean water on you, and you shall be clean ... I will put my Spirit within you.' The water-cleansing + Spirit-indwelling pairing is the OT eschatological-restoration theme. Per uW figs-metaphor: most natural reading. Jesus rebukes Nicodemus in v.10 for not recognizing 'these things' as a teacher of Israel — the Ezek 36 echo is the implicit-OT-substrate Nicodemus should-have-known. Also resonant with Jer 31:31-34 (new covenant on the heart) and Ezek 37:1-14 (dry bones revived by Spirit).

JHN 4:10.notes: ὕδωρ ζῶν ('living water') metaphor — draws on the prophetic-tradition of God-as-fountain-of-living-waters (JER 2:13 'they have forsaken me, the spring of living waters'; JER 17:13 'spring of living waters'), the eschatological-temple-river (EZEK 47:1-12), and the Jerusalem-as-source-of-living-waters in messianic age (ZECH 14:8 'on that day living waters shall flow out from Jerusalem'). Jesus identifies HIMSELF as the source — the prophetic-promised-spring is Christ. Recurs at JHN 7:38-39 where the Evangelist explicitly identifies the living-water as the Spirit.

JHN 4:14.notes: πηγὴ ὕδατος ἁλλομένου εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον ('spring of water leaping up to eternal life') — alludes to ISA 12:3 'with joy you will draw water from the wells of salvation' (eschatological joy-spring) and ISA 58:11 'you shall be like a watered garden, like a spring whose waters do not fail.' The Spirit-given inner-spring of Johannine theology fulfills both Isaianic images.

JHN 4:19.notes: Samaritan messianic expectation — the Tahebh ('restorer'), grounded in DEUT 18:15-19 (the Mosaic-prophet expectation: 'a prophet like me from your midst'). When the Samaritan woman calls Jesus 'prophet' (προφήτης), her conceptual framework places him on the Tahebh-trajectory: the eschatological-Mosaic-prophet who would restore Samaritan-Israel. Jesus' coming 'I AM' (v.26) accepts and transcends this framework.

JHN 4:26.notes: Ἐγώ εἰμι (I AM) — first explicit Johannine I-AM-self-disclosure (predicate-less form). Resonates with LXX EXO 3:14 ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν ('I am the existing one' — divine-name revelation to Moses) and the Isaianic ἐγώ εἰμι formulae (ISA 41:4 'I AM the first and the last'; 43:10 'before me no god was formed'; 43:25 'I, I am he who blots out your transgressions'; 46:4 'I am he, I will carry you'; 51:12 'I, I am he who comforts you' — all anarthrous ἐγώ εἰμι in LXX, divine-self-identification). The bare ἐγώ εἰμι in JHN 4:26 carries surface-Messianic-identification (answering the woman's expectation) AND deeper-divine-name resonance (especially explicit at JHN 8:58 'before Abraham was, I am'). First in the Johannine I-AM disclosure-cluster.

JHN 4:5.notes: Jacob's gift of the field at Shechem to Joseph — patriarchal land-grant tradition. GEN 48:22 records Jacob giving Joseph 'one portion above your brothers' (a Shechem land-grant, the Hebrew shechem ambiguous between place-name and 'shoulder/portion'). JOSH 24:32 records Joseph's bones interred at Shechem in 'the parcel of ground that Jacob bought from the sons of Hamor.' Johannine narrative-allusion grounding the well-locale's covenant-history.

JHN 5:17.notes: Jesus' claim "my Father is working until now and I am working" engages the Jewish exegesis of Gen 2:2-3 (God's Sabbath-rest from creation): rabbinic theology acknowledged that God's providential work continued even on Sabbath; Jesus claims the same prerogative as a deity-claim (recognized as such in v.18). Cf. Philo, On the Cherubim 87-90; Genesis Rabbah 11.10.

JHN 5:18.notes: The leaders' move to kill Jesus for blasphemy (calling God his own Father, making himself equal with God) invokes Lev 24:16 LXX (death-penalty for blasphemy of the Name). The legal basis for the persecution-program that culminates at the cross is laid here.

JHN 5:21.notes: The combination of "raises the dead" + "makes alive" (ἐγείρει τοὺς νεκροὺς καὶ ζῳοποιεῖ) draws on the OT divine-prerogative formula. Deut 32:39: "I kill and I make alive" (LXX ἀποκτενῶ καὶ ζῆν ποιήσω). 1 Sam 2:6: "YHWH kills and makes alive" (Hannah's prayer). 2 Kings 5:7: "Am I God to kill and make alive?" Jesus claims this exclusively-divine prerogative for the Son.

JHN 5:22.notes: Universal-judgment delegated from Father to Son matches Daniel 7:13-14: "to him was given dominion and glory and a kingdom, that all peoples, nations, and languages should serve him." The eschatological-judgment role assigned to the Son-of-Man figure is the basis for v.27's explicit citation.

JHN 5:27.notes: Anarthrous υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου (no articles on either noun) deliberately matches LXX Dan 7:13 (ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἐρχόμενος μετὰ τῶν νεφελῶν). This is the most explicit Daniel-7 echo in John's Gospel — Jesus identifies himself as the Daniel-7 Son-of-Man to whom the Ancient-of-Days has given universal judgment-authority.

JHN 5:29.notes: The double-resurrection language (ἀνάστασις ζωῆς / ἀνάστασις κρίσεως) directly echoes Dan 12:2 LXX: πολλοὶ τῶν καθευδόντων ἐν γῆς χώματι ἐξεγερθήσονται, οὗτοι εἰς ζωὴν αἰώνιον καὶ οὗτοι εἰς ὀνειδισμὸν καὶ εἰς αἰσχύνην αἰώνιον. The substantive shift from "life vs shame" to "life vs judgment" is Johannine theological clarification: the OT "shame and contempt" is an anthropological description; John's "judgment" names the divine-juridical reality behind it.

JHN 5:3.notes: τυφλῶν, χωλῶν, ξηρῶν (blind, lame, withered) echoes the messianic-age healing triad in Isa 35:5-6 ("the eyes of the blind shall be opened... the lame shall leap like a deer"). The crowd at Bethesda is a tableau of unredeemed Israel awaiting messianic intervention.

JHN 5:31.notes: Jesus invokes the two-witness rule of Deut 19:15: "a single witness shall not suffice... only on the evidence of two witnesses or three witnesses shall a charge be established." Self-testimony alone is legally invalid; Jesus then enumerates the additional witnesses (the Father v.32, John the Baptist v.33, the works v.36, the Father directly v.37, the Scriptures v.39, Moses v.46).

JHN 5:37.notes: "You have never heard his voice nor seen his form" alludes to Moses' uniquely-direct revelation in Num 12:8 ("with him I speak mouth to mouth, openly... and the form (תמונה / εἶδος LXX) of YHWH he beholds") and Deut 4:12 (Sinai theophany — Israel heard the voice but "saw no form, only a voice"). Jesus rebukes the leaders for not even matching their own ancestors' encounter — though they claim Mosaic authority.

JHN 5:44.notes: τὸν μόνον θεόν ("the only God") echoes the Shema (Deut 6:4 LXX: κύριος ὁ θεὸς ἡμῶν κύριος εἷς ἐστιν). Ironic indictment: the leaders pride themselves on monotheism but do not actually seek the only-God's honor — true devotion to the only-God includes honoring his Son (cf. v.23).

JHN 5:46.notes: "Moses wrote about me" includes (1) direct prophecy (Deut 18:15-19 "a prophet like me from your brothers"); (2) typology (Passover lamb Exod 12; bronze serpent Num 21:9 = JHN 3:14; Manna Exod 16 = JHN 6); (3) covenant-trajectory (Gen 3:15, Gen 49:10, Num 24:17). The Pentateuch's Christological-witness is the cumulative weight Jesus invokes.

JHN 5:5.notes: The 38-year duration of the man's illness parallels Israel's 38-year wilderness span between Kadesh-Barnea and the Zered-crossing (Deut 2:14). Johannine numeric-echo: the paralytic typifies Israel-under-judgment awaiting messianic rescue.

JHN 6:14.notes: The crowd's identification of Jesus as 'the Prophet' (ὁ προφήτης ὁ ἐρχόμενος εἰς τὸν κόσμον) draws on Deut 18:15-19 (the Mosaic-prophet promise: 'YHWH your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your midst, from your brothers'). The bread-from-heaven sign + the mountain-setting + the Passover-context all reinforce the new-Moses typology.

JHN 6:19.notes: Jesus walking on the sea (περιπατοῦντα ἐπὶ τῆς θαλάσσης) is OT-allusively a uniquely-divine prerogative. Job 9:8 LXX: 'ὁ τανύσας τὸν οὐρανὸν μόνος καὶ περιπατῶν ὡς ἐπ' ἐδάφους ἐπὶ θαλάσσης' (the one who alone stretched out heaven and walks as on the ground over the sea). Ps 77:19 (LXX 76:20): YHWH's path through the sea. Hab 3:15: YHWH 'trampled the sea with your horses.' The fifth Johannine sign is identity-declarative.

JHN 6:20.notes: Jesus' absolute Ἐγώ εἰμι (no predicate) on the storm-tossed sea evokes the divine 'I AM' formula: Exod 3:14 LXX (ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν), Isa 41:4 (κἀγὼ ἐγώ εἰμι), Isa 43:10 (ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι), Isa 51:12. The placement (post-storm, post-walking-on-water) makes the divine-allusion deliberate. First absolute I-AM in JHN; sets up the predicative I-AMs that follow (vv.35, 41, 48, 51 'I AM the bread of life').

JHN 6:31.notes: The crowd's ἐστιν γεγραμμένον quotation 'ἄρτον ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς φαγεῖν' is closest to Ps 78:24 LXX (Ps 77:24): 'ἄρτον οὐρανοῦ ἔδωκεν αὐτοῖς.' The full conflation also draws on Exod 16:4 ('ἐγὼ ὕω ὑμῖν ἄρτους ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ') and Exod 16:15. Pss 105:40 and Neh 9:15 also stand in the composite-tradition. Per Beale & Carson NT-Use-of-OT, this is the standard composite-citation form for the manna-tradition. Jesus' v.32 correction targets the implicit attribution to Moses.

JHN 6:35.notes: The 'never hunger / never thirst' double-promise (οὐ μὴ πεινάσῃ καὶ...οὐ μὴ διψήσει πώποτε) echoes Isa 49:10 (Servant-Song: 'they shall not hunger or thirst, neither scorching wind nor sun shall strike them') and Isa 55:1-3 (covenantal-banquet invitation: 'come, all who thirst, come to the waters; come, eat without money'). The first I-AM-with-predicate in John builds on these eschatological-banquet motifs.

JHN 6:4.notes: Passover-frame: John deliberately situates the bread-feeding + bread-of-life discourse in proximity to Passover (cf. JHN 2:13, 11:55) — preparing the reader to hear the v.51 claim ('the bread which I will give is my flesh...for the life of the world') in the Exod 12 paschal-lamb register. The substitutionary-atonement Christology of JHN 1:29 (Lamb of God) is structurally framed by the three Johannine Passovers.

JHN 6:41.notes: ἐγόγγυζον (imperfect of γογγύζω) deliberately echoes Israel's wilderness-grumbling against Moses and the manna provision. Exod 16:2 LXX uses γογγύζω: 'διεγόγγυζεν πᾶσα ἡ συναγωγὴ τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ ἐπὶ Μωυσῆν' (the whole congregation of the sons of Israel grumbled against Moses). The Jewish leaders' grumbling at the true bread replays the wilderness-rebellion against the original manna — a Johannine inverted-typology.

JHN 6:45.notes: Jesus' citation 'καὶ ἔσονται πάντες διδακτοὶ θεοῦ' aligns with Isa 54:13 LXX 'καὶ θήσω πάντας τοὺς υἱούς σου διδακτοὺς θεοῦ' (and I will set all your sons as taught-of-God). Jesus shortens by dropping 'sons,' generalizing to 'all.' The citation grounds the universal-Father-drawing of v.44 in OT prophecy. Cf. Jer 31:33-34 'all shall know me' — the new-covenant promise.

JHN 6:9.notes: Andrew's report of the boy's 'five barley loaves and two fish' echoes Elisha's miracle of multiplying twenty barley loaves to feed 100 (2 Kgs 4:42-44 — Elisha-feeding, with similar 'how can this feed so many?' incredulity from the servant, and the same barley + multiplication + leftovers pattern). John's signal: Jesus is the prophet-greater-than-Elisha; cf. v.14 'this is truly the Prophet who is to come.'

JHN 7:19.notes: Jesus indicts the leadership for plotting murder while claiming to keep Moses' law — implicit reference to the Sixth Commandment (Decalogue framing). The argument is qal va-chomer: those who claim Mosaic-fidelity are themselves breaking the most fundamental Mosaic-prohibition.

JHN 7:22.notes: Circumcision-rite institution. GEN 17:10-14 establishes the rite with Abraham (the πατέρες/Patriarchs reference); LEV 12:3 specifies the 8th-day timing. Jesus references 'from the patriarchs' (Genesis frame) rather than 'from Moses' (Leviticus frame), correctly noting the rite's pre-Mosaic origin.

JHN 7:24.notes: Right-judgment formula. Jesus' command 'do not judge by appearance, but judge with right judgment' echoes the OT-judicial-impartiality commands: LEV 19:15 ('do not show partiality... judge your neighbor in righteousness'); DEU 1:16-17 + 16:18-20 (judges to be impartial).

JHN 7:37.notes: Tabernacles water-pouring rite. Per Mishnah Sukkah 4.9, the water drawn from Siloam was carried in procession to the altar while ISA 12:3 ('with joy you will draw water from the wells of salvation') was sung. Jesus' invitation 'if anyone is thirsty, let him come to me and drink' lands at the climax of this rite. Wider echoes: ISA 55:1 ('come, all you who are thirsty, come to the waters'); EXO 17:6 + NUM 20:8-11 (water from rock at Massah/Meribah — wilderness-water-tradition).

JHN 7:38.notes: 'As Scripture said: rivers of living water will flow' — composite-thematic citation with no single OT verse matching verbatim. The cluster: EZK 47:1-12 (river from temple healing the Dead Sea); ZEC 14:8 (living waters from Jerusalem in messianic age); ISA 12:3 (wells of salvation — the Tabernacles rite citation); ISA 44:3 (Spirit poured on offspring); ISA 58:11 (like a watered garden); JOL 3:18 (fountain from house of YHWH watering valley of Shittim); PSA 78:16 + 105:41 (waters from rock). The whose-belly question (αὐτοῦ) — modern critical consensus reads it as the believer's; we follow that reading. v.39 makes plain: 'living water = Holy Spirit.'

JHN 7:40.notes: 'The Prophet' (definite ὁ προφήτης) refers to the eschatological Mosaic-prophet promised at DEU 18:15-18 ('The Lord your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your brothers — to him you shall listen'). This was a distinct messianic-figure expectation in Second Temple Judaism, sometimes identified with the Christ, sometimes seen as a separate eschatological figure.

JHN 7:42.notes: Davidic-Bethlehem messianic-prophecy cluster. The crowd cites the seed-of-David and Bethlehem-village criteria. SOURCES: MIC 5:2 (the Bethlehem-Ephrathah ruler-prophecy); 2SA 7:12-13 + PSA 89:3-4 + PSA 132:11 + JER 23:5 (Davidic-covenant seed-promise); 1SA 16:1 + 1SA 17:12 (David's Bethlehem origin). Johannine dramatic-irony: both criteria FIT Jesus (born in Bethlehem MAT 2:1 / LUK 2:4-7; Davidic-line MAT 1:6-16 / LUK 3:23-31), but the crowd lacks current biographical info.

JHN 7:51.notes: Nicodemus' due-process appeal invokes Mosaic judicial-procedure: DEU 1:16-17 (judge fairly between brothers); DEU 17:6 (capital case requires two-or-three witnesses); DEU 19:15 (single-witness insufficient); EXO 23:1 (no false report). The leadership is rushing-to-condemn without hearing Jesus directly — proto-mob-trial in violation of these procedures.

JHN 7:52.notes: Counterexample to the leadership's claim 'no prophet rises out of Galilee.' 2KI 14:25 — Jonah (the prophet sent to Nineveh) was from Gath-Hepher in tribal-Zebulun = Galilee. ISA 9:1-2 ('Galilee of the Gentiles ... the people walking in darkness have seen a great light') is THE prophecy of light-from-Galilee, fulfilled in Jesus' Galilean ministry (cf. MAT 4:13-16 explicit-citation). The leadership in their rush-to-judgment commits a Scripture-knowledge fail. Their own 'search-and-see' imperative ironically condemns them.

JHN 8:12.notes: ἐγώ εἰμι τὸ φῶς τοῦ κόσμου — composite allusion: (a) EXO 3:14 LXX 'ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν' divine-name echo; (b) EXO 13:21-22 wilderness pillar of fire (Sukkot context — see John 7-8); (c) ISA 9:2 'people walking in darkness have seen a great light'; (d) ISA 42:6 + 49:6 'a light to the nations' Servant Song theology. Cultural backdrop: post-Sukkot Jerusalem with the Court-of-Women menorahs still in mind.

JHN 8:17.notes: Jesus quotes the Mosaic two-witness rule. DEU 19:15 is the foundational text ('on the testimony of two or three witnesses every charge shall be established'); also DEU 17:6 (capital cases require 2+ witnesses), NUM 35:30 (homicide requires 2+ witnesses). Jesus invokes the rule to justify his self-testimony alongside the Father's — the two witnesses required by Mosaic law are met.

JHN 8:24.notes: FIRST ABSOLUTE ἐγώ εἰμι in JHN 8 (cluster with vv.28, 58). Composite allusion: (a) EXO 3:14 LXX 'ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν' YHWH self-name; (b) ISA 43:10 LXX 'ἵνα γνῶτε καὶ πιστεύσητε ... ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι' — the verbal closest match: 'know / believe / I am' triplet; (c) ISA 43:25, 45:18, 51:12 — Deutero-Isaiah's repeated 'I am he' divine self-declarations. Jesus claims for himself the response that Isaiah commands toward YHWH.

JHN 8:28.notes: SECOND ABSOLUTE ἐγώ εἰμι in JHN 8. ὑψόω 'lift up' is Johannine double-meaning: cross + glorification. Composite OT background: (a) NUM 21:8-9 the bronze serpent Moses lifted up — explicit cross-reference at JHN 3:14; (b) ISA 52:13 LXX 'ἰδοὺ συνήσει ὁ παῖς μου καὶ ὑψωθήσεται καὶ δοξασθήσεται σφόδρα' — Suffering Servant 'lifted up and glorified'; (c) EXO 3:14 ἐγώ εἰμι echo continued from v.24.

JHN 8:35.notes: Slave-vs-son house-membership contrast. GEN 21:10 Sarah's word: 'Cast out this slave woman with her son, for the son of the slave woman shall not inherit with my son Isaac.' Slaves can be sold/freed/displaced; sons retain permanent place. Pauline parallel: GAL 4:21-31 develops the same allegory.

JHN 8:41.notes: The Jewish leaders' counter-claim 'ἕνα πατέρα ἔχομεν τὸν θεόν / we have one Father, God' echoes the OT covenantal Father-language: ISA 63:16 'You, O LORD, are our Father, our Redeemer from of old is your name'; MAL 2:10 'Have we not all one Father? Has not one God created us?'; DEU 32:6 'Is he not your father, who created you?' The leaders invoke an authentic OT theme but use it as a self-justifying weapon — Jesus's reply (vv.42-44) shows that genuine God-paternity produces love-of-Christ, which the leaders lack.

JHN 8:44.notes: ἀνθρωποκτόνος ἀπ' ἀρχῆς + ψεύστης + πατὴρ τοῦ ψεύδους — composite allusion: (a) GEN 3 the serpent's deception leading to humanity's death; (b) GEN 4:8 Cain's murder of Abel as the first explicit human killing — 1JN 3:8, 12 makes the connection explicit; (c) WIS 2:24 LXX 'through the devil's envy death entered the world' — a 1st-century Jewish reading of GEN 3 widely received. The most theologically severe statement Jesus makes about his opponents in any of the gospels.

JHN 8:5.notes: The scribes/Pharisees cite the Mosaic adultery-death-penalty composite. LEV 20:10 prescribes death for both adulterer and adulteress without specifying method; DEU 22:22 reaffirms; DEU 22:23-24 specifies stoning for the betrothed-virgin offender and her partner. The trap selectively cites the stoning provision while procedurally skipping the both-parties-to-be-executed requirement.

JHN 8:51.notes: θάνατον οὐ μὴ θεωρήσῃ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα / 'shall never see death' — echoes PSA 89:48 'What man can live and not see death?' (a rhetorical lament about universal mortality). Jesus's 'never see death' is the Gospel inversion: the question Psalm 89 raises as a lament receives its answer in eschatological life through the Son. The Jewish leaders' v.52 retort ('Abraham died, the prophets died') misses that Jesus speaks of spiritual / eternal death (cf. REV 20:14).

JHN 8:56.notes: Abraham 'rejoiced to see my day'. Composite OT background: (a) GEN 17:17 Abraham 'laughed' (Hebrew root צחק underlies יצחק 'Isaac'); (b) GEN 22 the Aqedah / binding of Isaac (substitutionary-sacrifice typology); (c) GEN 22:14 'YHWH yireh' / 'YHWH will provide / see' — read messianically by some rabbinic traditions (e.g., b. Sanhedrin 89b). Patristic-period reading: Abraham saw the future Messiah-day in faith.

JHN 8:58.notes: THIRD AND CLIMACTIC ABSOLUTE ἐγώ εἰμι in JHN 8 (cluster with vv.24, 28). The verb-tense disjunction is the grammatical hammer: γενέσθαι (aorist infinitive 'to come into being') vs. εἰμί (present 'I am'). Echoes EXO 3:14 LXX 'ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ὤν' YHWH self-name and the Deutero-Isaiah 'I am he' cluster (43:10, 13, 25). The audience reaction in v.59 (immediate stoning attempt under LEV 24:16 blasphemy law) confirms they heard the divine-name claim — arguably the most explicit deity-claim in any of the gospels.

JHN 8:7.notes: Jesus's 'let him without sin cast the first stone' weaponizes DEU 17:7 'the hand of the witnesses shall be first against him to put him to death' — applies the law's witness-must-cast-first rule with surgical precision against the accusers' standing.

JHN 9:24.notes: δὸς δόξαν τῷ θεῷ — Hebrew juridical adjuration formula commanding a witness to confess truth as worship-of-God. JOSH 7:19 LXX: δὸς δὴ δόξαν σήμερον τῷ κυρίῳ θεῷ Ισραηλ καὶ δὸς τὴν ἐξομολόγησιν. Pharisees use the formula ironically — invoking truth-telling-as-worship while demanding the man recant his testimony.

JHN 9:35.notes: Jesus's signature self-designation 'Son of Man' (ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου) systematically echoes DAN 7:13 LXX 'ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἐρχόμενος' — the apocalyptic divine-human authority figure who receives dominion from the Ancient of Days. This Johannine instance, like the rest in John (1:51, 3:13-14, 5:27, 6:27, 6:53, 6:62, 8:28, 12:23, 12:34, 13:31), draws its weight from the Daniel 7 echo.

LUK 10:1.notes: The sending of the seventy-two (or seventy per MS variant) echoes Num 11:16-25 (Moses appointing 70 elders to share the Spirit and burden of leadership). Num 11:26-27 adds Eldad and Medad who prophesied in the camp outside the tent, possibly bringing the count to 72. If Luke reads 72 (SBLGNT/NA28), the number also matches LXX Gen 10's 72 nations-of-the-world (vs. MT's 70), framing the mission as anticipation of universal/Gentile mission. Either number carries typological significance — Jesus as the new Moses commissioning Israel-representing-and-extending envoys.

LUK 10:15.notes: Direct verbal echo of Isa 14:13-15 LXX (Babylon-taunt-song): εἰς τὸν οὐρανὸν ἀναβήσομαι... εἰς ᾅδου καταβιβασθήσῃ. Luke's καταβιβασθήσῃ matches the LXX verb exactly. Jesus applies the Babylon-taunt to Capernaum (his Galilean ministry base, Luke 4:31). Intertextual claim: Capernaum has placed itself in the position of Babylon's prideful king before imminent divine-descent to Sheol. Parallels Matt 11:23.

LUK 10:18.notes: Lightning-fall imagery of Satan evokes Isa 14:12 LXX (πῶς ἐξέπεσεν ἐκ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ὁ Ἑωσφόρος ὁ πρωὶ ἀνατέλλων — 'How has fallen from heaven the morning-star'), read from 2nd-century CE onward as Satan's primordial fall. Also connects to Jewish apocalyptic tradition (1 Enoch 86, 88; Rev 12:7-9). ἐθεώρουν imperf = Jesus sees Satan's cosmic-defeat actualized proleptically in the 72's exorcisms.

LUK 10:19.notes: Authority to tread on snakes-and-scorpions echoes Ps 91:13 LXX (Psalm 90:13 LXX): ἐπ᾽ ἀσπίδα καὶ βασιλίσκον ἐπιβήσῃ, καὶ καταπατήσεις λέοντα καὶ δράκοντα. Jesus extends the psalmist's promise-of-protection to his missionaries. Note the Ps 91 inclusio: Satan quoted Ps 91:11-12 in Luke 4:9-11 to tempt Jesus; Jesus now rightly applies the following verse (91:13) to his delegates' mission. Possible secondary-echo of Gen 3:15 (seed crushing serpent's head).

LUK 10:20.notes: 'Names written in heaven' echoes the OT book-of-life tradition: Exod 32:32 (Moses: 'blot me out of your book'); Ps 69:28; Dan 12:1 ('everyone whose name is found written in the book'); Mal 3:16 ('a book of remembrance was written before him'). The motif is elaborated in Rev 3:5, 13:8, 20:12-15, 21:27 (the Lamb's book-of-life); Phil 4:3. Jesus redirects the 72's joy from exorcistic-power to eschatological-security.

LUK 10:27.notes: COMPOSITE CITATION. The lawyer combines Deut 6:5 (Shema) and Lev 19:18 — the dual-summary of Torah also taught by Jesus himself at Mark 12:29-31 and Matt 22:37-40. Luke's form has FOUR elements (καρδία + ψυχή + ἰσχύς + διάνοια) for Deut 6:5, whereas MT Hebrew has three (לֵבָב נֶפֶשׁ מְאֹד). LXX manuscripts vary: some render MT מְאֹד as ἰσχύς, some as δύναμις; some add διάνοια as a fourth; some substitute διάνοια for καρδία. Luke's four-element form matches certain LXX traditions. Lev 19:18 ἀγαπήσεις τὸν πλησίον σου ὡς σεαυτόν rendered verbatim from LXX.

LUK 10:28.notes: FULFILLMENT/PRINCIPLE-ALLUSION. 'Do this and you will live' paraphrases Lev 18:5 LXX (καὶ ποιήσας αὐτὰ ἄνθρωπος ζήσεται ἐν αὐτοῖς). Paul cites the same verse at Rom 10:5 and Gal 3:12 as the principle of law-righteousness. Jesus agrees formally with the lawyer's Torah-answer; the Samaritan-parable then exposes whether the lawyer can actually DO it.

LUK 10:33.notes: The Samaritan-as-exemplary-neighbor enacts Lev 19:18 love-of-neighbor in a way that reverses first-century Jewish assumptions about who qualifies as 'neighbor.' Rabbinic debates variously limited πλησίον to fellow-Israelite; Jesus' parable redefines neighbor through action rather than ethnic or religious category-membership. The Samaritan (ethnically-religiously other) is the one who fulfills the commandment the priest and Levite (ethnically-religiously insider) fail to fulfill. See Luke 10:27 for the primary citation.

LUK 10:34.notes: The oil-and-wine-on-wounds care echoes Isa 1:6 LXX imagery of Israel's unbandaged wounds (ἀπὸ ποδῶν ἕως κεφαλῆς οὔτε τραῦμα οὔτε μώλωψ οὔτε πληγὴ φλεγμαίνουσα, οὐκ ἔστιν μάλαγμα ἐπιθεῖναι οὔτε ἔλαιον οὔτε καταδέσμους — 'from the feet to the head, there is no wound or bruise or inflamed blow; it is not possible to apply a plaster or oil or bandages'). The shared vocabulary (τραῦμα, ἔλαιον, καταδέω) is striking. Where Isa 1:6 describes Israel unbandaged, the Samaritan applies the very care Isaiah depicted as absent. Possible intertextual-gospel-message: the Samaritan models the compassion Israel's leaders failed to extend.

LUK 11:20.notes: 'Finger of God' (δάκτυλος θεοῦ) deliberately echoes Exod 8:19 LXX (Pharaoh's magicians: δάκτυλος θεοῦ ἐστιν τοῦτο, acknowledging the plagues) and Exod 31:18 / Deut 9:10 (the Ten Commandments written by God's finger). Luke's distinctive phrasing vs Matt 12:28's πνεύματι θεοῦ. The Exodus-allusion frames Jesus as the new-Moses bringing greater-exodus deliverance from demonic bondage. The kingdom-of-God 'has come upon you' (ἔφθασεν) = inaugurated-eschatology enacted through exorcism.

LUK 11:22.notes: The 'stronger-one overcoming the strong-man and dividing his spoils' echoes Isa 53:12 LXX (αὐτὸς ἁμαρτίας πολλῶν ἀνήνεγκεν... διὰ τοῦτο αὐτὸς κληρονομήσει πολλοὺς καὶ τῶν ἰσχυρῶν μεριεῖ σκῦλα — 'he will divide the spoils with the strong'). Divine-warrior imagery from Isa 49:24-25 ('will the captives of a warrior be taken? But thus says the LORD: even the captives of the warrior shall be taken') also resonates. Jesus applies divine-warrior-victor imagery to his own exorcism-ministry of liberating those held by Satan. Parallels Matt 12:29 / Mark 3:27.

LUK 11:29.notes: Introduces the 'sign of Jonah' motif developed in vv.30-32. OT referent = the book of Jonah as a whole. Jesus frames his generation's demand for a sign through the lens of Jonah's ministry — developed in v.30 as typological-equivalence. Parallels Matt 12:39, 16:4, Mark 8:12 (Mark truncates without interpretation).

LUK 11:30.notes: Luke's interpretation of the 'sign of Jonah' is Jonah's PREACHING to Nineveh (Jon 3) as typological-parallel to Jesus' preaching-to-this-generation. Distinct from Matt 12:40's three-days-in-fish typological-interpretation (which Luke does NOT include). Luke's reading suits his Gentile-mission theology: Jonah's word-to-pagans is paradigmatic-precedent for the Son-of-Man's word to his own Israel. Both Jonah-interpretations are authentic-synoptic-tradition preserved in parallel forms.

LUK 11:31.notes: Queen of Sheba's visit to Solomon (1 Kgs 10:1-13 / 2 Chr 9:1-12) — a paradigm-Gentile-seeker of divine-wisdom. Her eschatological-rising-to-condemn-this-generation damns-by-contrast: foreign-queen crossed-great-distance to hear Solomon's wisdom; Jesus' contemporaries reject the greater-wisdom speaking-to-them-directly. Parallels Matt 12:42. 'Ends of the earth' idiom echoes OT distance-formula (cf. Deut 28:49, Isa 5:26).

LUK 11:32.notes: Ninevites' repentance at Jonah's preaching (Jon 3) paradigm-Gentile-repenters. Eschatological-rising-to-condemn-this-generation by-contrast: pagan-Ninevites repented at the preaching-of-a-reluctant-prophet; Jesus' contemporaries reject the preaching of 'one-greater-than-Jonah.' Parallels Matt 12:41. Paired with v.31 as double-Gentile-testimony-against-Israel's-unresponsiveness.

LUK 11:40.notes: The ἄφρων ('fool') vocative echoes wisdom-literature condemnation of those-who-reject-God's-ways. Ps 14:1 LXX (13:1 LXX) εἶπεν ἄφρων ἐν καρδίᾳ αὐτοῦ· Οὐκ ἔστιν θεός ('the fool has said in his heart, there is no God') — same lexeme, same theological weight. Also Prov 1:7, 22-32 on fools rejecting wisdom. Jesus applies the wisdom-literature-condemnation of fools to the Pharisees — a sharp-rhetorical reversal since the Pharisees claimed wisdom-and-piety. The rhetorical-question 'did not the One who made the outside make the inside' echoes wisdom-creation-theology (Ps 139:13-16, Prov 20:12).

LUK 11:49.notes: 'I will send them prophets' is an OT prophetic-sending FORMULA attested across the Deuteronomistic-history and the prophetic corpus. 2 Chr 36:15-16 summarizes: 'the LORD... sent persistently to them by his messengers... but they kept mocking the messengers of God, despising his words, and scoffing at his prophets.' Cf. Jer 7:25 (ἀπέστειλα πρὸς ὑμᾶς πάντας τοὺς δούλους μου τοὺς προφήτας ἡμέρας καὶ ὄρθρου), Jer 25:4, 2 Kgs 17:13. Luke's 'wisdom of God said' presents this recurring-OT-formula as spoken-by-personified-divine-Wisdom. Matt 23:34 attributes the saying directly to Jesus without invoking 'wisdom.' Luke's addition 'and apostles' (ἀποστόλους) extends the Deuteronomistic-sending-formula forward to the New-Covenant apostolic-mission — a Luke-Acts distinctive framing.

LUK 11:50.notes: ἐκζητηθῇ τὸ αἷμα 'blood be sought-out/required' is technical-legal Hebraic language for divine-avenging of murder — Gen 9:5 LXX καὶ γὰρ τὸ ὑμέτερον αἷμα τῶν ψυχῶν ὑμῶν ἐκζητήσω ('for your blood of your lives I will require/seek-out'), the foundational post-Flood principle for human-bloodshed accountability. Extended to righteous-prophets' blood in 2 Kgs 9:7, Gen 42:22, Ps 9:12(11), Ezek 33:6. Jesus applies this foundational-principle to cumulative-blood-guilt of rejected-prophets falling on this-generation.

LUK 11:51.notes: FROM FIRST TO LAST MURDER IN THE JEWISH CANON. Abel = Gen 4:8-10 (first murder in Genesis, first book of Tanakh). Zechariah-ben-Jehoiada = 2 Chr 24:20-22 (killed in temple-court by King Joash's order — 'between altar and house' per 2 Chr 24:21). In the Jewish canonical-ordering, 2 Chronicles is the LAST book; so 'from Abel to Zechariah' = the entire biblical-history of murdered-innocents from first-to-last. This presupposes Jesus reading Scripture in the Tanakh-ordering. Matt 23:35 adds patronym 'son of Berechiah' (possibly conflating with prophet-Zechariah, Zech 1:1); Luke preserves the simpler attribution. ADDED in parallel with Matt 23:35.

LUK 12:14.notes: Jesus' response τίς με κατέστησεν κριτὴν ἢ μεριστὴν ἐφ' ὑμᾶς echoes Exod 2:14 LXX τίς σε κατέστησεν ἄρχοντα καὶ δικαστὴν ἐφ' ἡμῶν, where an Israelite rejected Moses as judge-and-ruler. Ironic-inversion: Moses was REJECTED as judge; Jesus REFUSES to be one. Shared lexical core κατέστησεν ... κριτήν ... ἐφ' makes the Moses-echo deliberate.

LUK 12:15.notes: οὐκ ἐν τῷ περισσεύειν τινὶ ἡ ζωὴ αὐτοῦ ἐστιν ἐκ τῶν ὑπαρχόντων echoes Eccl 5:10 (5:9 LXX) ἀγαπῶν ἀργύριον οὐ πλησθήσεται ἀργυρίου — wealth does not supply life or fulfillment. Sirach 31:1-11 extends this wisdom tradition on wealth's inadequacy for life-sustenance. The aphorism's form is sapiential-proverbial.

LUK 12:16.notes: The rich-fool parable resonates with OT wisdom-echoes on foolish-rich-man dying-and-leaving-wealth-to-unknown-heir. Eccl 2:18-21 (Qoheleth hates his toil because he must leave it to one who comes after him who-may-be-a-fool); Eccl 5:13-15 (hoarded riches harm the owner; he takes nothing with him at death); Prov 11:28 (ὁ πεποιθὼς ἐπὶ πλούτῳ αὐτοῦ οὗτος πεσεῖται — the one trusting in his riches will fall); Ps 49:16-20 LXX (do not fear when a man becomes rich, for he will take nothing when he dies). Lukan-distinctive parable assimilates this entire wisdom-tradition.

LUK 12:19.notes: φάγε πίε εὐφραίνου imperative-string evokes Eccl 8:15 LXX (τοῦ φαγεῖν καὶ τοῦ πιεῖν καὶ τοῦ εὐφρανθῆναι, Qoheleth's observation about life-under-sun) AND Isa 22:13 LXX (φάγωμεν καὶ πίωμεν αὔριον γὰρ ἀποθνῄσκομεν — which Paul cites at 1 Cor 15:32 as paradigm-of-hedonism-if-no-resurrection). The rich-fool's self-address assimilates the Qoheleth-formula without its הֶבֶל-qualification, collapsing into pagan-Epicurean carpe-diem-without-wisdom. Luke's v.20 God-speech then realizes the Isaiah-tomorrow-we-die threat literally.

LUK 12:20.notes: ἄφρων-vocative + διελογίζετο ἐν ἑαυτῷ (v.17) echoes Ps 14:1/53:1 LXX εἶπεν ἄφρων ἐν καρδίᾳ αὐτοῦ — the biblical fool speaks-to-himself-ignoring-God. The rich-farmer IS the Psalmic fool, and God's direct-address via ἄφρων names the diagnosis. Wis 15:8 provides a Second-Temple parallel for ἀπαιτέω (demand-back) of soul-as-entrusted-capital returned to Creator.

LUK 12:24.notes: Lukan-distinctive κόρακες (where Matt 6:26 has generic τὰ πετεινὰ τοῦ οὐρανοῦ). Ravens were unclean by Lev 11:15 / Deut 14:14. OT feeding-tradition: 1 Kgs 17:4-6 (ravens feed Elijah at Cherith); Job 38:41 LXX τίς δὲ ἡτοίμασεν κόρακι βοράν; Ps 147:9 LXX διδόντι τοῖς κτήνεσι τροφὴν αὐτῶν καὶ τοῖς νεοσσοῖς τῶν κοράκων. Luke's κόρακας picks up the Psalmic/Wisdom tradition of God-feeding-unclean-ravens as evidence of universal divine care. A-fortiori: if God feeds unclean birds, how much more his covenant-children.

LUK 12:27.notes: ἐν πάσῃ τῇ δόξῃ αὐτοῦ evokes 1 Kgs 10:4-5 LXX (Queen-of-Sheba witnessing τὴν δόξαν Σολομῶν); 2 Chr 9:3-4 parallel. Solomon as proverbial exemplar of royal-splendor. Natural-creation's simpler-beauty surpassing Solomon's glory implicitly honors the Creator; cf. Luke 11:31 where 'one-greater-than-Solomon is here' is Jesus himself.

LUK 12:32.notes: τὸ μικρὸν ποίμνιον invokes the OT shepherd-flock motif: Ezek 34 (God as Shepherd over false-shepherds, gathering scattered flock); Isa 40:11 LXX ὡς ποιμὴν ποιμανεῖ τὸ ποίμνιον αὐτοῦ (the coming-Lord as tender shepherd); Ps 100:3 LXX πρόβατα νομῆς αὐτοῦ. 'Little-flock' carries the covenant-flock weight while acknowledging the numerically-small Jesus-movement of c. 30 CE. Father's εὐδόκησεν (gnomic-aorist) frames the kingdom-gift as enduring-divine-pleasure.

LUK 12:35.notes: αἱ ὀσφύες περιεζωσμέναι verbatim echoes Exod 12:11 LXX καὶ οὕτως φάγεσθε αὐτό, αἱ ὀσφύες ὑμῶν περιεζωσμέναι — Passover-readiness for imminent-Exodus-departure. Jesus transposes the Exodus-readiness-motif onto disciples-awaiting-Master's-return. Participle-periphrastic and lexical-agreement make this a direct verbal-echo, not a distant allusion. 1 Pet 1:13 ἀναζωσάμενοι τὰς ὀσφύας τῆς διανοίας draws from the same nexus.

LUK 12:40.notes: ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἔρχεται combines Dan 7:13 Son-of-Man-coming (ἰδοὺ μετὰ τῶν νεφελῶν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἐρχόμενος) with the thief-unexpectedness-metaphor. Parousia-imminence-without-calendar draws on Danielic expectation of decisive-Son-of-Man-in-glory, reframed for ongoing-disciple-vigilance.

LUK 12:49.notes: πῦρ motif linking Jesus' ministry to OT fire-of-judgment. Mal 4:1 (3:19 MT) ἰδοὺ ἡμέρα ἔρχεται καιομένη ὡς κλίβανος — imagery picked up by John the Baptist at Luke 3:16-17. Isa 66:15-16 eschatological fire-judgment on flesh. Luke 3:16 already pairs fire with Holy-Spirit-baptism (πνεύματι ἁγίῳ καὶ πυρί), integrating Pentecost-flame-imagery Acts 2:3 into the Luke-Acts narrative theology. Here πῦρ primarily indicates division-crisis (v.51 διαμερισμόν) and possibly eschatological judgment, with the paradox that the fire-bringer must first endure baptism-of-suffering (v.50).

LUK 12:5.notes: τὴν Γέενναν < Hebrew גֵּי־הִנֹּם Valley-of-Hinnom, where Ahaz/Manasseh sacrificed children to Molech (2 Chr 28:3; 33:6); Josiah desecrated it (2 Kgs 23:10); Jeremiah prophesied it would become valley-of-slaughter (Jer 7:31-32; 19:5-7). By second-temple era a burning refuse-dump, whence the metonym for eschatological fiery-judgment. Isa 66:24 LXX πῦρ ... οὐ σβεσθήσεται undergirds the unquenchable-fire-imagery. Luke preserves the Greek transliteration γέεννα; our Thai renders as นรก for reader-comprehension with the Hinnom-etymology documented in the verse-summary.

LUK 12:53.notes: Explicit citation of Mic 7:6 LXX υἱὸς ἀτιμάζει πατέρα, θυγάτηρ ἐπαναστήσεται ἐπὶ τὴν μητέρα αὐτῆς, νύμφη ἐπὶ τὴν πενθεράν αὐτῆς. Luke EXPANDS Micah's asymmetric oppositions into SYMMETRIC bidirectional pairs (father-vs-son AND son-vs-father, mother-vs-daughter AND daughter-vs-mother, mother-in-law-vs-daughter-in-law AND daughter-in-law-vs-mother-in-law), heightening mutual alienation. Micah 7 diagnosed pre-exilic Israel's covenant breakdown; Jesus applies the same diagnostic to Messianic-era dividedness. Matt 10:35-36 preserves Micah's asymmetric pattern; Luke's symmetric form is distinctive.

LUK 12:56.notes: τὸν δὲ καιρὸν τοῦτον πῶς οὐκ οἴδατε δοκιμάζειν echoes the prophetic-critique of Israel for ignoring divinely-given signs. Isa 1:3 LXX ἔγνω βοῦς τὸν κτησάμενον καὶ ὄνος τὴν φάτνην τοῦ κυρίου αὐτοῦ, Ισραηλ δέ με οὐκ ἔγνω, καὶ ὁ λαός με οὐ συνῆκεν ('the ox knows its owner and the donkey its master's manger, but Israel does not know me, and my people do not understand'); Isa 6:9-10 hearing-without-understanding. Jesus applies the Isaianic diagnosis to the Pharisee-crowd who-can-read-weather-but-not-Messianic-signs.

LUK 12:8.notes: ὁ Υἱὸς τοῦ Ἀνθρώπου ὁμολογήσει ἐν αὐτῷ ἔμπροσθεν τῶν ἀγγέλων τοῦ Θεοῦ evokes Dan 7:13-14 LXX where the Son-of-Man enters the divine-court with myriads-of-attendants (Dan 7:10 θρόνοι ἐτέθησαν ... χίλιαι χιλιάδες ἐλειτούργουν αὐτῷ). Jesus switches from first-person to third-person Son-of-Man self-reference, maintaining the Danielic-title resonance while allowing ambiguity for outsiders. Court-room-witness frame (ἔμπροσθεν τῶν ἀγγέλων) mirrors Dan 7's heavenly-tribunal.

LUK 13:1.notes: Pilate mixing Galileans' blood with their sacrifices evokes the OT motif of innocent blood defiling holy space: 2 Kings 16:3 (Ahaz's Molech child-sacrifices), Ps 79:3 LXX (they poured out their blood like water around Jerusalem, and there was no one to bury them), Jer 2:34 (on your skirts is found the lifeblood of the guiltless poor), Ezek 22:3-4 (city that sheds blood defiles itself). Luke frames the atrocity in prophetic-judgment register: blood-guilt is the covenantal-catastrophe that precipitates judgment on the whole people — setting up Jesus's repent-or-perish call.

LUK 13:14.notes: The synagogue ruler's 'six days for work' (Ἓξ ἡμέραι εἰσὶν ἐν αἷς δεῖ ἐργάζεσθαι) echoes the Decalogue's fourth commandment: Exod 20:9 ἓξ ἡμέρας ἐργᾷ καὶ ποιήσεις πάντα τὰ ἔργα σου / Deut 5:13 ἓξ ἡμέρας ἐργᾷ. The citation is appealed to in order to criticize Jesus's Sabbath-healing; Jesus in response will reframe the Sabbath by appeal to Exodus-liberation-theology (Deut 5:15).

LUK 13:16.notes: Jesus's argument for Sabbath-healing-as-liberation rests on a cluster: Deut 5:15 (the Deuteronomic Sabbath grounded in Exodus-deliverance — the day of memory-of-slavery-ended); Isa 58:6 LXX λῦε πάντα σύνδεσμον ἀδικίας... ἀπόστελλε τεθραυσμένους ἐν ἀφέσει (the fast God chooses: to LOOSE the bonds of wickedness, to SET-FREE the oppressed — lexical-echo with ἔδησεν/λυθῆναι); Isa 61:1 LXX κηρύξαι αἰχμαλώτοις ἄφεσιν (proclaim release to captives — Luke 4:18 Nazareth-manifesto programmatic-text); Gen 17:7 (Abrahamic-covenant extends to descendants — 'daughter of Abraham' invokes covenant-inclusion). Jesus recasts Sabbath as the DAY OF RELEASE — fulfilling rather than violating the commandment.

LUK 13:19.notes: Mustard-seed-becomes-tree with birds-nesting-in-branches deliberately echoes the empire-as-cosmic-tree imagery: Dan 4:12, 21 LXX (Nebuchadnezzar's tree, birds of heaven dwelt in its branches, all flesh fed from it); Ezek 17:23 LXX (Messianic cedar under which every bird of every kind will dwell — direct background for the messianic-kingdom-as-tree reading); Ezek 31:6 (pharaoh-cedar with birds nesting in branches); Ps 104:12 LXX (birds of heaven dwell by waters and sing among the branches). The imperial-tree metaphor is co-opted for God's kingdom — subverting Daniel's Babylonian-tree reading.

LUK 13:21.notes: Three seahs of flour (ἀλεύρου σάτα τρία) matches the amount Sarah prepared for the three divine visitors at Mamre (Gen 18:6 LXX τρία μέτρα σεμιδάλεως). The hospitality-association suggests the hidden-leaven-transformation as new-Abrahamic-household-making: the kingdom silently transforms a community the way Sarah's baking fed the divine encounter. Hyperbolically large quantity (≈22 liters) matches the extravagance of Abraham's welcome.

LUK 13:25.notes: The householder's 'I do not know you' (οὐκ οἶδα ὑμᾶς) uses covenantal-knowing vocabulary: Amos 3:2 LXX ὑμᾶς ἔγνων ἐκ πασῶν φυλῶν τῆς γῆς (only you have I known from all families of the earth — covenantal-knowing = covenantal-recognition). Jer 24:5-10 (God 'knows' the good figs and rejects the bad). The formula is echoed in Matt 25:12 (ten-virgins parable) and Matt 7:23 (Lord-Lord pleaders). 'Do-not-know' is not memory-gap but covenantal-rejection.

LUK 13:27.notes: ἀπόστητε ἀπ’ ἐμοῦ, πάντες ἐργάται ἀδικίας directly cites LXX Ps 6:9 (ET 6:8) ἀπόστητε ἀπ’ ἐμοῦ, πάντες οἱ ἐργαζόμενοι τὴν ἀνομίαν. Luke's ἀδικίας replaces LXX ἀνομίαν (minor-lexical-variation, same semantic-field of covenantal-wickedness). Matthew's parallel (7:23) retains ἀνομίαν verbatim. Jesus-as-Judge assumes the Davidic-voice rejecting enemies in the psalm, a Christological move: the rejected-becomes-rejector, the psalmist-becomes-the-Lord-speaking-through-the-psalmist.

LUK 13:28.notes: Weeping and gnashing of teeth (κλαυθμὸς καὶ ὁ βρυγμὸς τῶν ὀδόντων) echoes Ps 112:10 LXX (ET 112:10) ὁ ἁμαρτωλὸς ὄψεται καὶ ὀργισθήσεται, τοὺς ὀδόντας αὐτοῦ βρύξει καὶ τακήσεται (the sinner will see and be angry, he will gnash his teeth and melt away) — same verb βρύχω + ὀδόντας. The feast-imagery of the Abrahamic-reunion in the kingdom draws from Isa 25:6-8 LXX (the Lord will make for all peoples on this mountain a feast of fat things). Matt 8:11-12 is near-parallel logion (at centurion-faith episode rather than Travel-Narrative).

LUK 13:29.notes: Four-directions gathering (ἀπὸ ἀνατολῶν καὶ δυσμῶν καὶ ἀπὸ βορρᾶ καὶ νότου) echoes Ps 107:3 LXX ἀπὸ ἀνατολῶν καὶ δυσμῶν, βορρᾶ καὶ θαλάσσης (Luke: νότου 'south' for LXX θαλάσσης 'sea' — a variant-pairing tradition).
