MRK 12:11.notes: Ps 117:23 LXX verbatim. LXX matches MT. Continues the Ps 118:22–23 citation begun in v.10 — the divine-reversal commentary on the stone-saying. αὕτη fem. refers to the whole stone-transformation event. Guillemet-pair ‹ › brackets the full v.10–11 unit.

MRK 12:19.notes: Sadducees' paraphrase of the levirate marriage law (Deut 25:5–6). Not verbatim LXX — a Sadducee-summary rather than a quotation. The rabbinic presupposition: Torah provides for death as genealogical-erasure via levirate kin, so resurrection-doctrine is unnecessary. Jesus responds not by disputing the Torah law but by showing it answers a different question than resurrection (v.25). Related echo: Gen 38:8 (Onan/Tamar).

MRK 12:26.notes: LXX Exod 3:6 verbatim (with Mark's minor omission of 'God of your father'). LXX matches MT. Jesus's scripture-argument for resurrection rests on the Torah's present-tense divine self-identification: God is — in the 'I AM' present — the God of the long-dead patriarchs, therefore they live. Canonical-strategy: Jesus cites Torah (Pentateuch) because the Sadducees rejected post-Pentateuchal canon (e.g., Dan 12:2). Meets them on their own Sola-Pentateuch terms.

MRK 12:29.notes: The Shema (Deut 6:4). LXX verbatim. Every observant 1st-century Jew recited this daily. Jesus's πρώτη-πάντων designation of this commandment as the greatest is not radical but conventional — what is distinctive is pairing it with Lev 19:18 (v.31) as the co-equal 'second.' Guillemets ‹ open at v.29, close at end of v.30 (spanning two Markan verses).

MRK 12:30.notes: Deut 6:5 continuation of the Shema. TEXTUAL CRUX: Mark has FOUR faculties (καρδία + ψυχή + διάνοια + ἰσχύς) where MT has THREE (lev/nefesh/me'od = heart/soul/might) and LXX has THREE (διάνοια OR καρδία, ψυχή, δύναμις). Mark uniquely adds both καρδία AND διάνοια separately plus substitutes ἰσχύος for LXX δυνάμεως. Matt 22:37 harmonizes to three (καρδία, ψυχή, διάνοια); Luke 10:27 has the same four as Mark but in different order. Each evangelist handles the citation slightly differently. Our translation preserves Mark's distinctive four-fold expansion.

MRK 12:31.notes: LXX Lev 19:18b verbatim. 'Love neighbor as yourself.' Jesus's innovation is not the pairing with Deut 6:5 (Hillel, Sir 7:30–31, Test.Dan 5:3 had similar double-love formulations) but the weighting — Lev 19:18 as co-equal 'second' at the top of halakhic hierarchy. Grounds all later Christian ethics (Rom 13:8–10, Gal 5:14, Jas 2:8).

MRK 12:32.notes: The scribe's restatement collapses OT monotheistic-confession tradition into one statement: Deut 4:35 ('there is none beside him'), Isa 45:5 ('I am the LORD, and there is no other'). Mark uses the scribe's sincerity to demonstrate that not all scribes were Jesus's opponents — a narrative corrective to the otherwise flat-opposition portrayal.

MRK 12:33.notes: The scribe's 'more than burnt offerings and sacrifices' directly echoes the prophetic tradition: Hos 6:6 (ἔλεος θέλω καὶ οὐ θυσίαν 'I desire mercy, not sacrifice'), 1 Sam 15:22 ('to obey is better than sacrifice'), Mic 6:6-8, Ps 40:6-8, Isa 1:11-17, Amos 5:21-24. In the temple courts, this statement is implicitly critical of the temple-system. The scribe has discerned something that prepares him for Jesus's response in v.34. Glossary: ὁλοκαύτωμα → เครื่องเผาบูชา, θυσία → เครื่องสัตวบูชา.

MRK 12:36.notes: Ps 109:1 LXX with a textual variant: SBLGNT (followed by our translation) has ὑποκάτω τῶν ποδῶν σου ('under your feet'), while LXX Ps 109:1 and some NT MSS have ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου ('as a footstool for your feet'). SBLGNT reading likely preserves Mark's original. The TWO different κύριος referents in the citation (YHWH speaking to Messiah) is the Christological-probing point: David calls the Messiah κύριος, implying the Messiah is David's superior despite also being his descendant. Ps 110:1 becomes the most-quoted OT verse in the NT (33 citations). Both κύριος-instances rendered องค์พระผู้เป็นเจ้า in Thai with disambiguation via the genitive ของข้าพเจ้า ('of me/my').

MRK 13:12.notes: Direct verbal echo of Mic 7:6 LXX ('υἱὸς ἀτιμάζει πατέρα, θυγάτηρ ἐπαναστήσεται ἐπὶ τὴν μητέρα... ἐχθροὶ ἀνδρὸς οἱ ἐν τῷ οἴκῳ αὐτοῦ'). Mark's ἐπαναστήσονται τέκνα ἐπὶ γονεῖς is lexically close. Second-Temple apocalyptic tradition already read Mic 7:6 eschatologically (m. Soṭah 9:15; 1QH; 4 Ezra 6:24). Markan theme: following Jesus divides families (3:31-35, 10:29-30); here the division darkens to family-betrayal-to-death.

MRK 13:14.notes: βδέλυγμα τῆς ἐρημώσεως is drawn from the Daniel triad: Dan 9:27 LXX ('βδέλυγμα τῶν ἐρημώσεων'), Dan 11:31 LXX ('βδέλυγμα ἠφανισμένων'), Dan 12:11 LXX ('βδέλυγμα ἐρημώσεως' — exact match). Original referent: Antiochus IV Epiphanes's 167 BCE temple desecration (altar to Zeus Olympios, swine sacrifices — 1 Macc 1:54-59; 2 Macc 6:2; Josephus Ant. 12.5.4). Mark's Jesus re-applies the Danielic pattern to a future desecration. Candidates: (a) Caligula's 40 CE statue attempt (stopped by assassination); (b) Zealot temple takeover 67 CE; (c) Titus's standards in the temple court 70 CE. The masculine participle ἑστηκότα favors a personal referent, consistent with (a) or (c). The parenthetical 'let the reader understand' is Mark's direct audience signal.

MRK 13:17.notes: Jesus's compassion-pause for pregnant and nursing women is pastoral-realism within cosmic prophecy. No direct OT citation; scholarly note-reference to Isa 7:14 in the general pregnancy-theme background. Luke 21:23 retains the verse almost verbatim.

MRK 13:19.notes: Lexically tracks Dan 12:1 LXX ('καὶ ἔσται καιρὸς θλίψεως, θλῖψις οἵα οὐ γέγονεν ἀφ' οὗ γεγένηται ἔθνος ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ἕως τοῦ καιροῦ ἐκείνου'). Mark substitutes ἀπ' ἀρχῆς κτίσεως ('from the beginning of creation') for Daniel's 'since a nation has existed,' cosmologically broadening. Daniel 12:1 context is the resurrection-announcement, so Mark's borrowing implicitly links the tribulation to eschatological deliverance. θλῖψις is the standard LXX apocalyptic term (Dan 12:1; Zeph 1:15 'ἡμέρα θλίψεως').

MRK 13:22.notes: Direct thematic echo of Deut 13:2-6 LXX on false prophets with σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα. The Torah principle — that miraculous capacity is NOT a sufficient criterion for prophetic authenticity — grounds Jesus's warning that the elect must test by doctrinal fidelity to the revealed Christ, not by sign-production.

MRK 13:23.notes: Jesus invokes the Deuteronomic test of prophetic reliability: Deut 18:22 — a true prophet's word comes to pass. Advance-warning creates an objective standard against which later-claimants' signs-and-wonders must measure. Transition verse: closes tribulation-section (vv.5-23), opens cosmic-Son-of-Man section (vv.24-27). The structural pivot 'I have told you beforehand' applies the Deuteronomic test to Jesus's own corpus.

MRK 13:24.notes: Composite pastiche of Day-of-YHWH imagery. Isa 13:10 LXX ('οἱ γὰρ ἀστέρες τοῦ οὐρανοῦ... τὸ φῶς οὐ δώσουσιν, καὶ σκοτισθήσεται τοῦ ἡλίου ἀνατέλλοντος, καὶ ἡ σελήνη οὐ δώσει τὸ φῶς αὐτῆς') — Mark's ἥλιος σκοτισθήσεται + σελήνη οὐ δώσει τὸ φέγγος αὐτῆς tracks this closely. Joel 2:10 LXX ('ὁ ἥλιος καὶ ἡ σελήνη συσκοτάσουσιν') confirms the stock apocalyptic vocabulary. Sun/moon/stars darkening is recurring OT cosmic imagery for YHWH's decisive intervention (not necessarily literal astrophysics); Jesus's audience hears 'the Day of the LORD has arrived.' Ezek 32:7 adds the Egypt-judgment parallel.

MRK 13:25.notes: Isa 34:4 LXX ('πάντες οἱ ἀστέρες πεσοῦνται... καὶ τακήσονται πᾶσαι αἱ δυνάμεις τῶν οὐρανῶν') closely matches Mark's falling-stars + shaken-powers language. Hag 2:6 LXX ('σείσω τὸν οὐρανὸν καὶ τὴν γῆν') supplies the heaven-and-earth-shaken theme (also cited in Heb 12:26). Cosmic upheaval as theophanic signage.

MRK 13:26.notes: Near-citation of Dan 7:13-14 LXX ('ὡς υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου ἐρχόμενος ἦν καὶ... ἐδόθη αὐτῷ ἐξουσία καὶ τιμὴ βασιλική'). Mark substitutes μετὰ δυνάμεως καὶ δόξης for Daniel's ἐξουσία καὶ τιμή — thematically-identical coronation vocabulary. Mark's most explicit self-identification with Daniel-7's Son of Man up to this point (repeated at 14:62). Directionality is deliberately ambiguous — parousia-to-earth OR ascension-to-throne (Dan 7 originally reads the latter, Acts 1's ascension fits the pattern). ἐν νεφέλαις recurs Markanly (9:7 transfiguration-cloud; here parousia-cloud) — the theophanic medium of YHWH (Exod 13:21, 19:9, 33:9; Num 11:25).

MRK 13:27.notes: Composite gathering-of-elect imagery. Zech 2:6 LXX (ἐκ τῶν τεσσάρων ἀνέμων τοῦ οὐρανοῦ συνάξω ὑμᾶς — post-exilic gathering); Deut 30:4 LXX (ἀπ' ἄκρου τοῦ οὐρανοῦ — covenant restoration); Isa 11:12 LXX (συνάξει τοὺς διασπαρέντας — eschatological gathering); Isa 27:13 (trumpet-gathering). Jesus applies the OT exile-gathering pattern to the Son of Man's parousia-gathering of the worldwide church. Elect are no longer just Jewish exiles but globally chosen (cf. v.10). Matt 24:31 reproduces almost verbatim; Luke 21:28 departs.

MRK 13:31.notes: Jesus claims-for-his-own-words the permanence Isa 40:8 attributes to the word of YHWH ('τὸ δὲ ῥῆμα τοῦ θεοῦ ἡμῶν μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα'). Cf. also Isa 51:6 ('heaven and earth shall pass away, but my salvation shall be forever'). A high-Christology claim preserved by Mark: Jesus's spoken words on par with YHWH's eternal word. Wordplay: γενεὰ αὕτη will not παρέλθῃ (v.30) balanced by οἱ λόγοι μου οὐ μὴ παρελεύσονται (v.31).

MRK 13:6.notes: ἐγώ εἰμι ambiguity preserved intentionally. Mark has used ἐγώ εἰμι for Jesus in divine-self-revelation moments: 6:50 (walking-on-water theophany, Exod 3:14 echo) and 14:62 (Sanhedrin trial). Here at 13:6, false-Christs appropriating precisely this phrase is Mark's signal that they claim the highest identification, not merely 'I am the Messiah.' Mark's Christology is understated but pointed: false ἐγώ εἰμι-claimants confirm Jesus's ἐγώ εἰμι claims are indeed ultimate. First-century messianic pretenders (Theudas, Judas the Galilean) exemplify this.

MRK 13:8.notes: ἔθνος ἐπ' ἔθνος + βασιλεία ἐπὶ βασιλείαν echoes Isa 19:2 LXX and 2 Chr 15:6 LXX (Hanani's oracle on inter-nation conflict as divine judgment). ὠδίν ('birth-pang') is the standard OT-prophetic image for eschatological travail (Isa 13:8, 26:17, 66:7-9; Jer 4:31, 6:24; Mic 4:9-10; Hos 13:13). In Second-Temple Judaism this becomes the technical phrase 'chevlei mashiach' (1 Enoch 62:4; 4 Ezra 4:42; 2 Baruch 29:3ff; 1QH 3:7-12) — a fixed tribulation preceding the Messiah's advent. Mark's Jesus uses this exact register, locating the present crisis as ἀρχή (beginning) of that birth-process.

MRK 13:9.notes: Synagogue floggings (ἐν συναγωγαῖς δαρήσεσθε) invoke Deut 25:2-3's 39-lash limit (m. Makk. 3:10). Jesus's prediction schematically maps the persecutions later documented in Acts (Peter/John before Sanhedrin 4-5; Paul beaten in synagogues, 2 Cor 11:24; Paul before Felix/Festus, Acts 24-26; Paul before Agrippa II, Acts 26; Paul before Caesar, Acts 27-28). Three concentric circles of persecution: Jewish-local (συνέδρια, συναγωγαί) → Roman-provincial (ἡγεμόνες) → Roman-imperial (βασιλεῖς).

MRK 14:18.notes: Jesus's 'the one eating with me' directly echoes Ps 41:10 MT / 40:10 LXX — the psalm of the righteous sufferer betrayed by a close friend. Early Christianity read Ps 41 Messianically (John 13:18 cites Ps 41:9/10 explicitly for Judas). Mark's Jesus ALLUDES rather than CITES, letting the echo do the interpretive work. Also foreshadows Ps 22 (cited at 15:24, 15:34) — Mark's Passion is saturated with the Lament Psalms' vocabulary. Meal-betrayal horror: Near-Eastern covenant-of-salt (Num 18:19) and common-table fellowship created a near-inviolable bond; betrayal at the table magnified the moral weight (Qumran 1QS 6.1-5 on table-fellowship discipline).

MRK 14:21.notes: The 'better not to have been born' formula is the strongest OT-style curse: echoes Jer 20:14 (Jeremiah's self-curse), Job 3:3, Sir 23:14. Applied to Judas it signals ultimate moral catastrophe. Mark holds in tension divine-necessity (Son-of-Man goes 'as written') and human-responsibility (woe to the betrayer) — same tension as Acts 2:23. Fourth occurrence of παραδίδωμι in vv.10-21. Jesus does not name Judas; the woe is spoken in his hearing, but Mark narrates no turning-point for him.

MRK 14:24.notes: Densest single-sentence OT-theological compression in Mark. (1) τὸ αἷμα τῆς διαθήκης = verbatim echo of Exod 24:8 LXX (Moses's Sinai covenant-ratification with sacrificial blood). (2) Jer 31:31's promised 'new covenant' stands behind the claim — 'new' implicit (vs. Luke/1 Cor which make it explicit). (3) ὑπὲρ πολλῶν echoes Isa 53:12 'for the sins of the many' — Jesus the Suffering Servant. Textual decision: SBLGNT's shorter τῆς διαθήκης (without καινῆς) is retained as the harder reading with stronger manuscript support and arguably richer theology (forcing Exod 24 allusion to primary status).

MRK 14:27.notes: Zech 13:7 LXX form (πατάξω 1sg future) where MT has imperative 2ms. The LXX reading makes God the direct-striker — sharpening the divine-agency of the crucifixion (cf. Isa 53:10 'it pleased the LORD to crush him'). Original Zechariah context: God strikes the shepherd, sheep scatter, but scattering leads to remnant-purification (Zech 13:8-9). Mark applies: Jesus = smitten shepherd; disciples = scattered sheep; implicit Galilean remnant-regathering (v.28). Formally introduced with γέγραπται.

MRK 14:34.notes: Jesus's complaint-of-soul uses Ps 42:5/11 LXX (41:6/12 LXX numbering) language verbatim: περίλυπος... ψυχή μου. The Psalmist's 'why are you cast down, O my soul?' becomes Jesus's own lament — the Son enters Israel's lament-piety completely. Mark's Passion is saturated with Lament Psalms (Ps 22 at 15:34; Ps 41:10 at 14:18; Ps 42 here; Ps 69 at 15:23, 36). Early church read this as the foundation of Christological-Psalter reading. γρηγορεῖτε command here structurally connects to the closing word of the Olivet Discourse (13:37).

MRK 14:41.notes: 'Son of Man handed over into the hands of...' matches Dan 7:25 LXX 'the saints of the Most High will be given into his hand' (the persecuting beast). Mark inverts apocalyptic-typology: the Son of Man suffers what Daniel's saints suffer — bearing their destiny in himself. Also echoes Mark's own 9:31 second passion-prediction; the prediction now actualizes.

MRK 14:49.notes: SCRIPTURE-FULFILLMENT CLAIM without specific identification. Jesus invokes 'that the Scriptures might be fulfilled' (ἵνα πληρωθῶσιν αἱ γραφαί) as a programmatic self-interpretation, Mark's densest concentration of divine-necessity language (δεῖ + καθὼς γέγραπται + ἵνα πληρωθῶσιν). The broad referent-field is the Passion-predicting OT complex: Servant Songs (Isa 52:13-53:12), Lament Psalms (Ps 22, 41, 69), Zech 13:7 (just cited v.27), and the prophetic-suffering tradition. Mark intentionally does NOT specify which text — the totality of Scripture's testimony to the Messiah's suffering is what is being fulfilled.

MRK 14:50.notes: Zech 13:7 FULFILLMENT of 14:27's citation. 'I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered' — Mark's literary precision pairs prophecy and fulfillment within five verses. πάντες includes Peter (who reappears at v.54 'following at a distance'). Also sets up v.51's mysterious young-man figure.

MRK 14:52.notes: Amos 2:16 MT/LXX: 'even the bravest warrior will flee away naked in that day.' The young-man's naked-flight may enact Amos's judgment-oracle as fulfilled in the disciples' collapse. Literary irony: the same or similar young-man figure reappears at the resurrection (16:5) in a white garment (στολὴ λευκή) — potential Markan reversal pattern (naked-flight at Passion → clothed-glory at Resurrection).

MRK 14:58.notes: TEMPLE-SAYING DISTORTION. False witnesses mangle Jesus's historical temple-saying (preserved accurately at John 2:19, Acts 6:14): (a) changed subject (Jesus as destroyer not raiser), (b) verb substitution (οἰκοδομήσω 'will-build' for original ἐγερῶ 'will-raise'), (c) added χειροποίητος/ἀχειροποίητος pair — LXX idol-vocabulary (Isa 46:6 etc.; Lev 26:1) suggesting Jesus calls the temple an idol. The entire accusation becomes Jesus-threatening-temple + implicitly-calling-it-idolatry + claiming-to-build-rival-sanctuary. Witnesses disagree (v.59) — court cannot establish even the fabricated charge.

MRK 14:61.notes: Jesus's silence before accusers performatively enacts Isa 53:7 (the Suffering Servant 'like a lamb... silent before her shearers'). Mark presents the Servant via narrative-silence rather than verbal citation. Along with Isa 52:13-53:12, Isa 50 is Mark's silent-servant soundtrack. The high priest's question fuses two Messianic titles — ὁ χριστός (political-messianic) + ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ εὐλογητοῦ (divine-sonship echoing Ps 2:7, 2 Sam 7) — which in first-century Judaism could be separate concepts; Caiaphas bundles them.

MRK 14:62.notes: Mark's Christological climax. Jesus affirms full-title Messianic + Davidic + divine-sonship + Danielic-Son-of-Man + Ps-110-enthroned-Lord in two sentences. Composite citation: Ps 110:1 (ἐκ δεξιῶν... τῆς δυνάμεως, LXX 109:1) + Dan 7:13 (ἐρχόμενον μετὰ τῶν νεφελῶν τοῦ οὐρανοῦ). Same dual-citation appears at 12:36 (Ps 110:1) + 13:26 (Dan 7:13 alluded) + 14:62 (both fused). Three passages form a Markan Christological triad. Directional ambiguity preserved: (a) Second Coming to earth (traditional); (b) Son of Man's ascension to the Ancient of Days (Dan 7 original direction, matching Acts 1 ascension-cloud). Caiaphas reads it as blasphemy (14:63-64) — claim to share God's throne.

MRK 14:65.notes: Isa 50:6: 'I gave my back to those who strike, and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard; I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting.' Mark's scene is the Servant Song enacted. Prophetic irony: they mock Jesus for not-prophesying, while he has just prophesied (v.62) his coming-in-power — and the prophecy they demand he could fulfill but chooses not to.

MRK 14:7.notes: Jesus does not DISMISS poor-care but INVOKES the verse that establishes Israel's ongoing poor-care obligation. Deut 15:11's full context: 'because there will never cease to be poor in the land, THEREFORE open your hand wide to your brother.' Jesus cites the premise of unending poor-care to justify the UNIQUE moment of this anointing. This saying becomes the scriptural warrant for Christian poor-care-as-continuing-discipline (Acts 4:32-35; 2 Cor 8-9) — precisely because poor-care is NORMAL, extraordinary worship-acts are not in competition with it.

MRK 15:19.notes: Roman mock-adoration reprises Isa 50:6 spitting-and-blows on the Servant; also Isa 53:3 (despised and rejected). Three parody-gestures: (1) strike head with reed-scepter (turning scepter into weapon), (2) spit (replacing anointing), (3) kneel (mock-adoration). Each is an antonym of the real royal-ceremony it parodies.

MRK 15:23.notes: Wine-with-myrrh (σμυρνισμένος, hapax) offered as pre-crucifixion anesthetic. Myrrh's double Life-of-Jesus appearance (Magi's gift at birth → burial preparation) is Christian-typological. Matt 27:34 substitutes 'gall' (χολή), harmonizing to Ps 69:21 LXX (ἔδωκαν εἰς τὸ βρῶμά μου χολήν). Mark preserves σμυρνίζω and leaves the Ps 69:21 echo implicit. Jesus's REFUSAL has been variously interpreted: (1) full consciousness for atoning suffering, (2) fulfilling Gethsemane's 'I will not drink again of the fruit of the vine until...' (14:25), (3) rejection of the mocking gesture.

MRK 15:24.notes: Near-verbatim quote of Ps 22:19 LXX (Ps 21:19 LXX numbering): διαμερίζονται τὰ ἱμάτια ≈ διεμερίσαντο τὰ ἱμάτιά μου; βάλλοντες κλῆρον ≈ ἔβαλον κλῆρον. Mark doesn't tag it with 'it was written,' following his MARKAN CITATION-STRATEGY of echo rather than formal-citation (unlike Matthew's frequent 'in order to fulfill'). Ps 22 is the crucifixion-psalm — its first line is Jesus's cry at v.34 and its motifs permeate Mark 15: mockery by passers-by (v.29 ↔ Ps 22:7), 'save yourself' taunt (v.30 ↔ Ps 22:8), garments divided (v.24 ↔ Ps 22:18), abandonment cry (v.34 ↔ Ps 22:1). The whole psalm is the hermeneutical key.

MRK 15:29.notes: Head-wagging mockery verbatim-verbal parallel to Ps 22:7/22:8 LXX (κινοῦντες κεφαλὴν). Lam 2:15 adds head-wagging over Zion imagery. Mark's readers hear the Psalm-of-the-Righteous-Sufferer being enacted by the mockers themselves. The mocking allusion to Jesus's alleged temple-saying (14:58) brings the formal charge into the crucifixion scene. HAPAX: οὐά.

MRK 15:30.notes: Ps 22:8 'he trusts in YHWH, let him deliver him, let him rescue him.' Mark's verbal echo is less direct than Matt 27:43 (which quotes verbatim) but the mockery-structure is identical. Recapitulates the wilderness-temptation pattern ('if you are the Son of God, prove it') in human mouths — Mark's lack of a wilderness-temptation-dialogue scene is supplied by this crucifixion-mockery as climactic temptation.

MRK 15:33.notes: The 3-hour supernatural midday darkness fulfils OT judgment-theophany motifs: Amos 8:9 ('I will make the sun go down at noon, and darken the earth in broad daylight' — Day-of-YHWH imagery); Exod 10:21-23 (ninth plague — 3 days of darkness before firstborn-death); Deut 28:29 (curse on disobedience). Natural-phenomenon explanations (eclipse, storm) are impossible at Passover (full moon; 3-hour duration). Mark presents it as supernatural.

MRK 15:34.notes: Most-quoted OT verse in the Gospels. Hebrew אֵלִי אֵלִי לָמָה עֲזַבְתָּנִי; Aramaic Targum equivalent approximates Mark's Greek transliteration Ἐλωΐ ἐλωΐ λεμὰ σαβαχθάνι. Mark's ἐγκατέλιπές aligns with the LXX verb elsewhere (direct LXX Ps 21:1 has προσέσχες μοι in the question form — textual complexity noted by scholars). THE CRUX: Is Jesus expressing genuine divine-abandonment or praying Ps 22 as confidence-expression? Both-and: the first line of Ps 22 is a real cry of desolation AND Jewish first-century hermeneutic identifies a psalm by its first line (quoting v.1 evokes the whole Psalm, which ends in vindication and universal worship — Ps 22:27-31). Genuine forsaken-ness + confident trust in the full arc held together.

MRK 15:35.notes: Phonetic near-miss: Ἐλωΐ (Aramaic 'my God') → Ἠλίας (Elijah). Bystanders either mishear or deliberately distort. Jewish expectation of Elijah's eschatological return (Mal 4:5-6) as Messiah-herald (cf. Mark 9:11-13) makes Elijah a natural wordplay-target. Mark 9:11-13 had already identified John the Baptist as the Elijah-who-has-come — the irony here is that the crowd still LOOKS for Elijah while Messiah-himself dies before them.

MRK 15:36.notes: Ps 69:22 LXX (Ps 68:22 LXX numbering): ἔδωκαν εἰς τὸ βρῶμά μου χολὴν καὶ εἰς τὴν δίψαν μου ἐπότισάν με ὄξος ('they gave me gall for my food, and for my thirst vinegar to drink'). Mark's ὄξος is the verbatim LXX word. The drink here is DIFFERENT from v.23 (wine-with-myrrh, refused). Scholars debate mercy vs mockery; John 19:28-30 frames it as fulfillment of Ps 69:21 tied to Jesus's 'I thirst.' Mark's Thai preserves the ambiguity.

MRK 15:38.notes: Temple curtain (καταπέτασμα, Exod 26:31-33 construction). Three major interpretive strands (all likely intended): (1) access-now-open — way to God's presence open for all (Heb 10:19-20); (2) judgment on the temple — its cultic role over, 40 years before physical destruction (70 AD); (3) revelatory sign — heavens torn to show who Jesus is. MARKAN INCLUSIO: σχίζω ('torn') binds 1:10 (heavens torn at baptism) with 15:38 (curtain torn at cross) — same verb, same function (revelation of the Son). Possible secondary echo of Isa 25:7 (God swallowing up the veil spread over nations).

MRK 15:40.notes: Women watching ἀπὸ μακρόθεν ('from afar') echoes Ps 38:11 LXX ('οἱ ἔγγιστά μου ἀπὸ μακρόθεν ἔστησαν' — 'my nearest stand afar off'), the Psalm of the abandoned sufferer. Mark's spatial note encodes the Psalm's abandonment-motif: the few who remain can only watch from distance. These women form Mark's GOSPEL-WITNESS CHAIN (crucifixion 15:40, burial 15:47, empty tomb 16:1-8) — the only humans present at all three pivotal events, contrasting with the male disciples' flight.

MRK 15:43.notes: Isa 53:9 'they made his grave with the wicked and with the rich in his death.' Joseph of Arimathea, a Sanhedrin member who provides a rich-man's rock-tomb, fulfils this detail of the Servant Song. Mark's spare presentation is theologically weighted: a religious-establishment insider breaks ranks to bury Jesus. Matthew adds 'rich' (27:57), Luke adds 'righteous' and 'had not consented' (23:50-51), John calls him a secret disciple (19:38) — Mark's minimalism lets the Isaiah fulfilment carry the interpretive weight.

MRK 15:45.notes: Pilate's release of the body to Joseph of Arimathea is part of the Isa 53:9 fulfillment sequence ('with a rich man in his death'). Roman practice normally denied crucifixion-victims proper burial (left on cross or mass grave as deterrent); Pilate's favor is unusual. πτῶμα (literally 'fallen-thing,' standard Greek for corpse) marks dehumanized state; Mark's Thai translation preserves human dignity via royal-register พระศพ — a theological choice.

MRK 15:46.notes: RICH-MAN-TOMB fulfills Isa 53:9 LXX ('δώσω τοὺς πονηροὺς ἀντὶ τῆς ταφῆς αὐτοῦ καὶ τοὺς πλουσίους ἀντὶ τοῦ θανάτου αὐτοῦ'). Mark's bare narration (buy linen → take down → wrap → place in tomb → roll stone → close up, 6 actions) lets the fulfillment-logic speak without editorial comment. σινδών glossary reaffirmed (established at 14:51); μνημεῖον → อุโมงค์ glossary established. Hapax: ἐνειλέω.

MRK 15:5.notes: Jesus's silence before Pilate enacts Isa 53:7 LXX ('ὡς πρόβατον ἐπὶ σφαγὴν ἤχθη καὶ ὡς ἀμνὸς ἐναντίον τοῦ κείροντος αὐτὸν ἄφωνος οὕτως οὐκ ἀνοίγει τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ'). Mark does not quote but performs the Servant-Song shape (cf. 14:61 silent before Caiaphas). Pilate's θαυμάζειν parallels and contrasts with the centurion's confession at v.39 — one marvels and misses who Jesus is; the other marvels and sees.

MRK 16:19.notes: ἐκάθισεν ἐκ δεξιῶν τοῦ θεοῦ echoes Ps 109:1 LXX κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου. Completes the Markan Ps-110 arc: 12:36 (Jesus's scribal-puzzle), 14:62 (Jesus's self-identification at trial), 16:19 (longer-ending composer's enthronement-narrative). The longer-ending composer provides the Christological-closure that Mark's primary body-text prepares but does not narrate. Part of the disputed longer-ending (vv.9-20); textual-status caveat applies but the OT-allusion is clear regardless.

MRK 1:11.notes: Baptism voice combines Royal-Son (Ps 2), Servant-beloved (Isa 42), and beloved-sacrificial-son (Gen 22).

MRK 1:2.notes: Mark attributes to Isaiah but quotes Malachi+Exodus. The 'ἄγγελόν μου' phrase matches LXX Mal 3:1 closely.

MRK 1:3.notes: Voice-in-wilderness text matches LXX ordering ('in the wilderness') rather than MT ('call in the wilderness prepare the way').

MRK 2:26.notes: David eating the Bread of the Presence (1 Sam 21:1-6). HISTORICAL CRUX: Mark's ἐπὶ Ἀβιαθάρ ἀρχιερέως — 1 Sam 21 records Ahimelech, not Abiathar, as priest at Nob; Abiathar (his son) succeeded him (1 Sam 22:20-23). Solutions in literature: (1) Mark/Jesus conflates by memory; (2) ἐπί + gen. = 'in the Scripture section named for Abiathar' (paralleling Mark 12:26's ἐπὶ τοῦ βάτου 'in the burning-bush passage'); (3) both shared priesthood during transition; (4) a variant reading. SBLGNT retains Ἀβιαθάρ as the harder reading. Jesus uses the David-precedent to defend disciples' sabbath plucking — human need supersedes ritual boundary.

MRK 3:13.notes: Jesus ascending the mountain to call the Twelve echoes Moses ascending Sinai to establish the covenant people (Exod 24:1-11). Mark's 'he went up the mountain and called whom he wanted' carries Moses-typology — the new people-of-God being constituted on a mountain by the Son.

MRK 4:12.notes: Mark's quotation diverges from LXX Isa 6:9–10 in a crucial way: LXX ends with ἰάσομαι αὐτούς ('I will heal them'); Mark has ἀφεθῇ αὐτοῖς ('it might be forgiven them'). This aligns with Targum Jonathan on Isa 6:10, suggesting Mark draws on an Aramaic paraphrase tradition rather than pure LXX. The substitution 'forgive' for 'heal' theologically reads physical restoration as spiritual forgiveness. Famous 'parables harden' crux (matt 13:13, Luke 8:10 soften; Mark preserves the sharpness).

MRK 4:29.notes: Mark 4:29 'εὐθὺς ἀποστέλλει τὸ δρέπανον, ὅτι παρέστηκεν ὁ θερισμός' directly echoes Joel 3:13 LXX (4:13 LXX numbering: ἐξαποστείλατε δρέπανα, ὅτι παρέστηκεν τρυγητός). Isa 17:5 provides secondary resonance. The harvest-sickle is standard OT imagery for eschatological divine judgment (cf. Rev 14:14-16). The parable's force: between sowing and reaping is hidden divine growth, which crashes into visibility at harvest.

MRK 4:3.notes: Jesus's opening 'Listen!' (Ἀκούετε) echoes the Shema's opening imperative (Deut 6:4 'Hear, O Israel'). The Parable of the Sower is structured as Jesus's own Shema — a call to covenant-listening that becomes the criterion for understanding all his parables (v.13).

MRK 4:32.notes: Mustard seed's birds-in-shade imagery alludes to OT cosmic-tree texts where birds-of-heaven dwelling in a great tree's branches = nations finding refuge in an empire (Ezek 17:23 Davidic cedar; Ezek 31:6 Assyrian cedar; Dan 4:12 Nebuchadnezzar's tree). Jesus subversively downgrades the cosmic cedar to a humble garden bush — but the refuge-for-nations sense is preserved.

MRK 4:41.notes: The disciples' 'Who is this?' invokes OT sea-subduing theophany: Ps 89:8-9 ('O LORD God of hosts, who is like you? You still the raging of the sea'); Ps 107:29 ('he stilled the storm to a whisper'); Job 38:8-11 (YHWH sets bounds to the sea). For first-century Jews, only YHWH commands the sea. Mark places this Christological question at the climax of ch.4 to force the same confession the centurion will voice at 15:39 ('Truly this was the Son of God'). THEME CLOSURE: ch.4 opens with 'Listen!' (v.3) and closes with 'the wind and sea OBEY' (ὑπακούει = 'hear-under') — creation hears what the disciples struggle to.

MRK 6:23.notes: Herod's 'up to half my kingdom' oath echoes Est 5:3, 5:6, 7:2 (Ahasuerus to Esther). Mark's ironic reversal: in Esther the formula saves Jewish lives; here it costs a prophet's. The escalation v.22 casual offer → v.23 sworn oath traps Herod in front of his court.

MRK 6:34.notes: Jesus's compassion at 'sheep without a shepherd' (ὡς πρόβατα μὴ ἔχοντα ποιμένα) tracks Num 27:17 LXX (ὡσεὶ πρόβατα οἷς οὐκ ἔστιν ποιμήν). Secondary allusions: 1 Kgs 22:17; Ezek 34:5; Zech 10:2. Num 27 is Joshua's commissioning as new-shepherd of Israel — Jesus as new-Joshua leading the wilderness-people-who-need-a-shepherd. σπλαγχνίζομαι (compassion) is Mark's signature verb for Jesus's shepherd-instinct. The chapter opened with rejection in his hometown; it ends with overflowing compassion for strangers.

MRK 6:48.notes: Walking-on-water + 'wished to pass them by' is layered OT theophany-language. (1) Job 9:8 LXX — only YHWH treads on the sea. (2) Exod 33:19, 22 — God 'passes by' Moses on the rock; Mark's παρελθεῖν echoes LXX παρελεύσομαι. (3) 1 Kgs 19:11 LXX — God 'passes by' (παρελεύσεται) Elijah at Horeb. Jesus's walking-on-water is a deliberate theophanic enactment: doing what YHWH does in OT theophanies, using OT theophany-vocabulary. The disciples nearly recognize the supernatural quality but misinterpret it as apparition (v.49). Fourth-watch timing (3-6 AM) after ~9 hours of rowing against the wind places the miracle at maximum exhaustion.

MRK 6:50.notes: ἐγώ εἰμι: Mark's most theologically charged self-disclosure. Echoes Exod 3:14 LXX (God's name to Moses) and Isa 41:4 / 43:10 / 43:25 / 51:12 / 52:6 (YHWH's self-identification 'I am he' / 'I am'). Combined with v.48's theophanic 'pass by' and walking-on-water (Job 9:8), Mark constructs a sustained YHWH-self-revelation tableau. The disciples should hear: this is GOD revealing himself in the storm. Their non-recognition (v.52 hearts hardened) is the tragedy. CHRISTOLOGICAL CRUX: Mark 6:50 is load-bearing for the high Christology — Jesus does what only YHWH does, says what only YHWH says. Thai 'เราเอง' dual-functions as natural self-identifier AND emphatic 'I AM.'

MRK 6:52.notes: πωρόω/πώρωσις (heart-hardness) carries Exodus-vocabulary — originally Pharaoh-language (Exod 7:3; 8:15; 9:12). Mark applies it to the Twelve, their Pharaoh-level resistance to repeated revelation. Paul picks up the same word-family for unbelieving Israel (Rom 11:25). Mark's theological point: the gospel doesn't assume the disciples' unique virtue — they too need the hardening broken open by repeated revelation.

MRK 7:10.notes: Two OT citations paired: (1) the 5th commandment to honor parents (Exod 20:12 / Deut 5:16); (2) the death penalty for parental cursing (Exod 21:17 / Lev 20:9). Both quotations align with LXX. Jesus pairs them deliberately to show that the Mosaic obligation toward parents is non-negotiable — a positive command and a negative sanction together establishing unchallengeable weight. The Corban-loophole in vv.11–12 violates BOTH the command and undermines the sanction.

MRK 7:32.notes: The rare word μογιλάλος ('hardly able to speak') used to describe the brought-to-Jesus man echoes LXX Isa 35:6 directly: 'τρανὴ ἔσται γλῶσσα μογιλάλων' ('the tongue of the dumb shall sing for joy'). Combined with κωφός (deaf, Isa 35:5 LXX 'the ears of the deaf'), Mark deliberately marks this healing as fulfillment of Isa 35's Messianic-restoration promises. μογιλάλος is rare in all of Greek literature — Mark's choice is not casual. Sets up v.37.

MRK 7:35.notes: Isa 35:5-6 Messianic-age healing-promise: 'the eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf shall be unstopped... the tongue of the dumb (μογιλάλων) shall sing for joy.' Mark's vocabulary tracks Isaiah's promised restoration precisely (μογιλάλος is a LXX hapax shared with Isa 35:6). The instantaneous reversal — ears opened, tongue loosed, plain speech — enacts the Messianic age. Double passive (ἠνοίγησαν, ἐλύθη) is divine-passive: agency is Jesus's-command-as-God's-power.

MRK 7:37.notes: Crowd's confession 'πάντα καλῶς πεποίηκεν' (he has done all things well) echoes Gen 1:31 LXX 'εἶδεν ὁ θεὸς τὰ πάντα ὅσα ἐποίησεν, καὶ ἰδοὺ καλὰ λίαν' — attributing creational-good language to Jesus' deeds. The specifics 'makes the deaf hear and the mute speak' echo Isa 35:5–6 LXX Messianic-restoration signs (ἀκούσονται κωφοί, γλῶσσα μογιλάλων / ἀλάλων). Together: Mark frames Jesus' Gentile-territory ministry as both new-creational (Gen 1) and Messianic-restorative (Isa 35). The Gentile crowd is unwittingly orthodox — confessing what the religious establishment of vv.1–13 refused to see.

MRK 7:6.notes: Jesus quotes Isa 29:13 LXX closely. The MT differs ('their fear of me is a commandment of men learned by rote'); the LXX phrasing 'in vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men' is what Jesus' argument depends on. One of the clearer cases in Mark where the citation specifically tracks LXX rather than MT — confirms Mark's audience reads Greek scripture. Full citation runs vv.6b–7.

MRK 7:7.notes: Continuation of Isa 29:13 LXX citation begun in v.6. Note: Mark's word order (διδασκαλίας ἐντάλματα ἀνθρώπων) differs slightly from standard LXX (ἐντάλματα ἀνθρώπων καὶ διδασκαλίας) — either a textual variant of LXX known to Mark or Mark's free citation. Theological force unchanged.

MRK 8:18.notes: Echo of Jer 5:21 LXX (ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτοῖς καὶ οὐ βλέπουσιν, ὦτα αὐτοῖς καὶ οὐκ ἀκούουσιν) and Ezek 12:2 LXX (similar phrasing). Both prophetic indictments of apostate Israel; Mark reapplies them to the Twelve. Also resonates with Isa 6:9–10 (already cited at Mark 4:12). The intertextual jolt is theologically devastating: the disciples function as Jesus' 'outsiders.' Inner-quotes used in Thai output to mark the OT echo.

MRK 8:31.notes: μετὰ τρεῖς ἡμέρας ἀναστῆναι ('after three days rise') possible echo of Hos 6:2 LXX μετὰ δύο ἡμέρας ... ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ ἀναστησόμεθα ('after two days...on the third day we shall rise'). Mark's 'after three days' is the harder reading; Matt/Luke harmonize to 'on the third day' (more directly matching Hos 6:2's day-three). ἀποδοκιμασθῆναι in same verse echoes Ps 118:22 LXX (cited later at Mark 12:10).

MRK 8:37.notes: Echo of Ps 49:7–8 (LXX 48:8–9): 'no man can ransom his brother, or give to God a price for his life ... for the ransom of their soul is costly.' Wisdom-tradition allusion shaping Jesus' rhetorical impossibility. Not direct citation.

MRK 8:38.notes: Son-of-Man-coming-in-glory-with-holy-ones combines Dan 7:13–14 LXX (eschatological Son of Man receiving dominion) with Zech 14:5 LXX (YHWH 'comes with all the holy ones'). The 'holy ones' tradition shifts from heavenly-host (Zech) to angels (Mark) — both Jewish-apocalyptic categories blending. First explicit 'Father' (πατήρ) reference Jesus makes of himself in Mark — opens the Son-Father christological depth that unfolds in Mark 9 (Transfiguration) and 14:36 (Gethsemane Abba).

MRK 9:11.notes: Disciples' question invokes the scribal doctrine derived from Mal 4:5-6 (Mal 3:23-24 Heb.): Elijah must come before the Messiah. Having just seen Elijah on the mountain (v.4), they ask whether the prophecy is fulfilled in what they just witnessed, or whether there is a future-Elijah. Jesus's reply (vv.12-13) identifies John the Baptist as the fulfillment (cf. Mark 1:6 — John's dress explicitly echoes Elijah's 2 Kgs 1:8 LXX).

MRK 9:12.notes: Jesus's question about the Son of Man being 'treated with contempt' (ἐξουδενηθῇ, hapax in Mark) echoes the Suffering-Servant rejection language of Isa 53:3 LXX and Ps 22:6 LXX (related form ἐξουθενέω). The Suffering-Servant and Suffering-Son-of-Man strands are here fused, preparing the Passion narrative. Jesus's reply holds the Elijah-prophecy AND the suffering-Son-of-Man in tension — both are 'written,' so there is no dilemma, only a sequence.

MRK 9:19.notes: γενεὰ ἄπιστος inherits Deuteronomic-exodus charge of Israel's wilderness-disobedience: Deut 32:5 (Song of Moses: 'crooked and perverse generation' γενεὰ σκολιὰ καὶ διεστραμμένη); also Deut 32:20; Ps 95:10 LXX (Heb 3:10). Jesus's exasperation encompasses disciples, crowd, scribes, father — the 'generation' of the present age fails to trust.

MRK 9:2.notes: Six-day delay echoes Exod 24:15-16 (Moses on Sinai: six days of waiting, then YHWH's voice on the seventh). Jesus takes the inner-three — Peter, James, John — the same three who will sleep in Gethsemane (14:33). The Transfiguration narratively confirms 8:38 ('the Son of Man will come in the glory of his Father'): here that glory is momentarily unveiled. The Moses-typology runs throughout (mountain, cloud, voice, glory).

MRK 9:3.notes: Two hapax in a single verse (στίλβω 'shine-radiantly', γναφεύς 'launderer') render the heavenly-whiteness superlatively. Parallels: Dan 7:9 LXX (Ancient of Days: τὸ ἔνδυμα αὐτοῦ ὡσεὶ χιὼν λευκόν 'his clothing white as snow') and Dan 10:6 (man-clothed-in-linen with body στίλβοντος as beryl). Mark's diction evokes apocalyptic theophany — Jesus visually identified with the Dan 7 divine-figure.

MRK 9:31.notes: Second passion prediction. 'Third day' resurrection timing echoes Hos 6:2 LXX (ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ ἀναστησόμεθα 'on the third day we shall rise'). Structural counterpart to 8:31 and 10:33-34. παραδίδοται (present tense 'is being handed over') more imminent than 8:31's δεῖ παθεῖν. Son-of-Man/hands-of-men wordplay (υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου → χεῖρας ἀνθρώπων) preserved in Thai 'บุตรมนุษย์/มือของมนุษย์.'

MRK 9:48.notes: Direct citation of Isa 66:24 LXX: ὁ σκώληξ αὐτῶν οὐ τελευτήσει, καὶ τὸ πῦρ αὐτῶν οὐ σβεσθήσεται ('their worm will not die, and their fire will not be quenched'). Mark's wording matches the LXX so precisely that it functions as a citation, though not formally-introduced. Isa 66:24 is the culmination of Isaiah's prophecy — the eschatological judgment where the wicked are consumed perpetually. σκώληξ is a Markan hapax. THEOLOGICALLY: worm + fire is metaphoric imagery for continuous judgment, not necessarily literal.

MRK 9:49.notes: TEXTUAL VARIANT: SBLGNT reads the shorter πᾶς γὰρ πυρὶ ἁλισθήσεται ('every one will be salted with fire'). Many Byz witnesses add 'καὶ πᾶσα θυσία ἁλὶ ἁλισθήσεται' ('and every sacrifice will be salted with salt' — Lev 2:13 echo). The shorter SBLGNT reading is almost certainly original (א B L W Δ f¹); the longer is a scribal harmonization. Lev 2:13 (salt-of-the-covenant in sacrifice) is the conceptual background regardless of which reading is preserved. vv.43-48's Gehenna-fire warnings are summarized by v.49's universal-salting — fire of trial preserves by testing (cf. 1 Pet 1:7; 4:12-13).

MRK 9:7.notes: The divine voice combines: Deut 18:15 LXX (αὐτοῦ ἀκούσεσθε 'you shall listen to him' — the prophet-like-Moses); Ps 2:7 (Davidic-royal 'you are my son'); Isa 42:1 (servant-beloved ὁ ἐκλεκτός μου). Command 'hear him' (ἀκούετε αὐτοῦ) is the transfiguration climax: Jesus supersedes Law-and-Prophets (embodied by Moses and Elijah present) as THE one to be heard. The cloud (Shekhinah) evokes Exod 24 + Exod 40:34 + Num 7:89 theophany-sequence. Not 'one of three' (as Peter proposed in v.5) but the unique Son.

2TH 2:4.notes: ὑπεραιρόμενος ἐπὶ πάντα λεγόμενον θεὸν echoes LXX Dan 11:36 'ὑψωθήσεται καὶ μεγαλυνθήσεται ἐπὶ πάντα θεόν' ('he will be exalted and magnified above every god'). Antiochus IV Epiphanes typology applied to the future antichrist. The temple-sitting-self-deification combines Dan 11:36 (self-exaltation) with Dan 9:27 / 11:31 / 12:11 (abomination of desolation in the holy place — also cited in Jesus' Olivet discourse, Mark 13:14).

2TH 2:8.notes: ὁ κύριος Ἰησοῦς ἀνελεῖ τῷ πνεύματι τοῦ στόματος αὐτοῦ direct echo of LXX Isa 11:4: '[ὁ Χριστός] πατάξει γῆν τῷ λόγῳ τοῦ στόματος αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐν πνεύματι διὰ χειλέων ἀνελεῖ ἀσεβῆ' ('[the Messiah] will strike the earth with the word of his mouth and with the breath through his lips will slay the wicked'). Paul applies the Messianic prophecy explicitly to Christ's destruction of the antichrist. The verbatim ἀνελεῖ + πνεύματι + στόματος matches LXX Isaiah's language, although Paul shortens χειλέων to στόματος.

2TH 3:10.notes: Echo of Gen 3:19 ('In the sweat of your face you shall eat bread, until you return to the ground'). The curse-of-the-fall established work-as-the-means-of-bread; Paul's maxim 'if anyone is unwilling to work, neither let him eat' applies the Genesis-ordinance to the Christian community. Thematic-not-verbal allusion.

2TH 3:16.notes: ὁ κύριος μετὰ πάντων ὑμῶν echoes the OT 'Yahweh-with-you / God-with-us' formula. Isa 7:14 (Immanuel = 'God with us'); Josh 1:5 ('I will be with you'); Judg 6:12 ('the Lord is with you'). Pauline blessing-form draws on this OT divine-presence-tradition. Thematic-not-verbal allusion.

1CO 1:19.notes: Close paraphrase of LXX Isa 29:14 (ἀπολῶ τὴν σοφίαν τῶν σοφῶν καὶ τὴν σύνεσιν τῶν συνετῶν κρύψω). Paul retains the first verb ἀπολῶ but substitutes ἀθετήσω for κρύψω, sharpening from concealment to active nullification. Original Isaianic context: indictment of Judah's leaders trusting Egyptian alliance over YHWH; God will frustrate the wise advisors. Paul applies the same divine action to the Greco-Roman wise.

1CO 1:31.notes: Heavily condensed citation of Jer 9:23-24 LXX (= 9:22-23 LXX numbering). Paul reduces a four-clause oracle ('let not the wise boast in his wisdom, nor the strong in his strength, nor the rich in his riches; but let him who boasts boast in this — that he understands and knows me') to its closing imperative-of-permitted-boasting, replacing 'understands and knows me' with 'in the Lord' (ἐν κυρίῳ). The three forbidden boasts in Jeremiah (wisdom/strength/riches) align with Paul's three not-many in v.26 (wise/powerful/noble-born). KYRIOS-ambiguity (YHWH in Jeremiah / Christ in Paul's typical usage) deliberately preserved. Cf. 2 Cor 10:17 for the same kernel.

1CO 1:20.notes: Loose echo of LXX Isa 33:18 (ποῦ εἰσιν οἱ γραμματικοί; ποῦ εἰσιν οἱ συμβουλεύοντες; ποῦ ἐστιν ὁ ἀριθμῶν τοὺς τρεφομένους...). Paul's triple ποῦ-question ('Where is the wise? Where is the scribe? Where is the disputer of this age?') reproduces the rhetorical pattern of LXX Isa 33:18 with a substituted-roster: γραμματικός / συμβουλεύων / ἀριθμῶν → σοφός / γραμματεύς / συζητητής. Original Isaianic context: after Sennacherib's army is destroyed, Judah looks for the foreign scribe-counselors-tributors who terrorized them and finds them gone. Paul applies the same vanishing-of-the-elite to the eschatological action of God in the cross-event.

1CO 2:9.notes: Composite citation, primary source Isa 64:4 LXX (ἀπὸ τοῦ αἰῶνος οὐκ ἠκούσαμεν οὐδὲ οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ ἡμῶν εἶδον θεὸν πλὴν σοῦ καὶ τὰ ἔργα σου ἃ ποιήσεις τοῖς ὑπομένουσιν ἔλεον). Paul rearranges and adds a third clause 'nor has it entered the heart of man' (ἐπὶ καρδίαν ἀνθρώπου οὐκ ἀνέβη), a Septuagintal idiom not in Isa 64:4 itself but found at LXX Jer 3:16 and Jer 39:35 (= MT 32:35). Possible additional influence from Isa 65:17 LXX. Origen attributed the citation to a non-canonical 'Apocalypse of Elijah' but the surface text reads as a free recombination of Isaianic motifs by Paul. The 'those who love him' (τοῖς ἀγαπῶσιν αὐτόν) replaces the LXX's 'those who wait for mercy' — Paul's relational framing.

1CO 2:16.notes: Close paraphrase of LXX Isa 40:13 (τίς ἔγνω νοῦν κυρίου, καὶ τίς αὐτοῦ σύμβουλος ἐγένετο, ὃς συμβιβᾷ αὐτόν;). Paul condenses to two clauses, retaining ἔγνω νοῦν κυρίου and ὃς συμβιβάσει αὐτόν. KYRIOS-ambiguity: in Isa 40 κύριος = YHWH (incomparability oracle); Paul's immediately-following 'but we have the mind of Christ' (νοῦν Χριστοῦ ἔχομεν) implicitly identifies Christ with the κύριος of the Isaiah text. Cf. Rom 11:34 for the same citation in a different argument. The LXX νοῦς renders Hebrew רוּחַ (spirit) — Paul's downstream νοῦν Χριστοῦ picks up the same lexeme.

1CO 3:19.notes: Loose paraphrase of Job 5:13 LXX (ὁ καταλαμβάνων σοφοὺς ἐν τῇ φρονήσει). Paul changes both verb (καταλαμβάνω 'overtake' → δράσσομαι 'grasp/seize') and noun (φρόνησις 'prudence' → πανουργία 'craftiness'), sharpening the negative connotation. Original Joban context: Eliphaz's first speech (Job 5) — God frustrates the schemes of the cunning. Paul applies the principle to first-century 'wise' (sophists, philosophers, faction-rhetors). HAPAX in NT: δράσσομαι (only at this verse).

1CO 3:20.notes: Close paraphrase of LXX Ps 94:11 (= 93:11 LXX): κύριος γινώσκει τοὺς διαλογισμοὺς τῶν ἀνθρώπων ὅτι εἰσὶν μάταιοι. Paul substitutes τῶν σοφῶν ('of the wise') for ἀνθρώπων ('of men'), narrowing the original anthropic application to the specific topic of wise-men's thoughts. Original psalm context: indictment of those who oppress widow/orphan/sojourner (Ps 94:6) — God knows their futile reasoning. Paul's word-substitution is itself an interpretive move: those whom the world calls wise are precisely those whose reasoning God knows to be futile.

1CO 5:13.notes: Direct citation of Deuteronomic community-purgation formula. LXX Deut 17:7 closing the death-penalty-stoning instruction for idolatrous Israelites: ἐξαρεῖς τὸν πονηρὸν ἐξ ὑμῶν αὐτῶν. The same formula recurs at Deut 19:19 (false witness), 21:21 (rebellious son), 22:21 (adultery), 22:24 (sexual sin), 24:7 (kidnapping). Paul changes the verb from second-singular future-indicative ἐξαρεῖς ('you-singular shall remove') to second-plural aorist imperative ἐξάρατε ('you-plural expel') — adapting the formula to communal discipline. The transformation is structural: Israel's death-penalty for the egregious sinner becomes the church's expulsion-from-fellowship. The principle of community-purgation is preserved; the means are eschatologically transposed (death → social-expulsion + handover-to-Satan, with redemptive purpose per v.5).

1CO 6:16.notes: Direct citation of Genesis 2:24 LXX (καὶ ἔσονται οἱ δύο εἰς σάρκα μίαν). Paul retains the future-tense LXX form word-for-word for the operative clause. Original Genesis context: divine institution of marriage at creation. Paul applies the marriage-text to prostitute-union, reading the 'one flesh' principle as covering ANY sexual union — not just marital — and using it as proof that sexual immorality forms a real ontological-bond. The same verse Jesus cites in Matt 19:5 / Mark 10:8 (corpus precedents). Paul's interpretive move extends the principle from marriage-establishment to all sexual union. The LXX-future ἔσονται is preserved exactly.

1CO 9:9.notes: Direct citation of Deut 25:4 LXX (οὐ φιμώσεις βοῦν ἀλοῶντα). SBLGNT reads κημώσεις ('muzzle' from κημός 'muzzle') vs LXX's φιμώσεις ('muzzle/silence' from φιμόω). The two verbs are synonyms; some manuscripts of 1 Cor have φιμώσεις (harmonization with LXX). Either way, it is the same OT command. Original Mosaic context: humane treatment of working animals during threshing — the ox treads grain on a threshing-floor and must be allowed to eat as it works. Paul applies typologically to gospel-workers: God's deeper concern is human-laborer rights, including ministerial support. Same OT verse cited at 1 Tim 5:18 alongside Luke 10:7. HAPAX in NT: κημόω (only at this verse).

1CO 10:7.notes: Direct citation of Exodus 32:6 LXX (καὶ ἐκάθισεν ὁ λαὸς φαγεῖν καὶ πιεῖν, καὶ ἀνέστησαν παίζειν). The verse is the climactic moment of the golden-calf incident: after offering sacrifices to the calf, the people sat down to feast and rose up to παίζειν (play, revel, dance — likely with sexual connotations given the context of Aaron's calf-cult and subsequent judgment). Paul cites verbatim. The HAPAX παίζω in NT (only at this verse) is from this OT-citation. Paul's typological use: idol-feast participation → revelry → judgment, the same pattern threatening the Corinthian idol-feast attendees.

1CO 10:26.notes: Direct citation of Psalm 24:1 LXX (= Ps 23:1 LXX numbering): τοῦ κυρίου ἡ γῆ καὶ τὸ πλήρωμα αὐτῆς. Paul reverses the word order slightly (LXX: τοῦ κυρίου ἡ γῆ; Paul: τοῦ κυρίου γὰρ ἡ γῆ — adds γάρ). Original psalm context: opens a creation-and-temple-entry psalm; the divine-ownership-of-the-earth grounds the Lord's throne-rights. Paul's appropriation: divine-ownership of all food grounds Christian-permission to eat anything from the marketplace. The food's prior pagan-sacrifice does not transfer ownership from God to demon.

1CO 10:5.notes: Paraphrastic allusion to the wilderness-judgment narrative: of the Israelites who left Egypt, only Joshua and Caleb of the adult-male generation entered the Promised Land (Num 14:23, 26:65). Paul's 'God was not pleased with most of them; they were strewn-down in the wilderness' summarizes this trajectory. HAPAX καταστρώννυμι in NT echoes the strewn-bodies imagery but is not a direct LXX-citation.

1CO 10:6.notes: Echo of Numbers 11:4 LXX (ἐπεθύμησαν ἐπιθυμίαν 'they craved a craving') — the Israelite craving for meat in the wilderness. Paul's ἐπιθυμητὰς κακῶν ('cravers of evil things') / ἐπεθύμησαν ('they craved') picks up the Numbers vocabulary. HAPAX ἐπιθυμητής in NT.

1CO 10:8.notes: Allusion to the Baal-Peor incident (Num 25): Israelites' sexual immorality with Moabite women + idolatrous worship of Baal-Peor; plague killed 24,000 (Num 25:9 LXX). Paul says 23,000 — most likely 'in one day' (μιᾷ ἡμέρᾳ) specifying within the immediate-day, while Numbers' 24,000 may include the totality including those who died after.

1CO 10:9.notes: Allusion to Numbers 21:5-6: Israelites complained against Moses about the manna; God sent fiery serpents (LXX τοὺς ὄφεις τοὺς θανατοῦντας) which bit and killed many. Paul's 'they tested Christ... and were destroyed by serpents' identifies the OT-judgment-agent with Christ-pre-incarnate (consistent with v.4's 'rock was Christ').

1CO 10:10.notes: Allusion to the post-Korah-rebellion plague (Num 16:41-49 LXX 17:6-15): Israelites grumbled against Moses and Aaron for the deaths of Korah's company; God sent a plague that killed 14,700 until Aaron interposed with incense. The 'destroyer' (ὀλοθρευτής, NT HAPAX) draws on the broader Septuagintal-tradition of a divine destroying-agent (cf. Ex 12:23 LXX ὀλεθρεύων at the Passover-night).

1CO 10:22.notes: Echo of Song of Moses (Deut 32:21 LXX): αὐτοὶ παρεζήλωσάν με ἐπ’ οὐ θεῷ — 'they provoked me to jealousy with what is no god.' Paul's παραζηλοῦμεν τὸν κύριον ('do we provoke the Lord to jealousy?') draws directly on the Mosaic-jealousy idiom. Original context: Israel's idolatry in Deut 32 brings divine-jealousy and impending judgment — the same pattern Paul fears for the Corinthians.

1CO 10:20.notes: Echo of LXX Deut 32:17 (ἔθυσαν δαιμονίοις καὶ οὐ θεῷ — 'they sacrificed to demons and not to God'). Paul's δαιμονίοις καὶ οὐ θεῷ θύουσιν is essentially a verbatim restatement, slightly reordered. Original Mosaic-Song context: Israel's idolatry exposed as demonic-worship masked as divine-worship. Paul applies to the Greco-Roman pagan-cult world: behind the metaphysically-non-existent idols stand real demonic powers receiving the worship.

1CO 10:21.notes: Echo of Malachi 1:7, 12 LXX which uses τράπεζα κυρίου ('table of the Lord') for the altar of YHWH. Paul's 'cup of the Lord / table of the Lord' draws on this OT-altar-vocabulary, applying it to the Christian Lord's-Supper-fellowship. The contrast with 'cup/table of demons' is Paul's own innovation: only one comparable Greco-Roman expression (Mal's altar-table-of-YHWH) made the demonic-table-counterpart visible by analogical contrast.

1CO 13:12.notes: Faint echo of LXX Num 12:8 (στόμα κατὰ στόμα λαλήσω αὐτῷ ἐν εἴδει καὶ οὐ δι' αἰνιγμάτων — 'mouth to mouth I will speak with him, in form, and not through riddles'). The Mosaic-precedent for face-to-face divine encounter (Moses uniquely 'spoke with God face-to-face' Ex 33:11). Paul's αἴνιγμα (NT HAPAX) directly recalls the LXX-Numbers term for indistinct/riddled speech. The eschatological 'face-to-face' fulfills universally-for-believers what was unique-to-Moses in the OT.

1CO 14:21.notes: Paraphrastic citation of Isaiah 28:11-12 LXX (διὰ φαυλισμὸν χειλέων διὰ γλώσσης ἑτέρας ὅτι λαλήσουσιν τῷ λαῷ τούτῳ...καὶ οὐκ ἠθέλησαν ἀκούειν). Paul rearranges and abbreviates substantially: keeps the 'other-tongues + foreign lips speaking to this people' core but adds 'and even-then they will not hear me, says the Lord' — a free-paraphrase rather than verbatim citation. Original Isaianic context: God's threat to speak-judgment to Israel through Assyrian-invaders' incomprehensible speech; Israel will refuse to listen even-then. Paul's application: tongues-as-judgment-sign in 1 Cor 14:22. HAPAX in NT: ἑτερόγλωσσος (only at this verse). Note: Paul introduces with ἐν τῷ νόμῳ ('in the Law') — using νόμος for OT-broadly, not just Torah, per rabbinic-usage.

1CO 14:25.notes: Echoes LXX Isa 45:14 (ἐν σοὶ ὁ θεός ἐστιν 'God is in you'). Original Isaianic context: pagan-nations (Egyptians, Sabeans) acknowledging that YHWH is uniquely in Israel. Paul's application: the unbeliever-visitor convicted by prophecy makes the same confession ('God is truly among you'). Additional septuagintism in v.25: prostration-worship (ἐπὶ πρόσωπον προσκυνέω) is a recurring LXX-formula (Gen 17:3, Lev 9:24, etc.) for divine-encounter — Paul adopts the OT-vocabulary of theophany for the conversion-experience.

1CO 15:25.notes: Echo of Psalm 110:1 LXX (κάθου ἐκ δεξιῶν μου ἕως ἂν θῶ τοὺς ἐχθρούς σου ὑποπόδιον τῶν ποδῶν σου). Most-cited OT psalm in NT (cf. Mk 12:36, Acts 2:34-35, Heb 1:13). Paul applies christologically: Christ reigns at God's right-hand until enemies subdued. The combination with Ps 8:6 (next verse) is a classic NT-pesher technique, joining two Davidic-sovereignty passages.

1CO 15:27.notes: Direct citation of Psalm 8:6 LXX (= Ps 8:7 LXX numbering): πάντα ὑπέταξας ὑποκάτω τῶν ποδῶν αὐτοῦ. Original psalm context: humanity's-dominion-over-creation as described in Gen 1:28. Paul applies christologically, treating the messiah as the true-Adam who fulfills humanity's-dominion-over-all. Cf. Heb 2:6-9 same OT citation with similar christological reading. Paul exegetes the citation in the next clause: the 'all things' explicitly excludes the Father (the subjector).

1CO 15:32.notes: Direct citation of Isa 22:13 LXX (φάγωμεν καὶ πίωμεν, αὔριον γὰρ ἀποθνῄσκομεν — 'let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die'). Original Isaianic context: Jerusalem's-hedonistic-resignation in face of impending-Assyrian-invasion; God rebukes their feasting-instead-of-repenting. Paul cites to characterize what life would-be without resurrection-hope: pointless hedonism.

1CO 15:45.notes: Citation of Genesis 2:7 LXX (καὶ ἐγένετο ὁ ἄνθρωπος εἰς ψυχὴν ζῶσαν — 'and the man became a living soul'). Paul adds 'first... Adam' to specify and contrasts with 'last Adam = life-giving spirit' (Christ). The LXX-text grounds Paul's typological-pairing of Adam-and-Christ. The 'last Adam' designation is unique-to-Paul and represents a major NT christological-development.

1CO 15:54.notes: Loose citation of Isaiah 25:8. The LXX text differs from Paul's text: LXX reads κατέπιεν ὁ θάνατος ἰσχύσας ('death prevailed-and-swallowed-up'); Paul reads κατεπόθη ὁ θάνατος εἰς νῖκος ('death has been swallowed up in victory'). Paul's reading is closer to the MT (בִּלַּע הַמָּוֶת לָנֶצַח) or possibly reflects an alternative-textual-tradition. The citation is part of Isaiah's eschatological-banquet vision (Isa 25:6-9): God removes death's-shroud from his people. Combined with Hos 13:14 (next verse) into a triumphal-resurrection-taunt.

1CO 15:55.notes: Loose paraphrase of Hosea 13:14 LXX (ποῦ ἡ δίκη σου, θάνατε; ποῦ τὸ κέντρον σου, ᾅδη; — 'where is your judgment, O Death? where is your sting, O Hades?'). Paul rearranges: replaces δίκη ('judgment') with νῖκος ('victory'); replaces ᾅδη ('Hades') with second θάνατε ('Death'); rearranges the question-order. Original Hosean context: complex — a divine-question that has been read both as defiant-promise (LXX/Paul reading) and as ironic-threat (some MT readings). Paul takes the defiant-resurrection reading. Combined with Isa 25:8 (preceding verse) as an Easter-victory-acclamation.

1CO 16:13.notes: Echo of LXX Joshua 1:6-9 (ἴσχυε καὶ ἀνδρίζου — 'be strong and be a man / courageous'). Same imperative-pair appears in LXX Deut 31:6, Ps 26:14 LXX (= 27:14). Paul's ἀνδρίζεσθε / κραταιοῦσθε pair (NT HAPAX ἀνδρίζομαι at this verse) directly invokes the OT-divine-encouragement formula given to Joshua at the start of conquest. The four-fold imperative-cluster (γρηγορεῖτε / στήκετε / ἀνδρίζεσθε / κραταιοῦσθε) gives the verse a military-Joshua flavor: stand-firm-and-fight.

2CO 3:3.notes: Composite allusion: 'tablets of stone' echoes Sinai-stone-tablets (Exod 31:18, 34:1); 'tablets of fleshly hearts' echoes Ezek 36:26 ('I will give you a heart of flesh in place of a heart of stone') and Jer 31:33 (new covenant 'I will write my law on their hearts'). Pauline-typology: new-covenant Spirit-inscribed-heart fulfills the Ezek + Jer promise.

2CO 3:6.notes: 'New covenant' (καινῆς διαθήκης) echoes LXX Jer 38:31 (καινὴν διαθήκην) and Ezek 36:26-27 (Spirit-given heart of flesh). Pauline letter-vs-Spirit antithesis presupposes the Jer-Ezek new-covenant theology.

2CO 3:7.notes: Moses' shining face after Sinai — Exod 34:29-30 'when Moses came down from Mount Sinai, the skin of his face shone' + Exod 34:33-35 (the veil). Paul's rendering 'fading' (καταργουμένην) of the glory is interpretive — the LXX/MT does not state the glory was fading; this is Pauline-midrashic-development.

2CO 3:13.notes: Moses placing the veil over his face after speaking with the Israelites (Exod 34:33). Paul's interpretation 'so that the Israelites would not gaze at the end of what was fading' adds a teleological motive not stated in Exodus.

2CO 3:16.notes: Echo of LXX Exod 34:34: ἡνίκα δ' ἂν εἰσεπορεύετο Μωϋσῆς ἔναντι κυρίου ... περιῃρεῖτο τὸ κάλυμμα. Paul preserves ἡνίκα + κύριος + περιαιρέω + κάλυμμα cluster. Pauline-application: ALL believers-in-Christ now have Moses' veil-removed-before-YHWH prerogative.

2CO 4:6.notes: Composite allusion: Greek wording 'Ἐκ σκότους φῶς λάμψει' is closer to LXX Isa 9:2 (= LXX Isa 9:1) 'ὁ λαὸς ὁ πορευόμενος ἐν σκότει ἴδετε φῶς μέγα' lexical-cluster (σκότος + φῶς) but conceptual-content from Gen 1:3 (creation light-out-of-darkness). Most commentators read this as Pauline-paraphrase of Gen 1:3 with Isa 9 wording-shape. Pauline-typology: God's first-creation light-out-of-darkness command is now the new-creation illumination of believers' hearts.

2CO 4:13.notes: Direct quotation: Greek 'Ἐπίστευσα διὸ ἐλάλησα' matches LXX Ps 115:1 exactly (MT/Hebrew Ps 116:10 has same content but different verse-numbering). Paul applies the psalmist's faith-then-speech pattern to apostolic-proclamation under suffering. Introduced with κατὰ τὸ γεγραμμένον 'according to that which is written' = standard Pauline OT-citation-formula.

2CO 5:4.notes: Echo of LXX Isa 25:8 'death is swallowed up forever' (καταπίνω + θάνατος cluster). Same pattern as 1 Cor 15:54 (Pauline-resurrection-cluster). Pauline-mortality-swallowed-up-by-life.

2CO 5:17.notes: Composite allusion: 'old things passed away / new have come' echoes LXX Isa 43:18-19 (μὴ μνημονεύετε τὰ πρῶτα ... ἰδοὺ ποιῶ καινὰ). καινὴ κτίσις 'new creation' echoes Gen 1 creation-pattern. Pauline-eschatological-new-creation now-already-not-yet.

2CO 6:2.notes: Direct quotation of LXX Isa 49:8. Greek 'Καιρῷ δεκτῷ ἐπήκουσά σου καὶ ἐν ἡμέρᾳ σωτηρίας ἐβοήθησά σοι' matches LXX exactly. The original LXX-context: YHWH's address to the Servant of YHWH (Christ in Pauline-Christological-typology). Pauline-application: the Servant-Song's promise is FULFILLED NOW in the gospel-era — realized eschatology.

2CO 6:9.notes: Pauline-direct-allusion: 'παιδευόμενοι καὶ μὴ θανατούμενοι' echoes LXX Ps 117:18 'παιδεύων ἐπαίδευσέν με ὁ κύριος, καὶ τῷ θανάτῳ οὐ παρέδωκέν με' (MT Ps 118:18; English Ps 118:18). The disciplined-but-not-killed phrasing is psalmic-Pauline.

2CO 6:14.notes: ἑτεροζυγέω (NT-HAPAX) coined from Lev 19:19 LXX prohibition of mixed-yoke (different animals plowing together) + Deut 22:10 (don't yoke ox-and-donkey together). Pauline-application: religious/spiritual-yoking with unbelievers.

2CO 6:16.notes: Composite OT-quotation. 'I will dwell with them and walk among them' echoes LXX Lev 26:11-12 + Ezek 37:27 — the OT-covenant-formula. 'I will be their God and they will be my people' is the canonical OT-covenant-formula (Gen 17:7-8, Exod 6:7, Lev 26:12, Jer 31:33, Ezek 36:28). Paul applies it to the believing-community-as-temple.

2CO 6:17.notes: Composite: 'come out from among them and be separate' echoes LXX Isa 52:11 (ἐξέλθατε ἐκεῖθεν καὶ ἀκαθάρτου μὴ ἅπτεσθε); 'I will receive you' echoes LXX Ezek 20:34, 41 (εἰσδέξομαι ὑμᾶς). Pauline-stitched OT-catena.

2CO 6:18.notes: Composite: 'I will be a Father to you, you will be sons to me' echoes 2 Sam 7:14 (=1 Chron 17:13) — the Davidic-covenant-promise to Solomon. 'sons AND daughters' is Pauline-expansion adding daughters from Isa 43:6 LXX (ἄγαγε τοὺς υἱούς μου ἀπὸ γῆς πόρρωθεν, καὶ τὰς θυγατέρας μου ἀπ' ἄκρων τῆς γῆς). 'λέγει κύριος παντοκράτωρ' is LXX-prophetic-formula (Hos 2:15, Amos, etc.). Pauline-democratization of the Davidic-covenant to ALL believers (men + women).

2CO 7:6.notes: Echo of LXX Isa 49:13 'παρεκάλεσεν ὁ θεὸς τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ καὶ τοὺς ταπεινοὺς τοῦ λαοῦ αὐτοῦ ἠλέησεν' (God comforts his people / has-mercy-on-the-humbled). Pauline-application: God's-comfort-of-the-downcast manifest through Titus's arrival.

2CO 8:15.notes: Direct quotation of LXX Exod 16:18. Greek 'Ὁ τὸ πολὺ οὐκ ἐπλεόνασεν, καὶ ὁ τὸ ὀλίγον οὐκ ἠλαττόνησεν' matches LXX exactly. Original context: manna-equality miracle in wilderness — those who gathered much had no excess; those who gathered little had no shortage. Pauline-application: God-orchestrated-economic-equity through community-giving.

2CO 8:21.notes: Pauline-allusion to LXX Prov 3:4 'προνοοῦ καλὰ ἐνώπιον κυρίου καὶ ἀνθρώπων' (find-favor-before-God-and-people). Pauline-double-accountability principle for financial-administration.

2CO 9:7.notes: Pauline near-direct allusion to LXX-only addition at Prov 22:8a (not in MT/Hebrew): 'ἄνδρα ἱλαρὸν καὶ δότην εὐλογεῖ ὁ θεός' (a cheerful man and giver God blesses). Paul's wording 'ἱλαρὸν δότην ἀγαπᾷ ὁ θεός' is closely parallel; the LXX-Greek shapes Paul's formulation. Famous Pauline-stewardship principle. ἱλαρός + δότης both NT-hapax.

2CO 9:9.notes: Direct quotation of LXX Ps 111:9 (= MT/Hebrew Ps 112:9). Greek 'Ἐσκόρπισεν, ἔδωκεν τοῖς πένησιν, ἡ δικαιοσύνη αὐτοῦ μένει εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα' matches LXX exactly. Original-context: the righteous-man who fears the Lord and gives to the poor — his righteousness endures forever. Pauline-application: God-empowered-generosity produces lasting-righteousness.

2CO 9:10.notes: Composite allusion: 'supplies seed to the sower and bread for food' echoes LXX Isa 55:10 (the rain-and-seed-and-bread-for-food provision). 'increase the harvest of your righteousness' echoes LXX Hos 10:12 (σπείρατε ἑαυτοῖς εἰς δικαιοσύνην, τρυγήσατε εἰς καρπὸν ζωῆς — 'sow for yourselves righteousness, reap fruit-of-life'). Pauline-OT-imagery cluster for divine-provision and harvest-of-righteousness.

2CO 10:17.notes: Pauline-paraphrase of LXX Jer 9:23 (= MT/English Jer 9:24): 'ὁ καυχώμενος καυχάσθω ἐν τούτῳ, συνίειν καὶ γινώσκειν...'. Paul abbreviates 'ἐν τούτῳ' to 'ἐν κυρίῳ' for direct-Christological-application. Same OT-quote Paul cites at 1 Co 1:31. Pauline-anti-self-boasting principle.

2CO 10:18.notes: Pauline-conceptual-echo of 1 Sam 16:7 ('the Lord looks at the heart' — God's evaluation differs from human-self-evaluation). Pauline-aphorism: only divine-commendation counts as approval.

2CO 11:3.notes: Pauline-allusion to Genesis 3 narrative — serpent deceiving Eve through cunning. Pauline-typology: rival-apostles' deceptive-rhetoric parallels the serpent's deception of Eve. ψευδαπόστολοι at v.13 → satanic-pattern-mimicry continues at vv.14-15 (Satan as angel-of-light).

2CO 11:24.notes: Background-allusion to Deut 25:3 LXX (forty-stripes-maximum for synagogue-discipline). Rabbinic-tradition (m. Makkot 3:10) reduced to 39 stripes ('forty minus one') to avoid accidental-overage = Torah-violation. Five floggings of this kind are unrecorded in Acts; Pauline-private-suffering.

2CO 13:1.notes: Pauline-quotation of LXX Deut 19:15 (two-or-three-witnesses-rule). Greek 'ἐπὶ στόματος δύο μαρτύρων καὶ τριῶν σταθήσεται πᾶν ῥῆμα' closely-matches LXX. Same OT-rule cited at Mat 18:16, 1 Tim 5:19. Pauline-application: his-coming-third-time provides the legal-witness-pattern for Corinthian-discipline.

ROM 1:17.notes: Direct quotation of Habakkuk 2:4 LXX, introduced by καθὼς γέγραπται. Greek: ὁ δὲ δίκαιος ἐκ πίστεως ζήσεται. SBLGNT matches LXX-B (without μου); LXX-A reads ἐκ πίστεως μου ('by my faithfulness'). Hebrew MT: צַדִּיק בֶּאֱמוּנָתוֹ יִחְיֶה ('the righteous by his faithfulness shall live'). Paul drops the genitival pronoun, generalizing 'by faith' as the universal principle. Same quotation used by Paul again at Gal 3:11 (likewise without μου) and by Heb 10:38 (with μου, matching LXX-A). Foundational for Pauline justification theology.

ROM 1:23.notes: Lexical echo of Psalm 106:20 LXX (Ps 105:20 LXX): καὶ ἠλλάξαντο τὴν δόξαν αὐτῶν ἐν ὁμοιώματι μόσχου ἔσθοντος χόρτον (golden-calf incident). Paul reuses ἀλλάσσω + δόξα + ἐν ὁμοιώματι. Secondary echo of Jer 2:11 LXX: ὁ δὲ λαός μου ἠλλάξαντο τὴν δόξαν αὐτοῦ. Paul universalizes Israel's golden-calf idolatry-pattern to all humanity — this is the move that makes ch.1 a universal-condemnation argument grounded in OT precedent.

ROM 2:6.notes: Paraphrase combining Psalm 62:13 LXX (Ps 61:13: σὺ ἀποδώσεις ἑκάστῳ κατὰ τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ) and Proverbs 24:12 LXX (ὃς ἀποδίδωσιν ἑκάστῳ κατὰ τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ). The same OT principle appears at Mat 16:27, 2 Tim 4:14, Rev 2:23, 22:12. Paul uses it as the foundation of impartial-judgment (vv.6-11). BSB English brackets it with quotation marks even though Paul does not introduce it with γέγραπται.

ROM 2:24.notes: Direct quotation of Isaiah 52:5 LXX (τὸ ὄνομά μου δι' ὑμᾶς διὰ παντὸς βλασφημεῖται ἐν τοῖς ἔθνεσιν), introduced by καθὼς γέγραπται postpositive. Paul adapts: (1) shifts μου ('my name') to τοῦ θεοῦ ('God's name') for diatribe-third-person framing; (2) drops διὰ παντός ('continually'). Theological move: in Isa 52:5 the Gentile-blasphemy concerns Israel's-suffering; in Paul, the blasphemy is BECAUSE-OF Israel's-hypocrisy — Paul universalizes/inverts.

ROM 2:29.notes: Heart-circumcision idiom from OT covenant-renewal language: Deut 10:16 (περιτεμεῖσθε τὴν σκληροκαρδίαν ὑμῶν), Deut 30:6 (καὶ περικαθαριεῖ κύριος τὴν καρδίαν σου), Jer 4:4 (περιτμήθητε τῷ θεῷ ὑμῶν καὶ περιτέμεσθε τὴν σκληροκαρδίαν ὑμῶν). Pauline-rabbinic-exegesis adapts the OT covenant-call into the gospel-criterion of true-Israel. Hebrew-wordplay: Ἰουδαῖος ('Jew' < Hebrew יהודה) // ἔπαινος ('praise' — Yehudah literally means praise; Gen 29:35; 49:8). True-Yehudah is the one whose praise comes from God.

ROM 2:15.notes: Echoes Jeremiah 31:33 LXX (Jer 38:33 LXX): δώσω νόμους μου εἰς τὴν διάνοιαν αὐτῶν καὶ ἐπὶ καρδίας αὐτῶν γράψω αὐτούς ('I will put my laws into their mind and on their hearts I will write them'). Paul applies the new-covenant heart-Law promise inversely — Gentiles already have law-on-heart by NATURE (φύσει), confirming their accountability rather than the new-covenant fulfillment. The same Jer 31 promise is positively-cited at 2 Cor 3:3 (heart-tablets vs stone-tablets) and Heb 8:10/10:16.

ROM 2:19.notes: Possible-allusion to Isaiah 42:6 (δέδωκά σε εἰς διαθήκην γένους εἰς φῶς ἐθνῶν) + Isaiah 49:6 (τέθεικά σε εἰς φῶς ἐθνῶν). The 'guide-for-blind / light-for-those-in-darkness' formula echoes Isaiah's servant-as-light-to-nations theme. Paul lists this as the JEWISH SELF-CONCEPTION which vv.21-24 will challenge as failing in practice.

ROM 3:4.notes: Direct quote of Psalm 51:4 LXX (Ps 50:6 LXX), introduced by καθὼς γέγραπται. David's confession after Bathsheba (Ps 51 superscription). Paul cites the LXX-form (which differs slightly from Hebrew MT). Used by Paul to argue that God is JUST in judging the sinner — even when human-unfaithfulness is the object of judgment.

ROM 3:10.notes: Opens the Romans-3-catena (vv.10-18) with γέγραπται. Combines Psalm 14:1-3 LXX (Ps 13:1-3 LXX = Ps 53:1-3 LXX = Ps 52:2-4 LXX) with Ecclesiastes 7:20 LXX (οὐκ ἔστιν ἄνθρωπος δίκαιος ἐν τῇ γῇ ὃς ποιήσει ἀγαθὸν καὶ οὐχ ἁμαρτήσεται). The 'no-one-righteous-not-even-one' formulation owes more to Eccl 7:20 than to Ps 14:1 (which lacks δίκαιος). Some LXX manuscripts of Ps 14 already include vv.13-18 of Romans 3 in their Psalm-text-tradition; the catena may be pre-Pauline.

ROM 3:11.notes: Catena continues: Psalm 14:2 LXX (Ps 13:2 LXX). Paul condenses the divine-search-from-heaven formula into a NEGATIVE-ASSERTION: there-is-none-who-understands-or-seeks.

ROM 3:12.notes: Catena continues: Psalm 14:3 LXX (Ps 13:3 LXX) — closes the Ps 14/53 + Eccl 7:20 first-segment of the catena.

ROM 3:13.notes: TWO distinct OT-quotes in one verse. (1) v.13a: Psalm 5:9 LXX (Ps 5:10 LXX) — exact-quote: τάφος ἀνεῳγμένος ὁ λάρυγξ αὐτῶν, ταῖς γλώσσαις αὐτῶν ἐδολιοῦσαν. (2) v.13b: Psalm 140:3 LXX (Ps 139:4 LXX) — second-half quoted: ἰὸς ἀσπίδων ὑπὸ τὰ χείλη αὐτῶν.

ROM 3:14.notes: Catena continues: Psalm 10:7 LXX (Ps 9:28 LXX). Paul rephrases — singular relative-clause becomes plural; word-order shifts. Body-progression: throat (v.13a) → tongue (v.13a) → lips (v.13b) → MOUTH (v.14).

ROM 3:15.notes: Begins Isaiah-segment of catena: Isaiah 59:7 LXX (οἱ δὲ πόδες αὐτῶν ἐπὶ πονηρίαν τρέχουσιν ταχινοὶ ἐκχέαι αἷμα). Paul condenses: ταχινοί → ὀξεῖς (synonym 'sharp/swift'). Possible secondary echo of Proverbs 1:16 LXX (same Hebrew-MT origin). Body-progression continues: feet.

ROM 3:16.notes: Isaiah-segment continues: Isaiah 59:7c LXX — exact-quote: σύντριμμα καὶ ταλαιπωρία ἐν ταῖς ὁδοῖς αὐτῶν.

ROM 3:17.notes: Closes Isaiah-segment: Isaiah 59:8a LXX (ὁδὸν εἰρήνης οὐκ οἴδασιν). Paul: aorist ἔγνωσαν (vs LXX-perfect οἴδασιν) — same sense.

ROM 3:18.notes: Final-catena-segment: Psalm 36:1 LXX (Ps 35:2 LXX). Paul: 3pl αὐτῶν vs LXX 3sg αὐτοῦ — generalizing-shift. Climactic-final-line: 'no-fear-of-God' = ROOT-cause of all body-part-sins above. Catena structure complete: head-mind (v.11) → throat-tongue-lips-mouth-feet (vv.13-15) → eyes (v.18).

ROM 3:20.notes: Adapted-echo: Psalm 143:2 LXX (Ps 142:2 LXX): οὐ δικαιωθήσεται ἐνώπιόν σου πᾶς ζῶν. Paul-adapts: πᾶς ζῶν 'every living thing' → πᾶσα σάρξ 'all flesh' (Hebraism σάρξ = humanity). Adds: ἐξ ἔργων νόμου ('by-works-of-Law') as the disqualifying-method. Foundational-verse for Pauline 'works-of-Law-do-not-justify' — central to the Reformation Sola-Fide doctrine. Same OT-text used at Gal 2:16 + Gal 3:11.

ROM 3:29.notes: Echoes the Shema (Deut 6:4 LXX: ἄκουε Ισραηλ κύριος ὁ θεὸς ἡμῶν κύριος εἷς ἐστιν). Paul appeals to monotheism — 'God is one' — as the basis for arguing that God justifies BOTH Jews and Gentiles by the same faith. Same Shema-echo used by Paul at v.30 (εἷς ὁ θεός), Gal 3:20, 1 Cor 8:6, 1 Tim 2:5.

ROM 4:3.notes: FOUNDATIONAL TEXT for Pauline-justification-by-faith. Genesis 15:6 LXX (καὶ ἐπίστευσεν Αβραμ τῷ θεῷ καὶ ἐλογίσθη αὐτῷ εἰς δικαιοσύνην) — Paul-exact-quote. Repeated at Rom 4:9 (paraphrase), 4:22 (verbatim); Gal 3:6 (verbatim); Jas 2:23 (verbatim, with opposite-emphasis). The single most important OT-text for Pauline-justification-doctrine. λογίζομαι 'reckon/credit' is the chapter's-theme-verb.

ROM 4:7.notes: Direct quote of Psalm 32:1 LXX (Ps 31:1 LXX). David's-blessedness-of-forgiven. Paul cites David alongside Abraham as second-OT-witness to justification-by-faith (rabbinic two-witnesses-method).

ROM 4:8.notes: Continuation of Ps 32 quote (v.7). Direct quote of Psalm 32:2 LXX (Ps 31:2 LXX). λογίσηται aorist-subjunctive 'NOT-count' — connects David's-imagery to the chapter's λογίζομαι theme.

ROM 4:9.notes: Paul-paraphrase of Gen 15:6 (cf v.3). Same-text in re-deployed-form: 'faith-was-credited-to-Abraham-as-righteousness.' Sets up the WHEN question (vv.10-12).

ROM 4:17.notes: Direct quote of Genesis 17:5 LXX (πατέρα πολλῶν ἐθνῶν τέθεικά σε). God's-renaming-of-Abram-to-Abraham (Heb 'av-hamon = father-of-multitudes). Paul reads ἐθνῶν as 'NATIONS / GENTILES' for the universal-fatherhood claim. The Gen 17 chapter ALSO contains the circumcision-mandate; Paul deliberately cites the FATHERHOOD-promise (v.5) AHEAD-OF the circumcision-commandment to confirm the chronological-priority of faith.

ROM 4:18.notes: Direct quote of Genesis 15:5 LXX (Οὕτως ἔσται τὸ σπέρμα σου). God's-promise-to-Abraham about offspring numerous-as-stars. Same Gen 15 chapter as the foundational-v.6-citation. Abraham's hope-against-hope is the paradigm of Christian-resurrection-faith.

ROM 4:22.notes: Third citation of Gen 15:6 in the chapter (cf 4:3, 4:9). Closes the Abraham-portrait by re-asserting the OT-proof-text for the imputational-justification doctrine.

ROM 7:7.notes: Direct quote of the 10th commandment (Exodus 20:17 LXX / Deuteronomy 5:21 LXX). Paul cites the BARE prohibition (οὐκ ἐπιθυμήσεις 'do not covet') without the explicit objects ('your neighbor's house, wife, etc.') — universalizing the prohibition to inner-desire-as-such. The 10th commandment is the only Decalogue-commandment whose violation can be entirely-internal (as opposed to outwardly-observable acts), making it Paul's chosen exemplar for Law's-revelatory-function: exposing inner-sin.

ROM 7:11.notes: Pauline-Eden-echo: ἐξηπάτησέν με 'deceived me' echoes the LXX of Eve's-confession at Genesis 3:13 LXX (ὁ ὄφις ἠπάτησέν με 'the serpent deceived me'). Paul-as-Adam/Eve-typology — sin (now playing the serpent's role) deceives the I (now playing humanity's role) through the commandment (now playing the prohibition's role). Reinforces the Adamic-typology reading of Rom 7:7-12.

ROM 8:32.notes: OT-ECHO of Genesis 22:16 LXX (Akedah/binding-of-Isaac): οὐκ ἐφείσω τοῦ υἱοῦ σου τοῦ ἀγαπητοῦ. Profound-typology: where Abraham was permitted-to-spare-Isaac, God-the-Father did-NOT-spare-the-Son. Paul reuses φείδομαι in same-construction. The Akedah is the OT-typological-mirror of the Cross.

ROM 8:33.notes: Echoes Isaiah 50:8 LXX (Servant's-confidence: τίς ὁ κρινόμενός μοι ... ὁ δικαιῶν με). Paul applies the Servant's-individual-confidence to the corporate-elect. Same δικαιόω verb, courtroom-vindication setting.

ROM 8:36.notes: Direct quote of Psalm 44:22 LXX (Ps 43:23 LXX): Ἕνεκεν σοῦ θανατούμεθα ὅλην τὴν ἡμέραν, ἐλογίσθημεν ὡς πρόβατα σφαγῆς — Paul-exact-quote. The Psalm is a corporate-Israel-lament under-persecution; Paul applies-corporate-Christian. Sets up the seven-fold-suffering catalog of v.35 within the OT-Psalmist's-suffering-tradition.

ROM 8:34.notes: Echoes Psalm 110:1 LXX (Ps 109:1 LXX) — Christ-at-God's-right-hand. The most-quoted-OT-text in the NT (cf Mat 22:44; Mark 12:36; Luke 20:42; Acts 2:34-35; Heb 1:13). Pauline-application: enthroned-Christ-as-intercessor.

ROM 8:20.notes: Echo of Genesis 3:17-19's creation-curse (the ground 'cursed because of you'; thorns and thistles; creation's-frustration). Paul universalizes the Edenic-curse: ALL creation was subjected to ματαιότης ('futility') — not by its own choice, but because-of-the-Subjector (= God, mainstream-evangelical reading). Paul reads creation's-cursed-state as temporary, oriented-toward-eschatological-restoration (v.21).

ROM 9:7.notes: Direct quote of Genesis 21:12 LXX — God's word that 'through Isaac' the line of promise will be established. The first OT-precedent for election-within-election: Abraham had Ishmael, but God elected Isaac as the line.

ROM 9:9.notes: Paul conflates Genesis 18:10 + 18:14 LXX — God's promise to Abraham about Sarah's miraculous conception of Isaac. Paul cites the promise to ground the 'children of promise' category in Sarah's-supernatural-conception.

ROM 9:12.notes: Direct quote of Genesis 25:23 LXX — God's pre-natal prophecy to Rebecca about Esau and Jacob ('the older shall serve the younger'). Foundational text for unconditional-election: spoken before the twins were born or had done good or evil.

ROM 9:13.notes: Direct quote of Malachi 1:2-3 LXX. God's-retrospective-statement (post-Israel/Edom-history). Hebraic-comparative ('loved-more / preferred-not') translated by LXX into strong-Greek-form ἀγαπάω/μισέω. Paul cites the strong form — God's electing-love vs Esau's-non-election.

ROM 9:15.notes: Direct quote of Exodus 33:19 LXX. God-to-Moses after the golden-calf incident. Foundational sovereign-mercy formula. Used by Paul as proof-text for unconditional-mercy (= mercy not contingent on human merit).

ROM 9:17.notes: Direct quote of Exodus 9:16 LXX. God-to-Pharaoh through Moses, mid-plagues. Pharaoh as the negative-counter-example to Moses (positive in v.15). God's-sovereign-purposes include hardening-the-rebellious for the world-wide proclamation of His name.

ROM 9:20.notes: Echoes Isaiah 29:16 LXX + Isaiah 45:9 LXX (potter-clay imagery). 'Shall what is formed say to him who formed it...' — Paul reuses πλάσσω-vocabulary from LXX-creation/potter contexts. Anticipates the explicit potter-metaphor in v.21.

ROM 9:25.notes: Direct quote of Hosea 2:23 LXX (Hos 2:25 LXX) — God's promise of restoration: 'I will call those who were not my people, my people.' In Hosea-context, the unfaithful-Israel restored. Paul-bold-application: extends to Gentile-inclusion.

ROM 9:26.notes: Direct quote of Hosea 1:10 LXX (Hos 2:1 LXX). The 'sons of the living God' — restored-status of Hosea's-rejected-children, applied corporately. Paired with v.25's Hos 2:23 quote for the Gentile-inclusion warrant.

ROM 9:27.notes: Direct quote of Isaiah 10:22 LXX (continues into v.28's Isa 10:23). Pauline-remnant-doctrine: even within ethnic-Israel, only-the-remnant is saved. Counterbalances the Hosea-Gentile-inclusion: NOT-all-Israel-but-only-the-remnant; AND-some-Gentiles.
